**Summary.** This paper proposes using spatially entangled photon pairs to achieve contrast-enhanced imaging through weakly scattering media. By employing coincidence detection and post-selection on correlated photon pairs, the technique effectively filters out scattered light, significantly improving image quality over standard single-photon measurements. This quantum approach offers a powerful tool for imaging in complex, scattering environments where traditional optical methods fail.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a quantum-enhanced imaging protocol that circumvents limitations of classical optical techniques (like time/spatial gating) in highly scattering environments, which is a key area of quantum optics research.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to enhance the image contrast when imaging objects immersed in weakly scattering media.

**Main result.** Using coincidence detection and post-selection on spatially entangled photon pairs significantly enhances image contrast compared to single-photon or non-post-selected coincidence measurements.

**Method.** The method involves exploiting the spatial entanglement of photon pairs and performing coincidence post-selection to filter out scattered bi-photons, thereby isolating the ballistic signal.

**Model / system.** The system uses Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC) to generate spatially entangled photon pairs which are then passed through a scattering medium and an object. The theoretical framework models the bi-photon wavefunction and the effect of random phase masks.

**Key observables.** Image contrast (quantified via Modulation Transfer Function, MTF), coincidence counts, and the ratio of post-selected coincidences to single-photon counts.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Scattering strength, quantified by the ratio $w_q/w_A$, and the degree of input spatial entanglement ($w_q/w_0$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the double-Gaussian approximation for the bi-photon wavefunction and assumes that post-selection is effective even when time gating or spatial filtering is impractical.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare MTF and reconstructed images for single-photon, non-post-selected coincidence, and post-selected coincidence measurements, demonstrating contrast enhancement across different scattering regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from conceptual schemes comparing classical vs. quantum filtering, through numerical simulations detailing the MTF dependence on scattering strength, and culminates in experimental demonstrations confirming the contrast improvement.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Improving the image contrast of objects immersed in weakly scattering media can be achieved using various strategies. One common approach is to reject events associated with scattered photons in favor of the detection of ballistic photons. While this is traditionally done via time gating or spatial filtering, we propose a different approach based on probing the object with spatio-temporally entangled photon pairs. We show that coincidence detection, followed by post-selection on spatially correlated events, allows us to isolate ballistic from scattered bi-photons, thereby enhancing image contrast relative to a single-photon detection strategy, and simultaneously removes events due to background light. Our predictions are obtained via numerical simulations and confirmed by experiments conducted in two configurations where either both photons or only one illuminates the scene. In both scenarios, correlation post-selection shows an improvement in image contrast at the expense of higher shot noise due to the lower number of events. The latter can be partially compensated for by appropriately combining events from several post-selection windows. Our findings will enable extending imaging through scattering media into the quantum imaging framework in settings where adaptive optics, time gating, and spatial filtering are impractical.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.28931"></a>
### [Learning quantum ground states in the space of measurement outcomes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28931v1)

**Authors:** Kartiek Agarwal  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28931v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `measurement-induced transitions` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.28931_figures/2605.28931_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. POVM dual operators Fi (blue dots) for a single qubit sit at the ends of the minimal tetrahedron which fully encapsulates the Bloch (unit) sphere representing all possible physical states. The coordinates (x, y, z) reflect the ampli- tude of σx, σy, σz in the dual operators. The inferred density matrix resides in the convex hull of the tetrahedron by con- struction. It can capture all the possible physical states, but for certain values of the POVM outcome probabilties, one also can obtain unphysical states. Increasing the number of POVM outcomes does not result in the shrinking of this con- vex hull to more tightly wrap the space of physical states.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.28931_figures/2605.28931_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Dual stream architecture. Two parallel RNNs are simulated, GRUπ which is fed the site parity (−1)j and, GRU0 which is not. We can consider the two streams as being attuned to momentum k = π, 0 sectors, respectively. The output of these two GRU streams is fed into a Fusion head (details in main text), from which logits denoting the POVM measurement outcomes is output. These logits are fi- nally used to compute outcome probabilities using a Softmax head.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.28931_figures/2605.28931_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Correlations in the XX channel, X0X∗ j , where X∗ j = Xj for j ̸= 0, and X∗ 0 = I, obtained via training (green) and DMRG (red) for the critical TFIM model, HTFIM, with different GRU architectures—standard architecture with no site parity information for (a) NL = 1 and (b) NL = 2 are shown, along with the results for the dual stream architec- ture in (c) with NL = 2. Adding more layers helps improve correlations but providing parity information is essential in addressing long range correlations. Operator range R = 4 is used in obtaining these results.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.28931_figures/2605.28931_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<img src="2605.28931_figures/2605.28931_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. (a) Computation of the energy variance per site, ∆H2/L and (inset) deviation of energy density from DMRG result, ∆E/L for the TFIM gapped with a Z field, HGapped TFIM, as a function of the training step, for various buffer and gradient batch sizes, B and G, respectively. (b) Maximum deviation in any XX, YY, or ZZ correlators from DMRG results, max{ X0X∗ j , Y0Y ∗ j , Z0Z∗ j }, as a func- tion of training step, for various values of B, G. The esti- mation of these values is obtained using an inference batch size I = B; this is generally sufficient to estimate ∆E/L and the deviation in correlations, but not for ∆H2/L which is in- ferred by summing O(L2) operators of weight w = 4....</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a method to find quantum ground states by optimizing a neural network that models the probability distribution of measurement outcomes. By enforcing physical constraints, such as the positive semi-definiteness of the operator Gram matrix, the method successfully simulates complex 1D quantum spin models. It represents a significant advancement in using machine learning to bridge the gap between theoretical quantum mechanics and experimental measurement data.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work offers a novel, data-driven pathway to quantum simulation that bypasses the explicit construction of complex wavefunctions or density matrices, making it highly relevant for simulating systems where measurement data is the primary input.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of variational learning of quantum many-body ground states directly in the space of experimentally measurable measurement outcomes.

**Main result.** The proposed method successfully obtains ground states for 1D spin models up to $L=128$, demonstrating efficacy across both gapped and gapless systems.

**Method.** It uses autoregressive neural networks (GRUs) to model the probability distribution over outcomes from a SIC-POVM, minimizing the energy expectation value subject to complex positivity constraints on the reconstructed density matrix.

**Model / system.** The approach is benchmarked on 1D spin models, specifically the transverse-field Ising model and the Heisenberg model, for system sizes up to $L=128$.

**Key observables.** Ground state energy density ($\Delta E/L$), local spin polarization, and two-point correlation functions ($\langle \Pi_i \Pi_j angle$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size ($L$), operator weight ($w$), operator range ($R$), and stochastic noise thresholds ($	au, s$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core assumption is that the quantum state can be accurately represented by a probability distribution over measurement outcomes, and the method relies on enforcing positivity constraints on the Gram matrix of Pauli observables.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the POVM dual operators, the dual-stream GRU architecture, and comparisons of calculated correlations (e.g., for the critical TFIM model) against established results like DMRG.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the theoretical framework (POVM representation, GRU encoding), the optimization procedure (minimizing energy subject to PSD constraints), and extensive benchmarking across various 1D spin models, comparing results to established numerical techniques.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate variational learning of quantum many-body ground states directly in measurement space using autoregressive neural networks. In particular, we represent quantum states via probability distributions of outcomes over a symmetric informationally complete positive operator-valued measure (SIC-POVM). The probability distribution is encoded in the parameters of an autoregressive neural-network-based on gated recurrent units (GRUs). Ground states are obtained by gradient descent that updates the probability distribution to minimize the energy with respect to a given Hamiltonian, while enforcing positivity constraints that ensure that the distribution of measurement outcomes correspond to a physical quantum state. We analyze the role of constraint enforcement (hierarchy of positivity conditions), variety of neural network architectures (multiple layers, dilation, and modifications of input data) in determining the success of this approach. We benchmark our approach on one-dimensional transverse-field Ising model and the Heisenberg model, along with gapping fields, for system sizes up to L=128, illustrating its efficacy across a wide variety of models.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper reports spectroscopic evidence, primarily using RIXS and XAS, to characterize $	ext{FeSb}_2$ as a molecular orbital Kondo insulator. By comparing experimental results with advanced theoretical models, the authors demonstrate that the electronic structure is governed by hybridized $	ext{Fe-Sb}$ molecular orbitals. This work advances the understanding of unconventional correlated insulators by providing a pathway to engineer high-temperature Kondo states.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides a concrete, measurable platform ($	ext{FeSb}_2$) to test the theoretical concept of molecular orbital Kondo insulators, bridging the gap between $4f$ heavy fermion physics and transition metal chemistry.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To understand the electronic structure and emergent phenomena in a Kondo insulator (KI) system, specifically moving beyond conventional atomic multiplet descriptions.

**Main result.** The study establishes that $	ext{FeSb}_2$ is a molecular orbital Kondo insulator, characterized by a mixed-configuration ground state derived from hybridized $	ext{Fe-Sb}$ molecular orbitals.

**Method.** The research combines experimental measurements like RIXS and XAS with advanced theoretical calculations, including DFT, the Charge-Transfer Hamiltonian (CTHFAM), and the $	ext{Oceaan}$ code.

**Model / system.** The physical system is $	ext{FeSb}_2$, which is modeled as a Kondo insulator where the local moments arise from hybridized molecular orbitals ($	ext{Fe-Sb}$), rather than purely atomic $d$ electrons.

**Key observables.** RIXS energy loss features ($	ext{M}1, 	ext{M}2$, etc.), XAS spectrum shape, momentum and temperature dependence of excitations, and doping dependence.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low energy excitations (e.g., $	ext{M}1$ at 200-300 meV, $	ext{M}2$ at 800 meV), temperature (30 K vs 300 K), and doping concentration ($x$ in $	ext{FeSb}_{2-x}	ext{Te}_x$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis compares continuum state calculations ($	ext{Oceaan}$) with localized multiplet calculations ($	ext{CTHFAM}$), assuming the latter captures the essential physics of the mixed-configuration ground state.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the crystal structure, XAS/RIXS spectra, momentum mapping of low-energy excitations ($	ext{M}1, 	ext{M}2$), and the evolution of these features with temperature and doping.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically presents experimental data (XAS/RIXS) and compares them against multiple theoretical frameworks (DFT, $	ext{Oceaan}$, CTHFAM) to build a consistent picture of the electronic ground state and its excitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

A Kondo insulator (KI) is a prototypical example of a highly entangled phase of matter, where many-body interactions between local moments and delocalized electrons engender the non-magnetic insulating ground state. Conventionally, the local moments arise from atomic multiplet states with a narrow bandwidth, limiting Kondo coherence to low temperatures. Here, we realize a new paradigm for constructing the KI state with hybridized molecular orbitals in FeSb2. Resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) at the Fe L-edge reveals distinct signatures of band-like continuum states and localized states. Comparisons with first-principles calculations establish a mixed-configuration ground state with hybridized Fe d-Sb p molecular orbitals as basis states. By systematically investigating the RIXS momentum, temperature, and doping dependences, we find propagating collective modes commensurate with many-body charge and spin excitations. Our results pave the way for understanding the emerging class of unconventional d electron insulators and engineering high temperature Kondo many-body states.

</details>

**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30074v1>

**Summary.** This work investigates the source of quasi-1D ferromagnetism in the material CeSb2 using ARPES. By showing that the magnetism is not due to Fermi surface nesting, the authors propose a band-selective Kondo coupling mechanism. This mechanism explains the strong magnetic anisotropy and provides a general model for emergent low-dimensional magnetism in correlated electron materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The identification of a band-selective Kondo coupling mechanism provides a concrete, microscopic electronic route to understanding emergent low-dimensional magnetism in correlated $f$-electron systems, which is highly relevant to strongly correlated physics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The origin of quasi-one-dimensional (q1D) ferromagnetic (FM) excitations in the candidate spin-triplet superconductor CeSb2 remains unclear.

**Main result.** The study identifies band-selective Kondo coupling as the microscopic mechanism generating strongly anisotropic magnetic exchange interactions, which accounts for the emergent q1D magnetism.

**Method.** Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), particularly resonant ARPES tuned across the Ce M-edge, was used to probe the electronic structure.

**Model / system.** The system is CeSb2, a material crystallizing in a quasi-two-dimensional lattice and being studied as a candidate for spin-triplet superconductivity. The analysis focuses on the electronic behavior of the Ce 4f electrons interacting with the conduction bands.

**Key observables.** Selective enhancement of Ce 4f spectral weight on specific Fermi pockets, absence of a spin-density-wave gap, and the temperature dependence of these spectral features.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low temperatures (down to 2 K), the Ce M-edge resonance condition, and the Fermi surface topology ($\alpha, eta, \gamma, \eta$ pockets).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the observed spectral features are dominated by the Kondo coupling mechanism, although the possibility of sub-meV gaps is noted as a limitation.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the crystal structure and Fermi surface maps, band dispersions, and photoemission intensity changes comparing off-resonance, pre-edge, and on-resonance conditions, highlighting the $4f$ spectral weight enhancement.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the mystery of the q1D magnetism in CeSb2. It then uses ARPES to rule out nesting-driven mechanisms. The core of the work presents resonant ARPES data showing band-selective $4f$ enhancement, leading to the conclusion of a band-selective Kondo coupling mechanism.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The origin of quasi-one-dimensional (q1D) ferromagnetic (FM) excitations in the candidate spin-triplet superconductor CeSb$_2$ has remained unclear. Here we report an electronic mechanism for emergent q1D magnetism in the quasi-two-dimensional lattice of CeSb$_2$, revealed by angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES). High-resolution ARPES resolves no spin-density-wave gap on the dispersive Fermi pockets, disfavoring a nesting-driven mechanism for the q1D FM excitations. Instead, resonant ARPES reveals a pronounced selective enhancement of Ce 4$f$ spectral weight on the $C_2$-distributed Fermi pockets aligned with the Ce ladder. This observation signifies band-selective Kondo coupling that generates strongly anisotropic magnetic exchange interactions, which can naturally account for both the q1D ferromagnetic excitations and the competing magnetic orders. Our results identify a band-selective Kondo coupling mechanism for emergent low-dimensional magnetism in correlated $f$-electron systems.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.29732"></a>
### [Exact Geometric Typicality and Bipartite Entanglement from the Projected Central Limit Theorem on Hyperspheres](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29732v1)

**Authors:** Zhi-Wei Wang, Pei-Wen Li, Samuel L. Braunstein  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29732v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29732_figures/2605.29732_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Geometric suppression of thermal fluctuations in a finite quantum system (dS = 2, dE = 6). (a) On a linear scale, the exact Beta distribution rigorously bounds physical probabilities to Pk ∈[0, 1], whereas the standard Gaussian as- sumption erroneously bleeds into unphysical regimes (shaded red). (b) Logarithmic scale: the exact density falls below the Gaussian approximation by more than an order of magnitude near the kinematic boundary, and the exact cumulative prob- ability falls below by more than two orders of magnitude for Pk &gt; 0.95.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work provides an exact, non-perturbative calculation of the bipartite quantum mutual information for random pure states in finite quantum systems. By utilizing the Projected Central Limit Theorem, the authors show that the mutual information has a highly structured asymptotic expansion in $1/N$ and cleanly separates into quantum coherence and classical contributions. This refines our understanding of entanglement scaling by correcting the limitations of standard Gaussian approximations.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous derivation of the mutual information structure and the identification of the exact quantum coherence contribution versus classical probability terms provide deep insights into how entanglement scales in finite quantum systems, which is crucial for understanding decoherence and thermalization.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the statistical profile of fluctuations in isolated quantum systems undergoing unitary evolution, specifically refining the understanding of entanglement measures beyond standard Gaussian approximations.

**Main result.** It derives an exact, non-perturbative closed form for the bipartite quantum mutual information $\langle I(A:B) \rangle$, showing its asymptotic expansion in $1/N$ admits a highly structured Bernoulli-factorized form.

**Method.** The analysis relies on the exact Projected Central Limit Theorem on hyperspheres, leading to the Beta distribution for subsystem probabilities, and involves algebraic reorganization of Page's formula.

**Model / system.** The system models isolated quantum states evolving unitarily, analyzed via the uniform measure on the unit hypersphere $S^{2N-1}$ for a total dimension $N=d_A d_B d_E$.

**Key observables.** Bipartite quantum mutual information $\langle I(A:B) \rangle$, subsystem occupation probabilities $P_k$, and the purity $\langle \text{Tr}(\rho_S^2) \rangle$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Subsystem dimensions ($d_A, d_B, d_E$), total dimension $N$, and the finite-size correction terms.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Standard treatments rely on the Gaussian approximation, which the authors show is inaccurate in finite systems; the analysis is most relevant for finite-size quantum regimes.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 (mentioned) illustrates the divergence between the Gaussian and Beta tail probabilities, highlighting the 'platykurtic shield' effect.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the exact Beta distribution for subsystem probabilities, to deriving the mutual information $\langle I(A:B) \rangle$, and finally establishing its exact factorization and asymptotic expansion in $1/N$ via algebraic reorganization of Page's formula.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Starting from the exact Projected Central Limit Theorem on hyperspheres, we rederive the Beta distribution for subsystem occupation probabilities and Lubkin's purity formula from elementary hyperspherical moments, quantifying the finite-size ``platykurtic'' suppression of tails relative to the Gaussian approximation used in standard eigenstate-thermalization and typicality treatments. Our main new result concerns the bipartite quantum mutual information $\langle I(A{:}B)\rangle$ for Haar-random pure states. We show that its full asymptotic expansion in $1/N$ admits a Bernoulli-factorized form in which every order $k \ge 1$ carries the symmetric factor $(d_A^{2k}-1)(d_B^{2k}-1)$ and all higher odd-order corrections vanish identically. Through an exact algebraic reorganization of Page's formula (conjectured in Ref.~\cite{Page1993} and subsequently proven~\cite{Foong1994, SanchezRuiz1995, Sen1996}), we establish that the leading finite-size correction separates into a dominant $\mathfrak{su}(d_A) \otimes \mathfrak{su}(d_B)$ bipartite quantum coherence contribution $(d_A^2 - 1)(d_B^2 - 1)/(2N)$ and a subtracted classical-probability (Cartan $\otimes$ Cartan) contribution $(d_A - 1)(d_B - 1)/(2N)$, and we trace this separation to the difference between diagonal and eigenvalue entropies via Schur's majorisation theorem, with the dimensional counts $(d-1)$ and $(d^2-1)$ acquiring meaning through the Cartan structure of the generalised Bloch decomposition. These results admit a single non-perturbative closed form: the exact typical mutual information factors as $\langle I(A{:}B)\rangle = (d_A^2-1)(d_B^2-1)\,\mathcal{G}(d_A,d_B,d_E)$, with $\mathcal{G}$ given by an explicit Bose--Einstein integral whose asymptotic expansion in $1/N$ reproduces the Bernoulli series.

</details>

**Authors:** Jai Lalita  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29717v1>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how non-classical quantum resources degrade in noisy, non-Markovian environments using the discrete Wigner function formalism. It demonstrates that specific 'negative quantum states' offer superior protection compared to standard Bell states. The research combines theoretical analysis with experimental implementation on superconducting qubits, advancing the practical use of quantum resources in realistic noisy settings.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant as it bridges quantum information theory (resource quantification) with open quantum systems dynamics (non-Markovianity), providing concrete, hardware-validated methods for preserving quantum resources.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The research aims to characterize, identify, and protect non-classical quantum resources in finite-dimensional quantum systems when subjected to realistic noise and non-Markovian environmental effects.

**Main result.** Negative quantum states are shown to be more resilient than traditional Bell states against non-Markovian noise, and weak measurement/quantum measurement reversal techniques can enhance quantum correlations and teleportation fidelity.

**Method.** The study employs the discrete Wigner functions (DWFs) framework to analyze state evolution under various open quantum channels, comparing results across Markovian and non-Markovian regimes.

**Model / system.** The work focuses on two-qubit quantum systems, utilizing models like the spin-boson and collision models, and is demonstrated experimentally using superconducting hardware (IBM's ibm_brisbane device).

**Key observables.** Wigner negativity, mana, coherence, teleportation fidelity, concurrence, CHSH violation, and Quantum Fisher Information.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Non-Markovian noise parameters (e.g., $\gamma$, $b$), coupling strengths, and specific state parameters ($p, q$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the applicability of the DWF framework and models the environment using specific noise channels (AD, RTN).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the time evolution of Wigner negativity, concurrence, and fidelity for various states under non-Markovian noise, comparing results with and without measurement reversal techniques.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the evolution of non-classical resources under non-Markovian noise, proposes measurement-based protection strategies, and concludes with the experimental realization of these states on superconducting hardware.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The central aim of the thesis is to examine how non-classical resources in finite-dimensional quantum systems can be identified, characterized, and protected for practical use in the presence of realistic noise. Using the discrete Wigner functions (DWFs) framework, we introduce negative quantum states and examine how their Wigner negativity, mana, coherence, and teleportation fidelity evolve under unital and non-unital channels, with particular attention to non-Markovian random-telegraph and amplitude-damping dynamics. We also analyze protection strategies based on weak measurement and quantum measurement reversal, showing that these methods can enhance quantum correlations, reduce fidelity deviation, and improve teleportation performance for two-qubit negative states in memory-bearing environments. Moreover, we demonstrate that certain negative states, derived from phase-space point operators, exhibit greater resilience than Bell states in measures of entanglement under non-Markovian noise. Further, this thesis focuses on developing and implementing quantum circuits for generating these states on superconducting hardware and realizing them for the first time on IBM's ibm-Brisbane device. Their preparation is verified using quantum state tomography, demonstrating high fidelity under realistic noise conditions. We propose a teleportation scheme that leverages one of the two-qubit negative quantum states as a resource. Moreover, these two-qubit negative quantum states are also found to perform better than the Bell states for maximal CHSH violation and Fisher information in noisy conditions. We believe that these negative quantum states have the potential to be used in place of the traditional Bell states in scenarios where non-Markovian errors are prevalent. (continued in the PDF)

</details>

**Authors:** Sofia Evangelou  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29724v1>

**Summary.** This paper analyzes phase metrology for a quantum emitter coupled to a dimerized SSH lattice bath. It shows that the ability to retain phase information is protected by a spectral gap, which supports an in-gap bound state. The metrological signal strength is quantified by the QFI, which tracks the spectral gap physics, providing a robust diagnostic tool for engineered quantum systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, lattice-based realization of how topological spectral features (the gap) can be harnessed to engineer robust quantum sensing protocols, linking condensed matter topology directly to quantum information metrics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how the structured environment provided by a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) bath protects and governs the persistence of phase information (metrology) in a quantum emitter.

**Main result.** The late-time phase-Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) is directly protected by the existence of an in-gap bound state, and this protection degrades predictably as the emitter detuning moves away from the gap center or as coupling strength increases.

**Method.** The analysis employs a microscopic approach using Dyson-equation analysis to derive the local bath Green's function, allowing for the calculation of the phase-QFI in the single-excitation sector.

**Model / system.** The system consists of a single quantum emitter (two-level system) coupled locally to a bosonic bath modeled by a dimerized Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) lattice Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), the emitter bound-state residue ($Z_{BS}$), and the late-time averaged phase-QFI ($\overline{F(\phi)}_{Q}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimerization parameter ($d$), emitter detuning ($\Delta$), and coupling strength ($g$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is restricted to the bulk, local-coupling geometry, meaning it probes spectral-gap physics rather than the SSH winding sector.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the SSH dispersion relation showing the central gap, and plot the QFI vs. dimerization strength, coupling strength, and detuning, demonstrating the gap-protected nature of the signal.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from defining the SSH model and the metrological observable (QFI) to deriving the bound-state condition via Dyson equations. It then systematically analyzes the QFI dependence on dimerization, coupling strength, and detuning, culminating in a detailed characterization of the gap-edge crossover.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study local phase estimation for a single quantum emitter coupled to a bosonic Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) bath within a microscopic lattice model. Dimerization opens a central gap supporting an in-gap emitter-bath bound state, which suppresses complete relaxation of the emitter coherence. A Dyson-equation analysis yields the local bath Green's function, the in-gap bound-state condition, and the emitter residue controlling the retained phase information. The phase quantum Fisher information links gap formation, detuning, and emitter-bath coupling to the post-transient metrological response. At resonance, stronger coupling enhances transient hybridization but reduces the retained signal by lowering the emitter weight in the bound state. Away from resonance, late-time averages, retention times, and useful interrogation windows track how phase-information protection weakens as the emitter is tuned toward and beyond the band edge. A uniform-chain control shows that the retained signal disappears when the gap closes. In the bulk local-coupling geometry considered here, the response is insensitive to the sign of the dimerization, so the protocol probes spectral-gap physics and bound-state support rather than the SSH winding sector.

</details>

### [Quantum Crossovers Revealed by Local Measurements](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29019v1)

**Authors:** A. C. S. Costa, E. C. Diniz, O. P. de Sa Neto  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29019v1>

**Summary.** This paper develops a method to identify quantum crossover points in open quantum systems using only local measurements, rather than relying on global properties. By comparing local diagnostics like Quantum Fisher Information against global measures like the QSE volume, the authors prove that local structure is a more fundamental indicator of crossover. This offers a powerful, experimentally accessible tool for characterizing quantum phase transitions.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant as it provides a rigorous, local diagnostic tool (QFI) to pinpoint critical quantum behavior in open systems, which is crucial for designing quantum sensors and understanding decoherence effects.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of robustly characterizing quantum crossover phenomena in open quantum systems, moving beyond reliance on global or model-dependent indicators.

**Main result.** It establishes that quantum crossovers can be robustly characterized using purely local measurements, specifically linking the onset of crossover to the behavior of the local Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) and the local Bloch vector.

**Method.** The authors employ theoretical analysis using a tripartite model, comparing local measurement diagnostics (QFI, local Bloch vectors) against global indicators (Quantum Steering Ellipsoid volume, Quantum Obesity).

**Model / system.** The study uses a minimal tripartite model of three qubits (A, B, C) governed by a Hamiltonian and subjected to dissipative dynamics described by a Microscopic Master Equation (MME). The system is analyzed at zero temperature.

**Key observables.** Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), local Bloch vectors, Quantum Steering Ellipsoid (QSE) volume, and Quantum Obesity (QO).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Tunable couplings ($J, J_C$), system frequencies ($\omega_0, \omega_C$), and the control parameter driving the crossover.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is performed on the global ground state $|\psi_Gangle$ and assumes the system dynamics are well-described by the MME.

**Figures summary.** Figures schematically illustrate the tripartite system and various observation schemes (full system, bipartitions). Other figures compare the behavior of QO and QSE volume against local measurement indicators as a coupling parameter is varied.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical model and the problem of crossover identification. It then develops and compares multiple diagnostic tools—local QFI vs. global QSE volume—to demonstrate the geometric distinction between these indicators.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum crossover phenomena play a central role in few-body open quantum systems, yet their identification often relies on global or model-dependent indicators. In this work, we demonstrate that crossovers can be robustly characterized through purely local measurements, establishing a direct connection between local quantum Fisher information and the onset of crossover behavior. We further demonstrate that quantum obesity does not, in general, generalize the quantum steering ellipsoid volume as a universal indicator of crossover. Instead, we identify regimes in which the ellipsoid volume remains insensitive to the transition, while the relevant signatures are encoded in the behavior of the local Bloch vector. These results reveal a geometric distinction between local and global indicators of crossovers.

</details>

### [A Quantum Algorithm for Simulating Nonunitary Dynamics Governed by Nonautonomous Linear Ordinary Differential Equations](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29052v1)

**Authors:** Pouya Khazaei, Eitan Geva  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29052v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29052_figures/2605.29052_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29052_figures/2605.29052_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Comparison of PD(t) as obtained by integrating Eq. 51 on a classical computer via the second-order</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a quantum algorithm, q-SVD-EOM, designed to simulate nonunitary quantum dynamics governed by time-dependent ODEs. It achieves this by decomposing the evolution operator via Singular Value Decomposition and evolving the resulting unitary and diagonal factors directly on quantum hardware. The method successfully demonstrates its applicability to molecular charge transfer dynamics, offering a promising route for simulating open quantum systems on near-term quantum computers.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, quantum-native pathway to simulate open quantum system dynamics, which are notoriously difficult for quantum hardware designed for unitary evolution, making it highly relevant for quantum simulation in AMO physics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of simulating nonunitary dynamics governed by nonautonomous linear ODEs ($\dot{v}(t) = A(t)v(t)$) on quantum hardware.

**Main result.** A novel quantum algorithm, q-SVD-EOM, was proposed that simulates the evolution by directly propagating the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) factors ($U, V, \Sigma_{\pm}$) on the quantum computer, bypassing the need for classical calculation of the nonunitary propagator.

**Method.** The method decomposes the nonunitary propagator using SVD ($\Phi(t) = U(t)\Sigma(t)V^\dagger(t)$) and then evolves the factors $U, V, \Sigma_{\pm}$ by casting their dynamics into coupled ODEs solvable via quantum gates (e.g., using Cayley transforms).

**Model / system.** The algorithm is demonstrated using a model for photoinduced charge transfer dynamics in a solvated molecular triad, governed by a time-dependent rate matrix derived from a quantum master equation.

**Key observables.** The donor population $\hat{ho}(t)$ and the time evolution of the SVD factors ($U(t), V(t), \Sigma_{\pm}(t)$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Time-dependent rate coefficients ($k_{D	o A}(t), k_{A	o D}(t)$) and the simulation time scale (e.g., $	ilde{t}=50 	ext{ au}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The algorithm assumes the ability to integrate the derived ODEs for the SVD factors directly on quantum hardware, and its performance is tested against ideal and noisy quantum backends.

**Figures summary.** Figure 3 compares the time evolution of the donor population $PD(t)$ obtained via classical Runge-Kutta (RK2), ideal quantum simulation, and noisy quantum simulations on two different hardware backends.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nonunitary dynamics, details the limitations of existing methods, proposes the q-SVD-EOM algorithm by deriving the factor evolution equations, and validates the method using a charge transfer model simulation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nonautonomous linear ordinary differential equations of the form $\dot{v}(t) = A(t)\, v(t)$, where $A(t)$ is non-skew-symmetric, are often used to describe nonunitary dynamics in a variety of fields that range from open quantum system dynamics to economic modeling. Because quantum computing hardware is designed to natively implement unitary transformations, existing algorithms for solving such equations on quantum hardware are based on the assumption that the nonunitary propagator is known, and use dilation techniques to embed the nonunitary dynamics within the unitary dynamics of a larger system. However, with the exception of cases where the nonunitary propagator is known in closed form, it needs to be calculated and manipulated on a classical computer at each time step. In this paper, we propose a quantum algorithm that does not require a priori knowledge of the explicit nonunitary propagator and effectively performs the dilation on the quantum hardware. Our algorithm combines a dilation scheme that uses singular value decomposition (SVD) to write the nonunitary propagator as a sum of unitaries with simulating the dynamics of the SVD factors on the quantum hardware. The population-only time-convolutionless quantum master equation describing photoinduced charge transfer in a solvated molecular triad is used as a demonstrative example of the applicability of the algorithm and its sensitivity to noise.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.29957"></a>
### [Universal thermokinetic decomposition of short-time information fluctuations](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29957v1)

**Authors:** Giorgio Nicoletti, Daniel M. Busiello  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29957v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `driven-dissipative phase transition` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5**

These fluctuations shape the information shared between present and future states, which is captured by the distribution of the pointwise mutual information (pMI) between x0 and xτ, iτ. While a dissipative driving f does not impact the aver- age pMI, Iτ, higher dissipation is in general able to reduce the short-time fluctuations of information, Fτ, quantified by the variance of the pMI. (b) For a one-dimensional Ornstein- Uhlenbeck (OU) process with F(x) = −kx, Iτ diverges loga- rithmically as τ →0, while Fτ...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29957_figures/2605.29957_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. (a-c) Steady-state distribution pst of a particle in a one-dimensional periodic potential with a nonequilibrium driving f. The entropy production Σ increases with f, while the varentropy FH(−log pst) decreases as the distribution be- comes more uniform. (d) Information fluctuations Fτ are sup- pressed by the nonequilibrium driving, up to the theoretical minimum (gray dashed line). Our short-time theory (solid lines) is well-matched by numerical simulations (circles) even for relatively large values of τ. In these panels, k = 2 and D = 1. (e-f) In a stochastic oscillator, entropy production quantifies long-term precision as it is related to the number of coherent oscillations, and is...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work develops a universal decomposition showing that information fluctuations in stochastic systems are governed by a balance between energy dissipation and dynamical activity. While the average predictability is robust, the variance of this predictability is fundamentally controlled by thermodynamic quantities like entropy production. This offers a new thermodynamic lens to analyze information reliability in complex, non-equilibrium physical and biological processes.

**Why it may be interesting.** The finding that thermodynamic constraints govern information *fluctuations* but not the average information transfer provides a novel, fundamental thermodynamic basis for understanding precision limits in non-equilibrium systems, relevant to open quantum systems modeling.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To understand how energy dissipation and dynamical activity influence the reliability of information acquisition in physical systems by characterizing the fluctuations of short-time information predictability.

**Main result.** Information fluctuations exhibit a universal thermokinetic decomposition, showing that thermodynamics controls the *fluctuations* of predictability, while the average predictability is independent of underlying thermodynamic details.

**Method.** The analysis focuses on the short-time behavior of the pointwise mutual information (pMI) using the Moment Generating Function (MGF) derived from the Langevin dynamics.

**Model / system.** The study applies to arbitrary Langevin dynamics for a system with N degrees of freedom, exemplified by Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes, Brownian motors, and stochastic oscillators near a Hopf bifurcation.

**Key observables.** Pointwise Mutual Information (pMI), Information Fluctuations ($F_	au$), Average Predictability ($I_	au$), Entropy Production Rate ($\Sigma$), and Stochastic Action ($T$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Short time ($	au$), dimensionality ($N$), and the strength of dissipation/driving forces.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation relies on the assumption of stationarity for the process, though extensions to non-stationary and multiplicative noise are mentioned.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the relationship between information fluctuations ($F_	au$) and thermodynamic quantities ($\Sigma, T$), showing how dissipation suppresses fluctuations while dynamical activity influences them.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the theoretical framework by defining pMI and analyzing its short-time MGF. It then derives the universal decomposition for $F_	au$, contrasting it with the insensitivity of $I_	au$ to thermodynamic details, and applies this to various physical models.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Biological, artificial, and physical systems dissipate energy to accurately transmit information. While tools of information theory have been used to characterize information-processing capabilities, how reliably this information is acquired along individual trajectories, and which aspects require a thermodynamic cost, is an open question. In this work, we focus on the stochastic predictability of an arbitrary Langevin dynamics, defined as the pointwise mutual information between the current and future states of a system. We show that the fluctuations of predictability obey a universal thermokinetic decomposition at short times, which reveals that information fluctuations are suppressed by energy dissipation and become stronger with increased dynamical activity. Remarkably, we find that the average predictability, i.e., the short-time mutual information, does not carry any dependence on the underlying thermodynamic and kinetic features. Thus, the role of dissipation at short times is not to enhance information, but to reduce its fluctuations. Such dissipative control is effective only when instantiated by nonlinear operations. Moreover, energy consumption governs short- and long-time precision in stochastic oscillators through structurally different mechanisms that can be independently tuned. Our decomposition offers a fundamental thermodynamic basis for understanding the reliability of information transmission in nonequilibrium systems, the constraints on precision in biological systems, and the design of energy-limited control strategies.

</details>

<sub>FIG. 2. Disorder-averaged logarithm of the wave density ⟨ln P(r)⟩at three evolution times, T1 = 103, T2 = 104, T3 = 105, for four representative values of t1. (b) t1 = 1.12 (TAI), the profiles also approach a common exponential form at long times, with the localiza- tion length ξ = 3.3. **Summary.** This work examines how disorder-induced topological phase transitions affect quantum transport by analyzing the fluctuations in the wave-packet density. The key finding is that these fluctuations are anomalously enhanced near the transition point due to multiple energy scales contributing, establishing wave-packet statistics as a powerful diagnostic tool for such transitions.

**Why it may be interesting.** The finding that wave-packet fluctuation statistics serve as a dynamical diagnostic for topological transitions is highly relevant for understanding transport in disordered quantum materials, connecting localization theory to topological invariants.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how a disorder-driven topological phase transition affects the spatial fluctuations of the logarithmic wave-packet density, $\ln P(r)$, in localized quantum states.

**Main result.** The fluctuation growth exponent ($	heta$) is enhanced above the conventional 1D value ($	heta \simeq 1/2$) near the transition due to multiple energy sectors contributing, but returns to $\simeq 1/2$ at the critical point.

**Method.** The analysis uses wave-packet propagation simulations and theoretical characterization via the zero-energy transfer-matrix recursion to diagnose the transition.

**Model / system.** The model is a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) chain subjected to off-diagonal disorder, which preserves chiral symmetry. The system is analyzed across topological (TI/TAI) and trivial insulating phases.

**Key observables.** Standard deviation of the logarithmic wave-packet density, $\sigma[\ln P(r)]$, and the associated scaling exponent $	heta$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Disorder strength ($W$), hopping ratios ($t_1, t_2$), and the energy $E$ relative to zero energy.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the thermodynamic limit ($N 	o \infty$) and the applicability of power-law fitting for scaling exponents.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the phase diagram (TI, TAI, NI) based on winding numbers, the energy-resolved density of states, and plots showing the fluctuation exponent $	heta$ vs. disorder strength, highlighting non-monotonic behavior.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the physical model and diagnostic tools (e.g., $\sigma[\ln P(r)]$). It then analyzes the system away from the transition, showing conventional scaling. The core of the work focuses on the non-monotonic enhancement of $	heta$ near the transition, followed by a discussion of the underlying multi-scale spectral weight contributions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The universal statistics of density fluctuations of localized quantum states may offer unprecedented opportunities to probe and understand quantum transport in connection with dimensionality, coherence, symmetry and disorder. To date, the possible role of topological phase transitions in the fluctuation statistics is not studied yet. Using a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger chain subject to off-diagonal disorder (so that chiral symmetry is preserved), this work investigates how a disorder driven topological phase transition impacts on the spatial fluctuations of the logarithmic wave-packet density $\ln P(r)$ at distance $r$ from the initial excitation. Away from the transition, in both topological and trivial localized phases, the standard deviation follows the conventional one-dimensional scaling $σ[\ln P(r)]\sim r^θ$ with $θ\simeq 1/2$. Near the transition, however, the fluctuation growth is enhanced: the fitted exponent $θ$ increases above $1/2$ in a nonmonotonic manner before returning close to $1/2$ at criticality. We interpret this behavior from the energy-resolved density of states and localization length. Near the transition, several energy sectors carry appreciable spectral weight and exhibit competitive decay rates, preventing a single localization scale from dominating the accessible wave-packet tail and thereby enhancing the fluctuations of $\ln P(r)$. Our results establish wave-packet fluctuation statistics as a dynamical diagnostic of disordered chiral topological transitions and motivate broader studies of fluctuation phenomena in disordered topological quantum systems.

</details>

### [Tripartite Interactions Induced Strongly Correlated Quantum Emissions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29694v1)

**Authors:** Qian Bin, Ying Wu, Franco Nori, Xin-You Lü

**Summary.** This paper theoretically demonstrates that direct tripartite interactions in a cavity-atom-mechanical system can efficiently generate strongly correlated multiquanta photon-phonon emissions. By analyzing the system's parity structure and engineering two-photon dissipation, the authors show a pathway to control emission, enabling both even- and odd-quanta correlated states. This has significant implications for building advanced hybrid quantum network components.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and quantum optics as it provides a concrete theoretical framework for enhancing quantum light sources by engineering interactions (tripartite coupling) and dissipation channels (two-photon loss).

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of efficiently generating strongly correlated multiquanta quantum emissions, which typically requires complex, higher-order quantum processes.

**Main result.** Direct tripartite interactions enable high-efficiency multiquanta emission by bypassing intermediate state transitions, and engineered two-photon dissipation allows for the controlled generation of odd-quanta states.

**Method.** The study uses theoretical modeling, deriving effective Hamiltonians and transition rates using perturbation theory (Fermi's Golden Rule) and analyzing system dynamics via master equations.

**Model / system.** The system is a hybrid quantum setup comprising a cavity mode, a two-level atom, and a mechanical oscillator, coupled via a direct tripartite interaction. The dynamics are governed by a Hamiltonian and analyzed using master equations.

**Key observables.** Strongly correlated even-quanta and odd-quanta emission rates, photon-phonon cross-correlations ($g^{(2)}_{ab}$), and effective multiquanta transition rates ($W_{	ext{eff}}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coupling strengths ($\lambda$), detunings ($\Delta_a, \omega_b$), driving amplitudes ($\Omega$), and dissipation rates ($\kappa$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the rotating-wave approximation (RWA) and the assumption that the two-boson-mode subsystem preserves parity symmetry.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate system schematics, energy spectra showing avoided crossings, time evolution of state populations, and comparisons between analytically derived and numerically simulated effective transition rates.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from establishing the tripartite interaction model, demonstrating even-quanta emission under dissipation, and then crucially extending the analysis to show how engineered two-photon dissipation enables the generation of odd-quanta emission.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Efficient generation of multiquanta emission is crucial for quantum information processing but remains challenging due to its typical reliance on higher-order quantum processes. Here, we theoretically demonstrate strongly correlated photon-phonon emission enabled by direct tripartite interaction. This interaction facilitates the formation of high-order multiquanta states without more intermediate state transitions, thereby avoiding the suppressed transition rates associated with multiple sequential processes and substantially improving resonant transitions. As a result, high-efficiency strongly correlated even-quanta emission (e.g., two photons and two phonons) can be achieved in the presences of dissipation. Beyond that, we show that introducing two-photon dissipation enables strongly correlated odd-quanta emission (e.g., two photons and one phonon) in the tripartite interaction system by parity-protected suppression of single-photon loss and reconstruction of higher-order multiquanta processes. Our work extends multiquanta emission into the tripartite coupling regime and holds promising potential for applications in hybrid quantum networks.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates the ability to engineer and optically probe donor-acceptor pairs (DAPs) in diamond nanolayers by controlling the separation distance between the donor and acceptor sites using an intermediate CVD layer. By measuring the PL intensity and lifetime as a function of this separation, the authors confirm that DAP recombination is the source of the narrowband emission, establishing a tunable platform for quantum emitters.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a highly controllable, solid-state platform for engineering quantum emitters whose optical properties (emission energy, intensity, lifetime) can be tuned by controlling the physical separation between two localized electronic states, which is a key concept in quantum optics and AMO physics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the optical properties of donor-acceptor pairs (DAPs) formed between near-surface acceptor species and substitutional nitrogen donors in hydrogenated diamond nanolayers.

**Main result.** The PL intensity and lifetime exhibit a non-monotonic dependence on the intermediate CVD layer thickness, peaking at a characteristic separation distance of approximately 1.7 nm.

**Method.** The research combines confocal photoluminescence (PL) microspectroscopy, time-resolved measurements, and electrostatic force microscopy (EFM) mapping.

**Model / system.** The physical system is hydrogenated homoepitaxial diamond nanolayers, where the donor-acceptor separation distance ($d$) is precisely tuned by inserting a nominally undoped CVD interlayer. The recombination process is modeled using a quantum yield ($\eta(d)$) derived from radiative and non-radiative rates.

**Key observables.** Bright, spectrally narrow PL lines (545-650 nm), PL intensity vs. thickness, PL lifetime ($	au_{mean}$), and EFM phase contrast changes.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Donor-acceptor separation distance ($d$), which is tunable via the CVD layer thickness (optimal range 1.5-2.5 nm).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the hydrogen-like model for DAP recombination and assumes the PL intensity is a product of the quantum yield and an energy matching function.

**Figures summary.** Figures show PL intensity maps correlating with topography, and PL spectra demonstrating the dependence of emission on nanolayer thickness, confirming the maximum intensity in the 1.5-2.5 nm range.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the physical system (H-terminated diamond on N-doped substrate), details the theoretical model linking PL to separation distance, presents measurements showing thickness dependence, and concludes by confirming DAP recombination as the source of narrowband emission.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Hydrogen-terminated diamond is known for its p-type surface conductivity, which arises from a near-surface hole accumulation layer induced by adsorbed acceptor species. Here, we demonstrate that these surface acceptors also form optically active donor-acceptor pairs (DAP) with substitutional nitrogen donors in diamond. The insertion of a nominally undoped CVD interlayer between a nitrogen-rich HPHT substrate and a hydrogen-terminated surface enables the precise tuning of the donor-acceptor separation with nanometer precision. Radiative DAP recombination appears as bright, spectrally narrow lines whose intensity, energy, and decay dynamics depend systematically on interlayer thickness. Individual lines show single-photon statistics, while ensembles exhibit strong polarization anisotropy reflecting the planar donor-acceptor geometry. These findings reveal an optical counterpart of hydrogen-induced surface transfer doping in diamond and establish a surface-defined, nanometer-tunable platform for engineering DAP-based quantum emitters.

</details>

### [Quantum Synchronization of Fock States](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30271v1)

**Authors:** Fabian Hassler, David Scheer, Samah Saquaque, Steven Kim  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30271v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `driven-dissipative phase transition` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `Tavis-Cummings & cavity-many-emitter` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `correlated cavity matter` **1/5**

It consists of a Josephson junction, with Josephson energy EJ, that is put in series with two LC-resonators with frequencies ωj and characteristic impedances Zj, j ∈{a, b}. The DC voltage-bias V is tuned to the value 2eV = ℏ(ωa+ωb) such that a tunneling Cooper pair can resonantly excite one photon in each resonator. The resonators are capacitively coupled to transmission lines, described by the damping rates γj. Through the transmission line, an external drive Iac can be applied to resonator a which allows the study of quantum synchronization to an external drive.</sub>

</details>

We see that the external drive injects its phase into the system via the localization of the Wigner function at ϕ = θ = 0. Importantly, the negativity persists and oscillations can be observed inside the limit-cycle, resembling the features of a Fock state. (b) Ratio of the variance ⟨⟨n2⟩⟩to the average photon number ¯n. For increasing driving strength f, the ratio increases but remains smaller than 1. The red dot corresponds to f = 2γa for which the Wigner function is shown in (a).</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.30271_figures/2605.30271_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

We see that the system goes from free phase diffusion (unsynchro- nized) to exponentially suppressed phase diffusion (synchro- nized) for small ∆or large f. The diagram reveals a so-called Arnold tongue, a benchmark for synchronization. Panels (b) and (c) reveal the exponential suppression. Black lines corre- spond to a numerical evaluation while the red dashed lines are fits to ln Γ± ∝−f√¯n as expected from the Kramers’ result. In (b), ∆= 0, while in (c), ∆= 0, f = γa, and n0 ∈[30, 80] leading to different ¯n....</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops a quantum theory to study synchronization in non-classical states, using a superconducting circuit model. It argues that synchronization must be measured dynamically by analyzing phase-slip rates derived from the system's Lindblad evolution. The key finding is that phase-locking is confirmed by the exponential suppression of phase diffusion, mapping out an Arnold tongue structure.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous, dynamical framework for defining and measuring quantum synchronization, which is crucial for understanding driven, dissipative quantum systems beyond simple steady-state analysis.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates quantum synchronization, a dynamical property, in non-classical quantum states, moving beyond insufficient stationary state analyses.

**Main result.** Quantum synchronization is characterized by the exponential suppression of phase diffusion (or phase-slip rates) when the system phase locks to an external drive within an Arnold tongue regime.

**Method.** The dynamics are analyzed using the Lindblad master equation, leading to a Fokker-Planck equation for the phase distribution, and phase-slip rates are extracted from this evolution.

**Model / system.** The system is a superconducting circuit modeled by a Josephson junction coupled to two single-mode resonators, evolving under a Lindblad equation. The analysis focuses on the phase dynamics $P(\phi, t)$ and the extended phase $\varphi$.

**Key observables.** Phase diffusion $\langle[\varphi(t) - \varphi(0)]^2angle$, phase-slip rates ($\Gamma_\pm$), and the Wigner function (to confirm non-classicality).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Loss rate ($\gamma_a$), nonlinear gain ($\epsilon$), detuning ($\Delta$), and driving strength ($f$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The semi-classical approach is insufficient due to the non-classical nature of the Fock state; the analysis relies on deriving the Fokker-Planck equation from the Lindblad evolution.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the Wigner function confirming phase localization and negativity upon driving, and phase diffusion plots revealing an Arnold tongue structure corresponding to synchronization.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the physical model (superconducting circuit) to deriving the phase dynamics via the Lindblad equation, contrasting insufficient stationary state measures with the necessary dynamical measure of phase-slip rates.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Synchronization, a ubiquitous phenomenon in classical systems, has recently been extended to the quantum domain. Here, we show quantum synchronization of a bosonic mode exhibiting a Fock state-like limit cycle, manifesting as a steady state with a negative Wigner function. We demonstrate that this non-classical state can be phase-locked to an external drive, achieving synchronization within an Arnold tongue regime. We argue that synchronization is a dynamical property and fundamentally tied to the suppression of phase slips, which we show to occur with exponentially decreasing probability. We introduce a novel method to extract the phase slip rate from the Lindblad time evolution of the system. This work opens new avenues for understanding and manipulating non-classical synchronization dynamics.

</details>

<img src="2605.29896_figures/2605.29896_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. M/H-T and M-H of NdOF and comparison with CEF modeling. (a) – (d) M/H-T under fields of 0.1, 3, 5, and 9 T. The CEF simulations (blue solid lines) reproduce the experimental data (symbols) over the entire range. (e), (f) M-H at 1.8 and 10 K. The CEF model (blue solid lines) is in good agreement with experiment. (g), (h) Calculated CEF magnetization for fields along the c-axis and within the ab-plane at 1.8 K.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work investigates NdOF, a rare-earth magnet, to show that its ground state can be controlled by external magnetic fields. By combining Raman spectroscopy and magnetization measurements with a CEF model, the authors prove that the ground state evolves from a simple dipolar state to a complex dipolar-multipolar state. This establishes NdOF as a key platform for studying the controlled induction of higher-order magnetic multipolar moments.

**Why it may be interesting.** The field-tunable induction of multipolar character is highly relevant to AMO physics and open quantum systems, as it demonstrates how external fields can drive a quantum system's ground state into a complex, non-simple magnetic state, which can be modeled using effective Hamiltonians.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The research aims to understand and control the transition of the ground state in rare-earth magnets from a purely dipolar character to a mixed dipolar-multipolar character.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that the ground state doublet in NdOF can be continuously tuned from dipolar to dipolar-multipolar by applying external magnetic fields, establishing the material as a model system for studying multipolar interactions.

**Method.** The study combines experimental techniques like Raman spectroscopy and magnetization measurements with theoretical modeling using the Crystal Electric Field (CEF) Hamiltonian.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the honeycomb rare-earth chalcohalide NdOF, where the magnetic properties are governed by the CEF Hamiltonian acting on the $J=9/2$ manifold of the Nd$^{3+}$ ion. The Hamiltonian includes terms up to the sixth power of Stevens operators, constrained by local $C_{3v}$ symmetry.

**Key observables.** CEF excitation energies (1.7, 15.6, 19.2, 80.9 meV), magnetization ($M$), susceptibility ($\chi$), and the ratio of dipolar-octupolar moments.

**Important parameters / regimes.** External magnetic field strength (0.1–9 T) and temperature (low T regime).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the structure remains stable and that the observed excitations are electronic in origin, allowing the use of the CEF model.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the crystal structure and low-temperature Raman spectra revealing four intrinsic CEF peaks. Further figures illustrate the field-induced nonlinear splitting of these excitations and the temperature/field dependence of magnetization matching the CEF model.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the material and the CEF model using Raman spectroscopy and magnetization measurements. It then uses the field dependence of the excitations and magnetic data to show that the ground state undergoes a continuous evolution from dipolar to multipolar character, controlled by the external field.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Field-tunable reconstruction of crystalline electric field (CEF) doublets offers a promising avenue for inducing multipolar character, while its observation in real materials has been little explored so far. Here we establish the honeycomb rare-earth chalcohalide NdOF as such a platform. Raman spectroscopy identifies four CEF excitations at 1.7, 15.6, 19.2, and 80.9~meV, and a Zeeman--CEF analysis reproduces their nonlinear field splitting into seven branches. Magnetization and susceptibility over 0.1--9~T are well described by a CEF model for the total angular momentum $J = 9/2$ manifold, confirming the robustness of the extracted CEF scheme. These results demonstrate a field-driven continuous evolution of the ground-state doublet from dipolar to dipolar-multipolar character, with pressure providing a complementary tuning knob, establishing NdOF as a model system for exploring the controlled induction of multipolar components in rare-earth magnets.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores how incorporating modular couplings into non-Hermitian lattice models enriches their topological features, particularly concerning the non-Hermitian skin effect. By analyzing spectral topological phase transitions associated with point gap closure, the authors demonstrate that these systems exhibit enhanced, Heisenberg-limited sensitivity for quantum sensing, even when estimating multiple unknown parameters simultaneously.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly connects topological phase transitions (a core concept in condensed matter theory) to practical quantum sensing limits, providing a concrete physical mechanism (modular structure) to engineer superior metrology performance in open quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how introducing modular structures enriches the topological properties of non-Hermitian systems, specifically focusing on the non-Hermitian skin effect and its application to enhancing quantum sensing.

**Main result.** The modular structure allows for the identification of critical points where spectral topological phase transitions occur, leading to a measurable enhancement in sensing performance, even in multi-parameter estimation scenarios.

**Method.** The analysis employs the Generalized Brillouin Zone (GBZ) formalism and calculates topological invariants like the winding number to characterize phase transitions, alongside applying quantum estimation theory (QFIM) for sensing.

**Model / system.** The model is a general 1D tight-binding non-Hermitian Hamiltonian, extended to include modular couplings ($J_m, J'_m$) across $L$ modules. Specific examples include the modular Hatano-Nelson and SSH models.

**Key observables.** Non-Hermitian skin effect, point/line gaps, winding number, Quantum Fisher Information Matrix (QFIM), and the scaling exponent $b$ of sensing metrics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Modular couplings ($J_m, J'_m$), module size ($r$), and the ratio of non-reciprocal couplings ($J_R/J_L$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the GBZ formalism to correctly map the bulk spectrum to the open boundary condition (OBC) spectrum, and the sensing analysis assumes the probe state is the right eigenstate with the largest imaginary eigenvalue.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the spectral topological phase transitions (point gap closure) for modular models, showing the skin effect vanishing at critical points, and demonstrate the quadratic scaling of QFIM elements for enhanced sensing.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the general NH model and its topological features (skin effect, gap closure) using the GBZ formalism, to analyzing the specific role of modular couplings in controlling these transitions, and finally applying this framework to derive and quantify the enhanced sensitivity in multi-parameter quantum estimation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Non-Hermitian topological systems have attracted a lot of research activities in recent times, both theoretically and experimentally, due to their unique physical properties and association with open quantum systems. We show that modular structures, where specific couplings at regular intervals take distinct values, enrich the unique topological attributes of these systems such as the non-Hermitian skin effect and the breakdown of conventional bulk-boundary correspondence. These systems also possess the capability of displaying criticality-enhanced sensitivity for precision metrology. We establish how the modular structure enhances their sensing performance near spectral topological phase transitions and show that the enhancement can be achieved in multi-parameter estimation scenarios as well.

</details>
