**Summary.** This theoretical study investigates the noise properties of parametrically driven cavity solitons (PDCSs) in Kerr resonators compared to conventional solitons. By analyzing quantum and classical noise sources, the authors demonstrate that PDCSs offer superior performance, particularly in terms of reduced timing jitter and enhanced quantum squeezing. This suggests PDCSs are highly promising for next-generation, low-noise photonic quantum applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work bridges quantum optics and nonlinear wave physics by quantifying noise resilience in a novel optical soliton system, providing concrete metrics (squeezing, jitter) for quantum resource assessment.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to elucidate how both technical (classical) and quantum noise sources affect the unique pure-Kerr parametrically driven cavity soliton (PDCS) structure.

**Main result.** PDCSs are shown to outperform conventional cavity solitons across key metrics, exhibiting lower fundamental timing jitter, reduced phase noise, and stronger two-mode squeezing.

**Method.** The authors employ theoretical modeling, analyzing the system using the generalized Lugiato-Lefever equation and linearizing quantum fluctuations around the mean field.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a dispersive Kerr resonator driven by bichromatic fields, modeled by the Lugiato-Lefever equation. The analysis quantizes this system by treating fluctuations as operators and solving the resulting linear equations in the frequency domain.

**Key observables.** Fundamental quantum-limited timing jitter, two-mode squeezing spectra, and phase noise transfer from driving lasers.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The relative strength of noise ($\epsilon$), the mode number ($l$), and the detuning/parametric driving strength ($\Delta_{	ext{eff}}, \mu$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often assumes that the system can be linearized around the mean field, and for noise calculations, the noise is assumed to be white and Gaussian.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare line profiles and normalized linewidths for CS vs. PDCS, and show the two-mode quadrature spectra, demonstrating superior squeezing and narrower lines for PDCSs.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically develops the model from the classical LLE to the quantized fluctuation equations, deriving analytical and numerical methods to calculate noise spectra and jitter, culminating in a direct comparison between PDCS and CS performance.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Temporal cavity solitons generated in monochromatically driven dispersive Kerr resonators offer an attractive avenue for on-chip optical frequency comb generation. Key to many of their applications is to understand how noise -- both technical and quantum -- affects the soliton states, which has accordingly been extensively investigated. Here, we report on a comprehensive theoretical study that elucidates how technical and quantum fluctuations impact a new type of soliton structure that has very recently been predicted and observed in dispersive Kerr resonators under conditions of bichromatic driving: the pure-Kerr parametrically driven cavity soliton (PDCS). We examine how classical laser phase noise transfers from the two pump fields onto the soliton frequency comb, and we calculate the solitons' fundamental quantum-limited timing jitter and two-mode squeezing spectra. In each case, we find that PDCSs can out-perform conventional cavity solitons with comparable characteristics, even when driven by two uncorrelated lasers. Our results demonstrate that pure-Kerr PDCSs can offer unprecedented performance in noise-sensitive photonic applications and as a quantum resource

</details>

### [Engineering recoil heating in coherent-scattering levitated optomechanics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29609v1)

**Authors:** Maksim Lednev, Uroš Delić, Johannes Feist, Carlos Gonzalez-Ballestero  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29609v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **3/5** · `correlated cavity matter` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `Tavis-Cummings & cavity-many-emitter` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

In free space, the angular distribution of Stokes and anti-Stokes photons scattered by the center-of-mass degree of freedom follows the characteristic information radiation pattern [8, 33] (shown in blue for the motion along y). Inside a cavity, the recoil heating rate changes as (i) part of the scattered photons populate the cavity mode instead of free-space, and (ii) the scattering pattern itself is modified by Purcell effect.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper develops a general quantum electrodynamics framework to calculate recoil heating in levitated nanoparticles trapped near optical cavities. It demonstrates that the cavity environment significantly modifies the heating rate, predicting that this decoherence can be actively suppressed by designing the photonic structure. This offers a pathway to engineer quantum motional states in optomechanical systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful, general theoretical tool to engineer decoherence in levitated optomechanics by designing the surrounding photonic structure, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and cavity QED.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Recoil heating from photon scattering is a major source of decoherence in optical trapping, and a predictive theory for this rate in cavity setups is missing.

**Main result.** The recoil heating rate is significantly modified by the electromagnetic environment, and the authors predict it can be strongly suppressed in state-of-the-art microcavities.

**Method.** The authors developed a general theoretical framework using macroscopic quantum electrodynamics and few-mode quantization to derive a coherent scattering master equation.

**Model / system.** The system involves a nanoparticle trapped in an optical tweezer near a microcavity, modeled by a Hamiltonian incorporating center-of-mass and librational motion, and analyzed via a master equation.

**Key observables.** Recoil heating rate, spectral density, coupling rate, and cavity linewidth.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Cavity parameters (e.g., mirror angular size $	heta_m$), particle mass $M$, and cavity detuning.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on approximations such as the Lamb-Dicke approximation and the Born-Markov approximation for mode elimination.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the setup comparing free-space vs. cavity scattering patterns, and show the dependence of coupling rates and normalized recoil heating on cavity geometry parameters.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from identifying the theoretical gap in cavity recoil heating to developing a general QED framework, applying it to specific cavity geometries (Fabry-Pérot), and finally deriving quantitative predictions for rate suppression.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recoil heating from photon scattering is a fundamental source of decoherence in optical trapping, severely limiting the preparation of nonclassical motional states. In cavity setups in the coherent scattering configuration, a predictive theory of recoil heating rate is missing, as usual perturbative approaches fail in the presence of sharp optical resonances. Hence, current works assume that the recoil heating rate is approximately equal to its free-space value. Here we show that this is not the case, as the electromagnetic environment can strongly modify recoil heating rate by the Purcell effect. Specifically, we predict that this rate can be significantly suppressed in state-of-the-art microcavities, for both center-of-mass and librational motion. To establish these results, we develop a general theoretical framework based on macroscopic quantum electrodynamics and on the few-mode quantization approach developed in nanophotonics. Our method applies to particles trapped in the presence of arbitrary electromagnetic structures, thus providing a route to engineering motional decoherence in levitated optomechanics by photonic structure design.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.29201"></a>
### [Partial Entropy production of active particles with hidden states in potentials](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29201v1)

**Authors:** Jacob Knight, Gunnar Pruessner  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29201v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29201_figures/2605.29201_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Run-and-tumble (RnT) process with (i) fully resolved (red corresponding to self-propulsion towards the right, blue to self-propulsion to the left) and (ii) hidden self-propulsion velocity, in the left and right columns respectively. Rows cor- respond to a: a) free, and b) harmonically confined, RnT par- ticle. In a), trajectories break time-reversal symmetry (TRS) when fully resolved, as revealed by the effective swapping of colours under mirroring along the vertical axis. They obey TRS when the self-propulsion state is hidden. The process therefore has positive total EPR ˙Sx,w &gt; 0 and zero partial EPR ˙Sx = 0. In b), trajectories break TRS even with hidden self-propulsion,...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical paper develops a framework to calculate the partial entropy production rate for active particles whose self-propulsion is hidden. Using advanced path integral and field-theoretic methods, the authors derive explicit, high-order expressions for this rate for models like the RnT particle. The results show how the partial observation of the system fundamentally alters the apparent time-reversal symmetry breaking compared to the full description.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous, field-theoretic approach to quantifying non-equilibrium thermodynamics in active matter, which is crucial for understanding energy dissipation and irreversible processes in complex biological or synthetic systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper quantifies the partial entropy production rate ($\dot{S}_x$) for active particles whose self-propulsion mechanism is hidden from observation, addressing how partial observation can mask non-equilibrium dynamics.

**Main result.** The partial EPR ($\dot{S}_x$) is derived through complex path integral expansions, yielding results that depend on the correlation functions of the hidden self-propulsion noise $w(t)$ and the confining potential $V(x)$.

**Method.** The methodology involves extending perturbative path integral frameworks, utilizing cumulant expansions, and employing Doi-Peliti field theory techniques to calculate time-ordered correlation functions.

**Model / system.** The system models overdamped active particles governed by a Langevin equation incorporating a confining potential $V(x)$ and a stochastic self-propulsion term $w(t)$. Specific models analyzed include the Active Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Particle (AOUP) and the Run-and-Tumble (RnT) particle.

**Key observables.** Partial Entropy Production Rate ($\dot{S}_x$), Full Entropy Production Rate ($\dot{S}_{x,w}$), and various time-ordered correlation functions (up to six-time).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Self-propulsion strength ($
u$), diffusion coefficient ($D$), and the parameters defining the confining potential ($k$ for harmonic potential).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the existence of a unique steady state and relies on approximations such as leading order in $
u$ and the use of the Stratonovich convention.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the comparison of full vs. partial EPR for RnT processes in free and harmonic cases, showing how hidden states change the observable asymmetry.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from the general formalism of partial EPR using path probability ratios, applies this to specific models (AOUP, RnT), and details the calculation using advanced field-theoretic diagrammatic expansions for higher-order correlation functions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Partially observed stochastic systems can appear (almost) time-reversal symmetric while in fact operating far from equilibrium. The present work extends the perturbative framework introduced in [Phys. Rev. Lett. 136, 198302 (2026)] to calculate in a generic confining potential the partial entropy production, which quantifies the time-reversal asymmetry of a generic active particle with hidden self-propulsion. Focusing on the harmonic case, we apply our framework to reproduce an exact result for the partial entropy production rate of an active Ornstein-Uhlenbeck particle and to derive the partial entropy production rate of a run-and-tumble particle.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a quantum-classical hybrid method, QAVG-DMFT, to calculate the Green's function for correlated materials like $	ext{SrVO}_3$ using quantum computers. It uses modified Quantum Phase Estimation circuits to sample the GF at finite temperature, which is then reconstructed classically by averaging over variable grids. This advances the feasibility of simulating strongly correlated electron systems on near-term quantum hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** It bridges the gap between quantum computation and strongly correlated materials physics by providing a concrete, albeit complex, pathway to calculate fundamental quantities like the Green's function.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a quantum-classical hybrid scheme to treat correlated electronic systems using Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) on quantum computers.

**Main result.** The proposed QPE averaged over variable grids (QAVG) scheme successfully reconstructs the Green's function by minimizing discrepancies between measured quantum sampling data and theoretical model distributions.

**Method.** The method combines modified Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) circuits to sample the Green's function (GF) at finite temperature with a classical optimization routine (QAVG) to reconstruct the GF parameters.

**Model / system.** The theory is applied to correlated electronic systems, demonstrated using $	ext{SrVO}_3$ within the framework of the Anderson Impurity Model (AIM) derived from DFT.

**Key observables.** One-particle Green's function (GF), Density of States (DOS), spectral amplitudes, and excitation energies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Finite temperature ($eta$), trial parameters ($\Lambda$), and the number of measurement shots ($M_d$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The QPE sampling data is processed via QAVG, which models the GF using a limited set of trial parameters, overcoming the need to know the specific excitation channel.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the QAVG-DMFT workflow, comparing reconstructed DOS/GFs against exact calculations (FCI) and showing the structure of excitation circuits.

**Paper structure.** The paper reviews the GF definition and DFT+DMFT structure before detailing the QAVG-based quantum-classical hybrid scheme, culminating in numerical simulations for $	ext{SrVO}_3$.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

For treating correlated electronic systems on quantum computers, we propose a quantum-classical hybrid scheme for dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT). In the quantum part of the scheme, we use modified quantum phase estimation (QPE) circuits suitable for the one-particle Green's function (GF) at a finite temperature so that we can extract spectral amplitudes and the excitation energies without knowing the excitation channel invoked at each measurement. In the classical part of the scheme, we adopt an approach that estimates reasonably the GF based on the data collected from the QPE sampling. We dub the approach the QPE averaged over variable grids (QAVG), that may help one to reconstruct the GF via optimization of trial parameters and modeling the probability distributions for various settings of the QPE circuits. We apply the QAVG-DMFT scheme to SrVO$_3$ to demonstrate its validity via numerical simulations.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops the Double Covariance Model (DCM) to derive quantum dynamics from classical stochastic processes. By employing multi-scale calculus and coarse-graining, the authors show that the standard GKSL master equation emerges naturally as the effective macroscopic equation. This framework unifies quantum theory by showing the von Neumann equation is a limiting case of the general stochastic evolution.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a deep, non-standard foundation for open quantum systems, suggesting that the GKSL structure is an emergent statistical consequence of underlying classical noise, rather than a postulate.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to derive the standard, Markovian evolution equations of quantum mechanics (like the GKSL equation) from a more fundamental, underlying classical stochastic process.

**Main result.** The Double Covariance Model successfully reproduces the GKSL master equation in the macroscopic limit, while the closed-system von Neumann equation emerges as the fluctuation-free limit.

**Method.** It employs multi-scale Itô calculus and a hydrodynamic coarse-graining procedure, averaging over fast microscopic fluctuations to derive the effective macroscopic dynamics.

**Model / system.** The model uses the Double Covariance Model (DCM), treating quantum states as macroscopic covariance operators derived from complex-valued stochastic processes. The dynamics are governed by a coarse-grained evolution equation that yields the GKSL form.

**Key observables.** The normalized double covariance operator $C(	au)$ and the resulting density operator $ho(	au)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The scale separation parameter $\epsilon$, which governs the transition from fast microscopic fluctuations ($\delta t = O(\epsilon^2)$) to slow macroscopic observation ($\Delta = O(\epsilon)$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core assumptions include the existence of two separated temporal scales and the assumption of white-noise (delta-correlated) microscopic noise to enforce Markovianity.

**Figures summary.** Not specified.

**Paper structure.** The structure follows a derivation path: defining the microscopic stochastic process, applying multi-scale Itô calculus to find the evolution of the covariance tensor, and finally performing the hydrodynamic coarse-graining to yield the macroscopic GKSL equation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop an interacting extension of the Double Covariance Model (DCM), a stochastic subquantum framework in which macroscopic quantum dynamics emerge through coarse-graining of correlated microscopic fluctuations. Starting from local stochastic differential equations on subsystem Hilbert spaces, we derive a closed evolution equation for a coarse-grained double covariance operator using multi-scale Itô calculus and sliding-window averaging. The construction explicitly incorporates two separated temporal scales: a fast microscopic fluctuation scale governing subquantum stochastic processes and a slower macroscopic observation scale associated with coarse-grained dynamics. Within the hydrodynamic limit, where the ratio between microscopic correlation time and averaging-window scale vanishes, rapidly fluctuating corrections disappear and the effective dynamics converges to a deterministic macroscopic transport equation. We show that the emergent macroscopic dynamics has the exact Gorini-Kossakowski-Sudarshan-Lindblad (GKSL) form: coherent Hamiltonian evolution arises from deterministic subquantum flow, while dissipative channels emerge from quadratic noise correlations. The framework further demonstrates how non-separable interaction Hamiltonians can arise from strictly local, state-dependent stochastic feedback fields. In the fluctuation-free limit, the model reduces naturally to the standard von Neumann equation, providing a unified stochastic foundation for both open and closed quantum dynamics.

</details>

**Summary.** This work provides a comprehensive, foundational examination of quantum mechanics, questioning the physical meaning of probability, the wave function, and Planck's constant. It develops rigorous mathematical axioms and models the measurement process using field theory and decoherence theory. Ultimately, it aims to reconcile the unitary evolution of quantum theory with the definite outcomes observed in the macroscopic world.

**Why it may be interesting.** The deep dive into the measurement problem, decoherence, and superselection rules provides crucial theoretical context for understanding how quantum descriptions must interface with classical, macroscopic reality, which is vital for open quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper tackles the foundational problems of quantum mechanics, specifically the origin of probability, the nature of the wave function, and the measurement problem.

**Main result.** It develops axiomatic frameworks for quantum theory, showing that the mathematical structure is consistent with standard probability theory while highlighting the conceptual gap between unitary evolution and measurement collapse.

**Method.** The approach combines axiomatic formulation (Kolmogorov axioms), mathematical analysis of complex amplitudes, and physical modeling using field theory and oscillator chains.

**Model / system.** The analysis considers quantum mechanical systems ranging from simple oscillators to quantum fields in Minkowski space, and models the measurement process using the transition from amplitudes to probabilities.

**Key observables.** Probability amplitudes ($\psi$), probability densities ($|\psi|^2$), Planck's constant ($h$), and the density matrix ($ho$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Planck distances ($l_P$), Planck's constant ($h$), and the distinction between quantum and classical limits.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The necessity of formulating a rigorous axiomatic basis for QM, and the assumption that superselection rules govern macroscopic reality.

**Figures summary.** Not specified.

**Paper structure.** The structure moves from general foundational questions (probability origin, $h$) to specific mathematical formalisms (characteristic functions, axioms), then to physical models (field quantization, oscillator chains), and finally to resolving the measurement paradox via decoherence and superselection rules.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This book examines a number of problems of quantum mechanics, most of which are not usually discussed. What is the origin of probabilities in the mechanics of the microworld? What is the nature of Planck's constant h? What is the nature of probability amplitudes? What is the wave function? A system of axioms for quantum theory is formulated. A model is studied according to which a classical oscillator in a thermostat can be interpreted as a quantum one. The measurement problem is discussed in detail.   For advanced undergraduate students, graduate students, and specialists interested in the foundations of quantum theory.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates single-photon scattering in waveguide arrays that are either chiral or antichiral. By modeling the system with Dirac equations, the authors show that the inherent chirality breaks reciprocity, leading to unique wave features like a light-cone in the scattering pattern. This work provides a powerful theoretical tool for designing quantum optical devices based on engineered symmetry breaking.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a detailed theoretical framework for understanding how broken or preserved spatial symmetries (chirality) fundamentally alter wave propagation and scattering phenomena in engineered quantum optical structures.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study compares single-photon scattering by atoms in chiral versus antichiral arrays of one-way waveguides, analyzing the resulting wave phenomena.

**Main result.** The chiral array exhibits a unique 'light-cone feature' in its scattering pattern, while the antichiral array's scattering behavior more closely resembles classical wave optics.

**Method.** The analysis uses the Dirac equation framework, transitioning through geometrical optics, diffraction, and scattering regimes by solving associated eikonal and transport equations.

**Model / system.** The system consists of arrays of one-way waveguides containing two-level atoms, modeled by a Hamiltonian $H = H_A + H_F + H_I$. The governing equations are hyperbolic (chiral) or elliptic (antichiral) Dirac equations.

**Key observables.** Scattering cross section ($\sigma_s$), reflection ($r$) and transmission ($t$) coefficients, and the characteristic field patterns.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Group velocities ($v_n$), coupling strength ($J_0$), interaction strength ($g$), and the potential gradients defining the array structure.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the system is in a single-excitation state and relies on taking the continuum limit ($\ell 	o 0$) for the field equations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the comparison between chiral and antichiral arrays, showing light-cone features in scattering probability (Fig. 5) and plotting reflection/transmission coefficients vs. angle or width (Fig. 9).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from deriving the coupled differential equations (Dirac equations) for both array types, to analyzing the geometrical optics limit via eikonal equations, and finally to calculating diffraction and scattering cross sections for point scatterers and slabs.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study single-photon scattering by atoms in arrays of one-way waveguides. We investigate both chiral and antichiral arrays, where the one-way waveguides are aligned in the same and opposite directions, respectively. In the chiral array, reciprocity is broken: one of the (spatial) dimensions is time-like, resulting in a light-cone feature of the scattered fields. In contrast, the antichiral array preserves reciprocity and exhibit scattering behavior typical of wave systems. In analogy with classical physical optics, we exmaine the geometrical optics, diffraction, and scattering regimes in the waveguide arrays. We illustrate our results using numerical simulations.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces QPE averaged over variable grids (QAVG) as a robust technique to overcome noise and low resolution in quantum simulations of electronic spectra. By applying QAVG to a model of CO adsorption on a metal surface, the authors demonstrate that the method accurately reconstructs the system's Density of States using both physical and error-corrected logical quantum circuits. This paves a path for simulating complex correlated materials on early fault-tolerant quantum computers.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the practical limitations of quantum simulation by proposing a noise-resilient algorithm (QAVG) that makes high-resolution spectroscopy feasible on near-term, noisy quantum hardware.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core challenge is accurately extracting the excitation spectra of many-electron systems using Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE), which is limited by low grid resolution and environmental noise on current quantum hardware.

**Main result.** The proposed QPE averaged over variable grids (QAVG) method successfully reconstructs spectra with high accuracy, even when using noisy measurements from physical and logical quantum circuits.

**Method.** The authors developed QAVG, a vernier-type approach combining low-resolution QPE with multiple origin shifts and continuous parametrization to stabilize spectral reconstruction.

**Model / system.** The study applies this method to model the electronic structure of a CO molecule adsorbed onto a $\chi$-Fe$_5$C$_2$ surface, simplified to a two-orbital dimer model for feasibility.

**Key observables.** Hyperacuity spectra, one-particle Green's functions (GFs), and the Density of States (DOS).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The chemical potential shift ($\Delta\mu$) was set to ensure a two-electron ground state; the calculation uses the Steane code for logical qubits.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that averaging over multiple energy origins (QAVG) can suppress spectral leakage and stabilize the optimization process, making the method robust for early fault-tolerant quantum computing.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the unit cell and DOS from DFT, comparisons of DOS from QAVG vs. FCI/noiseless simulations, and cost landscapes demonstrating the suppression of local minima via averaging.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the physical model (CO/$\chi$-Fe$_5$C$_2$) and the need for spectral extraction. It then details the QAVG methodology, implementing it on both physical and logical quantum circuits (Steane code) to calculate the GF and DOS, culminating in a comparison of results against theoretical benchmarks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum phase estimation (QPE) is an underlying technology for extracting the excitation spectra of many-electron systems, yet its practical use on current hardware is hindered by low grid resolution and environmental noises. Here we propose QPE averaged over variable grids (QAVG), a vernier-type approach that combines low-resolution QPE with multiple origin shifts and physically motivated continuous parametrization to reconstruct the spectra accurately. We introduce this approach into an end-to-end workflow for the {\it ab initio}-based model system for a CO molecule adsorbed onto the $χ$-Fe$_5$C$_2$ surface. We perform experiments on Quantinuum H2-2 using both physical QPE circuits and logical QPE circuits encoded in the Steane code with offline bit-flip correction. We demonstrate that QAVG accurately reconstructs the spectra with deviations much smaller than the nominal QPE resolution, even when the noisy histograms are used. The cost landscapes averaged over the shifted grids substantially suppress the local minima arising from the spectral leakage, thereby stabilizing the optimization of trial parameters. These results indicate that QAVG provides a robust route to quantum simulations of correlated spectra toward the era of early-fault-tolerant quantum computers.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Estimates of ground state energies for the quantum SK and 2D-EA model, using deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field annealing dynamics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29875v1)

**Authors:** Soumyaditya Das, Soumyajyoti Biswas, Bikas K. Chakrabarti  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29875v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `exotic spin models in light-matter` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5**

**Summary.** This work develops and applies the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field annealing dynamics to calculate the ground state energies of quantum spin glass models, namely the SK and EA models. By simulating quantum annealing on large systems, the authors achieve high-precision estimates for the ground state energy, demonstrating the method's efficiency and accuracy for disordered quantum systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The use of mean-field dynamics to study quantum phase transitions and ground state properties in disordered systems is highly relevant for understanding complex quantum many-body physics, which shares conceptual links with quantum optimization problems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to estimate the ground state energies for quantum spin glass models, specifically the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick (SK) and 2D-Edwards-Anderson (EA) models.

**Main result.** The developed method successfully estimates the ground state energy for the SK model to high precision ($E_0/N \approx -0.7623$), and extends this framework to the EA model.

**Method.** The estimation relies on simulating quantum annealing dynamics using the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field approach.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on quantum spin glass Hamiltonians, including the mean-field SK model and the nearest-neighbor EA model, both subjected to a transverse magnetic field ($\Gamma$).

**Key observables.** Ground state energy ($E_0$), finite size scaling exponents ($N^{-2/3}$ for SK, $L^{-8/5}$ for EA), and energy fluctuations ($\sigma_N, \sigma_L$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size ($N$ or $L$), transverse field ($\Gamma$), and the annealing schedule.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is the applicability of the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field approximation, which is most rigorously applied to the mean-field SK model.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the energy scaling for SK and EA models, showing convergence to the infinite limit, and also depict the computational cost scaling ($L^4$ or $N^2$) and the saturation of the total ground state energy with annealing time.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of ground state energy estimation in quantum spin glasses, details the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field annealing dynamics, applies this method to the SK model with large simulations, and finally extends the methodology to the 2D EA model.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We perform a large scale simulation of quantum annealing in the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick (SK) spin glass up to a system size $N=40000$ to estimate its ground state energy using the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field Ising dynamics, extending the earlier results (reported in Eur. Phys. J. B {\bf 98}, 226 (2025)). Here we numerically solve the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo annealing dynamics to obtain the spin configurations and subsequently the ground state energy for a given system size at the end of the annealing (to the desired quantum system at the corresponding values of the transverse field), starting from a quantum paramagnetic state. The method shows high efficiency, with an overall algorithmic cost of $O(N^3)$ in estimating the energy of the ground state. We later extend this method to study the ground state energy of the Edwards-Anderson (EA) spin glass on a square lattice.

</details>

### [Toward Practical Two-Way Covert Communication](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29840v1)

**Summary.** This paper tackles the practical implementation of two-way covert quantum communication, aiming to send secret messages without alerting a quantum eavesdropper. The authors overcome the experimental hurdle of mode-matching by proposing a correlator-based receiver using squeezed vacuum states. They provide both theoretical bounds and experimental demonstrations showing how to maximize secure information transfer rates.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems, as it deals with information security and channel capacity limits in the presence of eavesdropping, requiring deep knowledge of quantum channel characterization and error bounding.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of designing practical two-way covert communication schemes that allow information transfer while remaining undetected by a quantum-capable adversary.

**Main result.** The authors propose and analyze a correlator-based receiver using quantum two-mode squeezed vacuum states, which achieves broadband communication gains without the need for difficult mode-matching, significantly outperforming other analyzed schemes.

**Method.** The work combines theoretical analysis using quantum relative entropy (QRE) bounds for covertness with experimental demonstrations using narrowband lasers and theoretical derivations involving Taylor expansions of quantum operators.

**Model / system.** The system is modeled using quantum bosonic channels, involving Alice transmitting to Bob, who then modulates and reflects the signal back to Alice. The analysis considers both broadband (ideal) and narrowband (practical) optical setups.

**Key observables.** Covert throughput, Quantum Relative Entropy (QRE), Shannon capacity, and the probability of successful range determination ($p_s$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Channel transmittance ($\eta_i$), thermal-noise mean photon number ($ar{n}_{Bi}$), and the tolerance to detection ($\delta_{QRE}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The adversary is assumed to be quantum-capable, and the authors often assume lossless channels or specific approximations for the receiver performance.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the communication model, experimental setups (using EDFA/coherent sources), and comparative plots showing covert capacity lower bounds and achievable throughput across different schemes.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical limits of covert communication using quantum channels, demonstrating a proof-of-concept with practical narrowband sources, and finally comparing the performance of several advanced receiver schemes, culminating in detailed mathematical derivations of error bounds.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study two-way covert communication schemes, where information is transmitted by passively modulating a reflected signal back to the source. We consider optical systems, described by quantum bosonic channels. While broadband classical and quantum light sources offer high covert throughput in theory, the associated mode-matching and phase-synchronization requirements make them impractical. Therefore, we employ a narrowband laser source to experimentally demonstrate a proof-of-concept two-way covert communication system, where the adversary is assumed to be quantum-capable. Furthermore, we propose a correlator-based receiver that attains the broadband gain offered by a quantum light source without the need for precise mode matching.

</details>

**Summary.** This work introduces a machine-learning framework to overcome the ambiguity in using the Kinetic Isotope Effect (KIE) to measure quantum tunneling. By training ensemble models on DFT-derived reaction data, the authors successfully decouple the true tunneling factor ($\kappa$) from classical kinetic contributions. The resulting phase diagram provides a quantitative map for assessing quantum tunneling across various temperature regimes.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on chemical kinetics, the core methodology—using ML to decouple complex, non-linear physical parameters ($\kappa$) from measurable proxies (KIE)—is highly relevant to modeling open quantum systems where multiple coupled effects obscure the true quantum dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The Kinetic Isotope Effect (KIE) is an insufficient probe for quantifying quantum tunneling because it conflates tunneling effects with zero-point energy and classical kinetics.

**Main result.** The developed machine-learning framework successfully decodes the nonlinear relationship between KIE and the tunneling factor ($\kappa$), revealing a complex 'tunneling phase diagram' that defines quantitative assessment regimes.

**Method.** The study employs ensemble machine learning (e.g., XGBoost) to map the multi-dimensional relationship between KIE, temperature, and other descriptors to predict the intrinsic tunneling factor ($\kappa$).

**Model / system.** The research models chemical reaction dynamics, specifically concerted proton transfer reactions involving amino acids, using DFT-derived Potential Energy Surfaces (PESs) to calculate quantum and classical rate constants.

**Key observables.** KIE, $\kappa$ (tunneling factor), quantum rate constant ($k_{	ext{Tun}}$), classical rate constant ($k_{	ext{Cla}}$), and temperature ($T$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The anomalous high KIE–low $\kappa$ spanning 300–600 K defines a critical regime for quantum tunneling assessment.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis treats isolated molecules in the gas phase, simplifying the complex condensed-phase environment.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the conceptual workflow from KIE paradox to ML benchmarking, and the final phase diagram mapping KIE vs. $\kappa$ across different temperature regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the physical problem (KIE ambiguity), details the computational methods (DFT, VTST, ML), presents the ML model training and validation (using various algorithms), and culminates in the interpretation of the resulting multidimensional tunneling phase diagram.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The kinetic isotope effect (KIE) is the conventional probe for quantum tunneling, yet its composite nature conflates tunneling with zero-point energy and classical kinetics. Here, we introduce the tunneling phase diagram, a machine-learning framework that decouples true tunneling strength by decoding the nonlinear relationship between KIE and the tunneling factor (\k{appa}). With exceptional fidelity (R^2 > 0.98, RMSE = 0.21), this framework reveals an anomalous high KIE-low \k{appa} spanning 300-600 K, thereby defining a paradigm for the quantitative assessment of quantum tunneling.

</details>

**Authors:** Tejinder P. Singh

Three matching hypotheses (red) enter the linear master equation and benchmark rate: the system–bath coupling gint, the spatial kernel reff C , and the...</sub>

**Summary.** This paper proposes using Generalized Trace Dynamics (GTD) to derive a microscopic source for the noise field responsible for objective quantum collapse. It calculates a narrow-band spectral correlator at twice the Hubble frequency, which is structurally distinct from current phenomenological bounds. The derivation, however, relies on several non-trivial structural postulates, leaving the final objective-collapse theory incomplete.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work tackles the fundamental problem of objective collapse by providing a structural, field-theoretic candidate for the noise source, offering a potential link between cosmology (Hubble scale) and quantum measurement theory.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To derive a microscopic, parameter-free source for the noise field associated with objective collapse models (like CSL) using Generalized Trace Dynamics (GTD).

**Main result.** The framework predicts a narrow-band spectral line at twice the Hubble scale ($2H_0$), which is structurally distinct from the white-noise spectra predicted by standard CSL bounds.

**Method.** The authors compute the two-point function of a candidate collapse-noise operator using auxiliary canonical fermionic Fock-space quantization within the GTD framework.

**Model / system.** The model is based on Generalized Trace Dynamics (GTD), which attempts to derive quantum mechanics from a trace structure involving Grassmann algebras and fermionic sectors. The calculation involves coupling a system to a bath via a bilinear operator $J$.

**Key observables.** The two-point correlation function $\langle 0|J(	au)J(0)|0angle$, which predicts a spectral line at $|\omega| = 2\omega_0$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hubble scale ($\omega_0 \sim H_0$), the amplitude $A_J$, and the structural postulates required for the calculation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation relies heavily on several structural postulates, including replacing a nilpotent coefficient with an auxiliary scalar parameter and assuming positive-norm canonical quantization for the fermionic sector.

**Figures summary.** Not specified.

**Paper structure.** The paper proceeds by defining the GTD structure, identifying the noise operator $J$, performing the two-point function calculation in the auxiliary Fock space, and then discussing the necessary structural postulates and open problems required to achieve a parameter-free derivation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a conditional construction of candidate CSL-type collapse-noise correlators inspired by Generalized Trace Dynamics (GTD). The construction is not a parameter-free derivation from the minimal GTD Grassmann algebra. It rests on a chain of explicit structural postulates, listed in Section 1; within that auxiliary structure the spectral form and amplitude follow by computation rather than by phenomenological fitting. The resulting narrow-band spectrum at the Hubble scale lies outside the bands of current CSL bounds, so the framework is not in tension with existing high-frequency data.   We compute the two-point function of a candidate collapse-noise operator associated with the GTD aikyon decomposition $q_i = q_B + a_0β_i q_F$. In the minimal Grassmann algebra, $q_F$ appears only multiplied by Grassmann generators $β_i$, the reduction of $\mathrm{Tr}(q_F^\daggerΓ^μq_F)$ to ghost-mode operators is obstructed by the nilpotent $δβ= β_2 - β_1$, and the pure-fermion coefficient $β_1β_2$ has no ordinary sign, modulus, or inverse. We therefore introduce an auxiliary canonical fermionic Fock-space sector for $q_F$, equivalently replacing the nilpotent pure-fermion coefficient by an ordinary effective scalar body parameter. This replacement is an independent structural postulate, not a consequence of the original minimal action.   Under this auxiliary postulate, together with a scalar bilinear $J = \mathrm{Tr}(q_F^\dagger q_F)$ as bath operator, positive-norm canonical quantization, and an effective sign choice $σ= \pm1$ for the scalarized pure-fermion sector, elementary Wick contraction gives a Wightman line at $|ω| = 2ω_0$ with amplitude $A_J = (\hbar/2m_Rω_0 L_{\mathrm{aik}}^2)^2\cdot N\cdot D$. The cosmological identification $ω_0 \sim H_0$ places the line at twice the Hubble scale. [truncated]

</details>

### [Effective Theory of Fermion Quartet Condensation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29338v1)

**Authors:** Qiao-Ru Xu, Congjun Wu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29338v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29338_figures/2605.29338_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Schematic plots of two different scenarios for the fermion quartet formation in spin- 1</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops an effective theory for superconductivity in dilute spin-1/2 Fermi systems based on the condensation of fermion quartets. By constructing a variational wavefunction and deriving a gap equation, the authors calculate key properties like excitation energies and the superfluid fraction. The results show that despite the complex quartet interaction, the superfluid behavior mirrors that of conventional superconductors.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides a theoretical framework for exotic pairing mechanisms (quartet condensation) in a gas-like setting, drawing parallels to established BCS theory while exploring higher-order correlations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing an effective theory for superconductivity or superfluidity arising from the condensation of fermion quartets.

**Main result.** The theory successfully derives a gap equation and predicts that the superfluid fraction remains the same as in conventional BCS superconductors.

**Method.** The authors construct a variational wavefunction and minimize the total energy using an effective quartic Hamiltonian, analogous to BCS theory.

**Model / system.** The system under study is a dilute spin-1/2 Fermi gas at zero temperature, modeled by an effective Hamiltonian involving quartet creation/annihilation operators.

**Key observables.** Superfluid fraction ($f_s$), single-particle excitation energy ($\omega_k$), and three-particle excitation energy ($\omega_{kk'q}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The gap parameter ($\Delta_{kk'q}$) characterizes the quartet condensation; the analysis is restricted to the dilute quartet approximation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary approximation used is the Dilute Quartet Approximation (DQA), which assumes commutators of quartet operators are negligible for widely separated brackets.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 schematically illustrates two scenarios for fermion quartet formation: binding of two tightly bound Cooper pairs and binding of two loosely bound Cooper pairs on the Fermi surface.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the effective Hamiltonian, derives the gap equation by minimizing the energy, and then calculates key observables like excitation energies and the superfluid fraction using linear response theory.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a theory of superconductivity (or superfluidity) based on condensed fermion quartets focusing on the dilute spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ systems at zero temperature. In the spirit of the Bardeen--Cooper--Schrieffer ansatz, a variational wavefunction is constructed such that, within the so-called ``dilute quartet approximation", it is the ground state of an effective quartic Hamiltonian. For a given two-body interaction in favor of quartetting, the gap parameter is suitably defined and the gap equation is also derived. As to the excited states, an intuitive physical picture based on a sixteen-dimensional ``occupation space" is depicted and the associated eigen-energies are obtained. This theory is applied to compute the superfluid fraction, which is found to be the same as in conventional superconductors, despite the interacting nature of the quartet problem.

</details>

### [Exponentially Fast Solution State Preparation for the Heat Equation and its use for Option Pricing](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28950v1)

**Authors:** Gumaro Rendon, Stepan Smid, Sarvagya Upadhyay  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28950v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

**Summary.** This work applies advanced quantum computing techniques to the problem of pricing complex financial derivatives. It achieves this by mathematically transforming the governing partial differential equations into a solvable, diagonal heat equation. The resulting quantum algorithm promises an exponential advantage in computational resources over current classical methods for these high-dimensional problems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The core technique of reducing a complex, high-dimensional PDE to a simple, solvable heat equation structure is mathematically analogous to finding effective Hamiltonians or simplified dynamics in many-body quantum systems, making the quantum simulation aspect highly relevant.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing quantum algorithms to achieve exponential speedups in solving complex Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) that arise from pricing financial derivatives.

**Main result.** The authors propose methods to solve diffusion-type PDEs, like the heat equation, using quantum techniques that achieve superior scaling compared to classical Monte Carlo or direct PDE solvers.

**Method.** The approach involves transforming the pricing PDE into a time-dependent, diagonal heat equation, which is then solved quantum mechanically via Hamiltonian simulation techniques exploiting fast-forwardable structure.

**Model / system.** The underlying model is the pricing of financial derivatives (e.g., swaptions, cross-currency swaptions) whose value functions satisfy parabolic PDEs derived from stochastic differential equations (SDEs) under risk-neutral measures.

**Key observables.** The option price itself, the solution surface of the PDE, and the required qubit count for computation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The time horizon $T$, the required precision $\epsilon$, and the dimensionality of the underlying asset space.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumptions include the ability to reduce the complex PDE to a heat-type equation, the piecewise-constant nature of volatilities, and the theoretical feasibility of extracting the full solution surface without negating the quantum speedup.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the financial modeling (SDEs $ightarrow$ PDEs via Feynman-Kac) to the mathematical reduction (transforming the PDE into a diagonal heat equation) and finally detailing the quantum algorithmic implementation for solving this reduced form.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we present the methods necessary to price an important set of derivatives on a quantum device while offering an advantage over existing classical methods. The methods developed here, in conjunction with ~\cite{GumaroS2026}, also provide an exponential advantage in requirement of qubits when pricing some option contracts with path-dependent payoff compared to state-of-the-art quantum Monte Carlo methods.

</details>

**Authors:** Francisco Divi, Leonardo A. Lessa, Chong Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28967v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

**Summary.** This work introduces a local fidelity correlator as a robust order parameter for Strong-to-Weak Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking (SW-SSB). This local approach overcomes the computational and mathematical difficulties of global measures, allowing for its application in infinite systems and complex models like free-fermion metals. It establishes that this local measure is stable and provides universal scaling laws for various critical phases.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful, scalable diagnostic tool for characterizing symmetry breaking in complex, large-scale quantum systems, bridging concepts from quantum information theory (fidelity) to condensed matter physics (SSB).

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper proposes a local definition for Strong-to-Weak Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking (SW-SSB) to overcome the limitations of previous definitions, especially in the thermodynamic limit.

**Main result.** The local one-point fidelity correlator provides a robust, scalable, and well-defined order parameter for SW-SSB, which remains valid even when the global density matrix is ill-defined.

**Method.** The authors utilize the one-point fidelity correlator, $F(ho_A; O_x)$, which measures the local deviation from symmetry, and establish its existence and uniqueness using tools like the Data Processing Inequality.

**Model / system.** The analysis applies to general quantum systems, including pure states satisfying the Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH), free-fermion metals, and thermal paramagnets exhibiting various symmetry breaking patterns.

**Key observables.** Local one-point fidelity correlator ($F(ho_A; O_x)$), Conditional Mutual Information (CMI), and the scaling behavior of the fidelity correlator.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size ($N$), region size ($|A|$), temperature ($eta$), and the correlation length scale ($\xi_M$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation relies on the fidelity measure satisfying the Data Processing Inequality (DPI) for the local order parameter to be well-defined in the infinite limit.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the local fidelity correlator, the tripartition used for CMI calculation, and the scaling behavior of the correlator in various critical systems (e.g., CFTs, free-fermion metals).

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the local definition by comparing it to global measures, proving its robustness (stability under local channels), establishing its existence in the thermodynamic limit, and finally applying it to calculate universal scaling laws in specific physical models.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a local notion of strong-to-weak spontaneous symmetry breaking (SW-SSB), through a local one-point fidelity correlator. Compared with the previous definition in terms of a two-point fidelity correlator, our local formulation offers two key advantages: (1) it is easier to detect in large systems: for a system of size $N$ and with ${\rm poly}(N)$ amount of resources, one can detect the local fidelity order up to volume scale $O(\log(N))$; and (2) the local SW-SSB order remains well defined in the thermodynamic limit, where the density matrix itself is not well defined. We show that key features of SW-SSB, including stability under finite-depth symmetric channels and long-range conditional mutual information, persist within this local framework. Our definition is conceptually analogous to local thermalization, as exemplified by pure states obeying the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis (ETH). For critical states, the local one-point fidelity correlator defines an interesting class of defect problems. We demonstrate the applicability of the local formulation through several concrete examples, and derive the universal scaling behavior of the local fidelity correlator in a range of critical systems, including ground states of conformal field theories as well as ballistic and diffusive free-fermion metals.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper rigorously compares the Permutation Matrix Representation (PMR) method against leading quantum simulation algorithms like Qubitization and qHOP. By analyzing resource costs for both time-independent and time-dependent Hamiltonians, the authors demonstrate that PMR offers favorable scaling, suggesting it is a practical alternative for near-term quantum hardware implementation.

**Why it may be interesting.** The direct comparison of scaling laws ($O(N^2 \log N)$ vs. higher polynomial scaling) provides concrete guidance on when PMR might outperform established QSP-based methods for resource-constrained quantum hardware.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper conducts a comparative resource analysis of various quantum algorithms designed for simulating the time evolution governed by complex Hamiltonians.

**Main result.** The Permutation Matrix Representation (PMR) method is shown to offer complementary advantages in resource scaling compared to established methods like Qubitization and qHOP, particularly regarding its dependence on system parameters.

**Method.** The authors compare PMR against QSP and qHOP by analyzing the gate and qubit resource costs for both time-independent (Rydberg model) and time-dependent (Floquet-driven Ising model) Hamiltonians.

**Model / system.** The analysis covers two main physical models: the Rydberg interaction Hamiltonian for time-independent simulation, and the Floquet-driven transverse field Ising model for time-dependent simulation in arbitrary dimensions.

**Key observables.** Resource costs, including gate complexity and ancilla qubit requirements, are the primary observables.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size (N), interaction strengths ($\Omega, \delta, C_6$), driving frequency ($\omega$), and simulation time ($T$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on truncating series expansions (e.g., in PMR) and making specific approximations for error bounding in the time-dependent regime.

**Figures summary.** The notes reference Table I and Table II, which provide direct comparisons of gate and qubit costs between PMR, Qubitization, and qHOP for the respective simulation regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper is structured around benchmarking PMR against state-of-the-art techniques. It first analyzes the time-independent case using the Rydberg model, then extends the comparison to the time-dependent Floquet-driven Ising model, concluding with a detailed resource comparison.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a comparative study of the permutation matrix representation (PMR) method for Hamiltonian simulation alongside other leading quantum algorithms. Our analysis focuses on resource costs for simulating both time-independent and time-dependent Hamiltonians. For the time-independent case, we benchmark PMR against quantum signal processing (QSP) and qubitization, using the Rydberg interaction Hamiltonian as a representative example. For the time-dependent case, we compare the time-dependent extension of PMR with the quantum highly oscillatory protocol (qHOP), applied to a Floquet-driven transverse field Ising model in arbitrary spatial dimensions. In both regimes, we find that PMR offers complementary advantages in resource requirements and exhibits favorable scaling with certain system parameters, suggesting that it may provide practical benefits on resource-constrained quantum hardware.

</details>

**Summary.** This work proposes a quantum protocol to identify prime numbers by analyzing the Fourier spectrum of entanglement dynamics in a bipartite quantum system. To make this feasible on current noisy hardware, the authors introduce and validate a rescaling-based error mitigation technique called CFE. The success of the method demonstrates a pathway for applying number theory directly to NISQ quantum processors.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a concrete, albeit highly complex, example of mapping fundamental number theory problems onto quantum hardware diagnostics, which is a key area for quantum algorithm development.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of performing number-theoretic computations, specifically prime number identification, using noisy quantum processors.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that the Fourier components extracted from the time evolution of entanglement purity can serve as a robust signature for primality, even when enhanced by a novel noise mitigation technique.

**Method.** The core methodology involves linking primality to the minimal values of Fourier modes derived from the purity ($\gamma_A(t)$) of a bipartite quantum system, while employing Correction Factor Extrapolation (CFE) to correct for hardware noise.

**Model / system.** The system is a bipartite quantum system AB evolving under a time-independent Hamiltonian. The protocol uses the SWAP test to measure entanglement purity, $\gamma_A(t)$, whose Fourier components are analyzed for number-theoretic properties.

**Key observables.** Purity of subsystem A ($\gamma_A(t)$), Fourier modes ($\alpha_n$), and the optimized correction factor ($\Lambda_{opt}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** System dimensions ($d=4, 8, 16$), time evolution parameters ($\omega=0.1 	ext{s}^{-1}$), and noise scaling factors ($\xi_j$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theoretical bounds for primality are derived assuming an initial uniform superposition state, and the CFE method requires calibration on a specific subset of circuits.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the CFE procedure, showing raw vs. mitigated purity and Fourier modes; they also plot the extrapolation of the optimal correction factor $\Lambda_{opt}(d)$ across different dimensions.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the theoretical link between entanglement dynamics and primality, introduces the CFE error mitigation technique, validates this technique using numerical simulations on IBM hardware, and finally presents the refined theoretical bounds for composite numbers.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We implement a quantum protocol for prime number identification based on entanglement dynamics, using IBM quantum processors. The method links the primality of an integer to specific Fourier components extracted from the time evolution of entanglement in a bipartite quantum system. To mitigate experimental noise, we introduce a noise-mitigation method based on a global rescaling factor, which is calibrated on a subset of circuits and extrapolated across different configurations. Theoretical support is provided by a new analytical bound for the Fourier modes derived assuming an initial uniform superposition state. This new bound enhances the separation between prime and composite numbers under moderate experimental deviations. These results represent a step toward practical number-theoretic applications on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices.

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical work analyzes the metal-insulator transition in an altermagnetic Hubbard model on a Lieb lattice using the slave-rotor approach. It predicts a sequence of four phases as the onsite repulsion U increases, characterized by changes in the quasiparticle weight and the opening of a Mott gap. The findings suggest that observable spin splitting in the electronic spectrum is limited to the weak-to-moderate correlation regime.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed theoretical mapping of a complex correlated system (Hubbard model) onto a solvable bosonic/fermionic framework (slave-rotor) provides a powerful tool for understanding exotic magnetic and electronic states in strongly correlated materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the metal-insulator transition driven by onsite repulsion in an altermagnetic Hubbard model on a Lieb lattice.

**Main result.** The system undergoes a sequence of phase transitions (NM $	o$ A$\ell$M $	o$ A$\ell$I $	o$ A$\ell$MI) as interaction $U$ increases, with the Mott insulator phase showing suppressed spin splitting.

**Method.** The analysis employs the slave-rotor approach, mapping the Hubbard model onto an effective Lagrangian and solving self-consistent equations for order parameters.

**Model / system.** The system is an altermagnetic Hubbard model defined on a Lieb lattice, studied at half-filling. The Hamiltonian includes nearest-neighbor and next-nearest-neighbor hopping terms, along with the onsite repulsive interaction U.

**Key observables.** Electronic spectral function (showing spin-split bands), quasiparticle weight (Z), staggered magnetization (M), and the Mott gap ($\Delta_X$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The onsite repulsive interaction U, which drives the phase transitions; the hopping parameters $t_1, t_{2a}, t_{2b}$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the slave-rotor theory and the saddle-point approximation for solving the resulting path integral/action.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the phase diagram showing the four distinct phases as U increases, and the spectral function $A_k(\omega)$ confirms spin splitting in metallic/insulating regimes but its suppression in the Mott phase.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically derives the self-consistent equations for order parameters ($Q$ and $M$) by mapping the Hubbard model onto a slave-rotor formulation, calculating the spectral function, and mapping out the phase diagram based on the behavior of $Z$ and $\Delta_X$.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the metal-insulator transition driven by the onsite repulsive interaction $U$ in an altermagnetic Hubbard model defined on a Lieb lattice. Using the slave-rotor approach at half filling, we find that the system exhibits a cascade of interaction-driven phase transitions. As $U$ increases, the system evolves from a normal metal to an altermagnetic metal, then to an altermagnetic insulator, and eventually to an altermagnetic Mott insulator characterized by the complete suppression of the quasiparticle weight. These phases are supported by the calculation of the electronic spectral function, which features spin-split bands in both the metallic and insulating regimes. However, the spin splitting becomes substantially suppressed in the Mott insulating phase. Our results suggest that the observation of spin splitting in the spectral function of $d$-wave altermagnets with a Lieb-lattice-like structure may be limited to the weak-to-moderate correlation regime.

</details>

**Summary.** This work systematically analyzes the concept of integrability in anyonic chains, which are quantum models built upon the abstract structure of fusion categories. By utilizing the constrained boost operator formalism, the authors classify numerous new integrable models, providing a powerful mathematical framework for studying constrained quantum systems beyond simple spin chains.

**Why it may be interesting.** The deep connection between topological order (anyons/fusion categories) and exactly solvable quantum models (integrability) provides a powerful theoretical tool for understanding critical phenomena in condensed matter systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper reviews and classifies Yang-Baxter integrability for spin chains whose Hilbert spaces are constrained by mathematical structures, specifically focusing on anyonic chains.

**Main result.** The authors classify new integrable anyonic chains for various fusion categories, including those based on $\mathfrak{su}(2)_k$, Tambara-Yamagami categories, and product categories.

**Method.** The study employs the constrained boost operator formalism and relies heavily on the mathematical framework of fusion categories, which requires solving the pentagon equations for consistency.

**Model / system.** The primary model is the anyonic chain, which generalizes spin chains by using fusion rules from a fusion category $C$ to define local projection operators $P_a^{b,i}$ that form the Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Integrability, central charge ($c$), entanglement entropy, and the existence of Temperley-Lieb algebra representations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The rank of the fusion category (up to 7), the specific fusion category (e.g., $	ext{Fib} 	imes 	ext{Fib}$), and the coupling constants ($\alpha_i, eta_i$) in the Hamiltonian.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is assumed to be periodic, and the mathematical structure is assumed to be governed by a consistent fusion category satisfying the pentagon equations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate fusion diagrams, the measurement process for three particles, and plots showing entanglement entropy and gap scaling with chain length.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first defining the mathematical machinery (fusion rings, fusion categories, pentagon equations), then introducing the Hamiltonian via local projection operators, and finally applying the constrained boost formalism to classify and derive specific integrable models for various known fusion categories.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We review the notion of Yang-Baxter integrability for spin chains that have Hilbert spaces with constraints, such as a Rydberg blockade. We focus on anyonic chains, whose constraints arise from the fusion rules of the fusion categories on which they are based. We discuss the emergence of Temperley-Lieb algebras and present a new result on which types of anyonic chains exhibit them. We then give an overview of known results for integrable anyonic chains and extend them to several fusion categories up to rank $7$. Using a modification of the boost operator formalism, we find several new integrable anyonic chains and discuss some of their properties. These include spin-$\frac32$ models for $\mathfrak{su}(2)_k$ fusion categories, anyonic chains based on the Tambara-Yamagami fusion categories TY$(\mathbb{Z}_n)$, and product fusion categories Fib$\times$Fib and Fib$\times$Ising. We review recent results for spin chains based on the Haagerup-Izumi fusion category HI$(\mathbb{Z}_3)$, and present preliminary numerics for a HI$(\mathbb{Z}_5)$ model.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper theoretically explains Chirality-Induced Spin Selectivity (CISS) in helical organic molecules by modeling electron-electron interactions. It demonstrates that strong, non-collinear spin correlations, rather than relying solely on spin-orbit coupling or long-range magnetic order, are the primary drivers of spin polarization. The research utilizes advanced numerical methods like DMRG and CPT to validate this mechanism.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides a detailed theoretical framework for understanding spin-selective transport in molecular systems, linking correlation physics (Hubbard/Kondo models) directly to a key phenomenon in spintronics and molecular electronics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses Chirality-Induced Spin Selectivity (CISS)—the spin polarization of currents through chiral media—by investigating the role of electron-electron interactions.

**Main result.** Non-collinear spin correlations driven by Coulomb interactions are proposed as the mechanism for CISS, showing that strong correlations, rather than long-range magnetic order or large spin-orbit coupling, are sufficient to induce spin selectivity.

**Method.** The study employs advanced theoretical techniques including Density-Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG), Cluster Perturbation Theory (CPT), and Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) simulations.

**Model / system.** The model focuses on low-dimensional helical organic molecules, utilizing an extended two-orbital model incorporating on-site Coulomb ($U$) and Hund's-rule ($J_H$) interactions, leading to an effective Kondo-lattice model.

**Key observables.** Spin polarization in transmitted electrons, one-particle spectral functions ($N(\omega)$ and $N(k, \omega)$), and the spin-structure factor $S(\vec{k})$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The mechanism is shown to be robust even when the spin-orbit coupling (SOC) is vanishingly small, provided strong correlations are present.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the Kondo-lattice model derived from considering the orbital-selective Mott phase transition, and CPT is noted to only include short-range correlations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate DMRG results showing spin-polarized density-of-states and momentum-resolved spectral density, and classical spin simulations showing spin-structure factors peaking at incommensurate wavevectors.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the model by incorporating interactions into a two-orbital framework, applies various numerical methods (DMRG, CPT, MCMC) to calculate spectral properties, and concludes by proposing the role of non-collinear spin correlations in explaining CISS.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We explicitly account for electron-electron interactions when modeling low-dimensional helical organic molecules. We show that competition between various hopping channels, together with interaction-induced double- and superexchange mechanisms, can stabilize non-collinear helical magnetic order. The resulting single-electron bands exhibit partial spin polarization, a manifestation of $p$-wave magnetism. Using density-matrix renormalization group, cluster perturbation theory, and Monte Carlo methods, we find that even vanishingly small spin-orbit coupling triggers strong spin selectivity at temperatures significantly above the spin-orbit scale. While strong correlations are essential for this mechanism, long-range spin ordering is not required. We thus propose non-collinear spin correlations driven by Coulomb interactions as an explanation of chirality-induced spin selectivity and discuss connections to experiments.

</details>
