</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops a quantum algorithm framework to calculate Green-Kubo transport coefficients from classical molecular dynamics. It uses the Koopman-von Neumann representation to map classical dynamics onto unitary quantum evolutions, which are then simulated using Quantum Phase Estimation. The results demonstrate improved statistical scaling and provide resource estimates for implementing these complex simulations on quantum hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, quantum-algorithm-based pathway to calculate time-dependent, non-equilibrium properties (transport coefficients) that are central to understanding complex condensed matter systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper formulates the calculation of Green-Kubo transport coefficients from classical molecular dynamics as a readout problem solvable by quantum algorithms using the Koopman-von Neumann (KvN) representation.

**Main result.** The framework successfully maps classical dynamics (NVE/NVT) to unitary quantum evolutions, and the transport coefficients are read out via Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) with improved statistical scaling.

**Method.** The core method involves linearizing classical Liouville evolution into a unitary quantum evolution using the KvN representation, followed by estimating the required correlation function via QPE and Maximum-Likelihood Amplitude Estimation (MLAE).

**Model / system.** The model is classical molecular dynamics, encompassing NVE (Hamiltonian) and Nosé-Hoover NVT dynamics, applied to systems like those found in lithium-ion batteries.

**Key observables.** Green-Kubo transport coefficients (e.g., diffusion coefficient), the probability $P_0$ of the QPE ancilla register being zero, and the time-dependent velocity autocorrelation function (VACF).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The required qubit resources scale polynomially for NVE ($O(n^2)$ CX gates) but exhibit exponential scaling for NVT ($O(n_\xi n_p 2^{n_p})$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes the existence of a Hermitian KvN generator for both NVE and NVT flows, and the statistical estimation relies on MLAE improving scaling from $N^{-1/2}$ to $N^{-1}$.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the conceptual workflow (MD $	o$ KvN-MD $	o$ QPE), show quantum circuits for NVE and NVT propagators, and present convergence studies comparing KvN results against classical MD references.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the KvN formalism for NVE and NVT dynamics, details the quantum circuit implementation for the propagators, maps the Green-Kubo integral to a QPE readout, and finally analyzes the resource scaling and convergence of the resulting estimator.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We formulate the Green--Kubo transport coefficients of classical molecular dynamics as a readout problem for quantum algorithms using the Koopman--von Neumann (KvN) representation. Both NVE and Nosé--Hoover-type NVT dynamics are derived as unitary evolutions on Hilbert spaces associated with the corresponding classical phase spaces. Numerical benchmarks on finite grids show that the discretization error in the correlation function decreases as a power law in the number of grid points $N_z$. Equivalently, with $N_z=2^{n_z}$, the error decreases exponentially in the register size $n_z$, so a target accuracy $ε$ requires $n_z=\mathcal{O}(\log(1/ε))$ qubits. To read out a transport coefficient, we input a flux-excited state to quantum phase estimation (QPE). The probability $P_0$ of measuring the QPE ancilla register in the all-zero state corresponds to a Bartlett-windowed Green--Kubo integral. With maximum-likelihood amplitude estimation, the statistical estimation of $P_0$ defined by this QPE oracle improves from the $N_{\rm queries}^{-1/2}$ scaling of direct shot sampling to scaling close to $N_{\rm queries}^{-1}$. Our circuit-resource analysis shows that one step of the NVE propagator can be built with $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ CX gates, where $n=n_x+n_p$ is the total number of position and momentum qubits. For the NVT propagator, the centered-difference Pauli-decomposition implementation of the Nosé--Hoover friction term scales as $\mathcal{O}(n_ξn_p\,2^{n_p})$, where $n_p$ and $n_ξ$ are the numbers of momentum and thermostat qubits, respectively. The proposed framework is a concrete step toward translating the principles of quantum algorithms into the transport-coefficient calculations required in practical molecular simulation.

</details>

**Authors:** Ben Harper, Azar C. Nakhl, Martin Sevior, Muhammad Usman  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29514v1>

The ancilla is prepared in the |0⟩state and measures Z1Z2Z3Z4 or X1X2X3X4 on the four adjacent data qubits di. Vertical dashed lines indicate when crosstalk noise occurs after the entangling CNOT gates in each syndrome extraction circuit.</sub>

The vertical lines indicate the threshold for each type of noise.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.29514_figures/2605.29514_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 6. Logical error rates under each noise model for d = 9. The inclusion of coherent crosstalk noise increases logical error rates over the baseline Pauli noise model, while coherent crosstalk noise in random directions somewhat reduces logical error rates again.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper simulates quantum error correction on surface codes using advanced tensor network methods to account for coherent crosstalk noise. It demonstrates that this coherence significantly degrades performance, increasing logical error rates and lowering the error threshold compared to simpler noise assumptions. The work provides a vital tool for accurately characterizing hardware limitations in scalable quantum computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides a rigorous, scalable simulation framework for incorporating non-Markovian and coherent noise sources into QEC, which is crucial for designing fault-tolerant quantum hardware based on superconducting qubits or similar platforms.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of simulating quantum error correction (QEC) under realistic, coherent quantum crosstalk noise, which is beyond the scope of simpler noise models or classical simulations.

**Main result.** The inclusion of coherent crosstalk noise significantly increases logical error rates and lowers the code threshold compared to idealized models, demonstrating that standard approximations fail to capture the full dynamics.

**Method.** The authors employ advanced hybrid stabilizer-tensor network simulation techniques (using GCAMPS) to simulate syndrome extraction on surface codes, allowing for the inclusion of coherent noise terms.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the surface code, a topological stabilizer code used for fault-tolerant quantum computation. The noise model specifically incorporates gate-based nearest neighbor crosstalk noise modeled as a coherent ZZ rotation.

**Key observables.** Logical Error Rate ($P_L$) and the Code Threshold.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Crosstalk parameter ($	heta$), physical error rates ($p_1, p_2$), and code distance ($d$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The simulation results are a lower bound due to tensor network truncation; previous studies often relied on stochastic approximations of coherent effects.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare logical error rates for various noise models (Pauli vs. coherent crosstalk vs. random sign noise) across different code distances, showing the quantitative impact of coherence.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of coherent noise in QEC, details the hybrid simulation method, presents results comparing coherent noise to idealized models, and concludes by emphasizing the necessity of accurate noise characterization.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Scalable realisation of quantum computing is reliant on the development of fault tolerant devices. Analysis of quantum error correction protocols typically considers incoherent noise models or noise-free syndrome measurements. While this is simple to simulate classically and straightforward to compute analytically, these simplifications are unable to capture the full dynamics of a noisy quantum system. In this work we use advanced hybrid stabilizer-tensor network simulation techniques to simulate coherent quantum crosstalk noise during syndrome extraction on a surface code. We show that the inclusion of coherence increases logical error rates and lowers the code threshold. In addition, we show that the specific distribution of the noise can quantitatively change logical error rates. The methods in this work allow simulation of quantum error correction with noise models previously inaccessible to classical simulation, providing new insights on the effect of crosstalk noise on quantum error correction codes.

</details>

### [Total, quantum, and classical measures of anticoherence for mixed spin states](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29436v1)

**Authors:** Jérôme Denis, Tara Lacaille, John Martin, Eduardo Serrano-Ensástiga  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29436v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29436_figures/2605.29436_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

Thick curves correspond to the fidelity-based definitions (27) and (37), evaluated analytically in Sec. IV; thin curves show the purity-based measures. In particular, AQ,F 2 = 1</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29436_figures/2605.29436_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2. Quantum purity-based t-anticoherence of reduced q-qubit states (seen as spin-q/2 states). Left (right): t = 1 (t = 2). Reduced states are obtained by partial tracing HOAP states [41] (top), GHZ states (middle), and W states (bottom). Disk areas scale with the magnitude of AQ,P t ; dark- purple disks of minimal size indicate vanishing anticoherence.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.29436_figures/2605.29436_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3. Maximal achievable total AC order as a function of the global purity. For each purity threshold, the pie chart shows the decomposition of the purity-based AC measure into its quantum contribution AQ,P t (blue) and classical contribution AC,P t (orange), evaluated at the maximal attainable order t. The number at the center of each pie gives this order. A fully filled pie indicates a state that is exactly t-AC. The states realizing the jumps are given in Eqs. (87), (89), (91), (92), and (94); see also Table I. The leftmost charts correspond to the MMS state, while the rightmost charts correspond to HOAP states [41].</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a comprehensive axiomatic framework to quantify 'anticoherence' in mixed spin states, separating the total observed isotropy into quantum and classical components. By defining total, quantum, and classical measures, the authors provide tools to diagnose whether a state's directional insensitivity stems from genuine quantum entanglement or mere classical statistical mixing. This has significant implications for quantum metrology and quantum reference-frame alignment.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides advanced tools for characterizing quantum resources (like directional insensitivity) in mixed states, which is crucial for understanding realistic quantum metrology protocols where noise and decoherence are present.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper develops a systematic framework to quantify 'anticoherence' (AC) for mixed spin states, crucially distinguishing between contributions arising from genuine quantum correlations and those from classical statistical mixing.

**Main result.** It rigorously defines total, quantum, and classical $t$-anticoherence measures, establishing mathematical relationships (e.g., $A_T^t = A_Q^t + A_C^t$) and showing how the quantum measure isolates intrinsically quantum directional insensitivity.

**Method.** The methodology is axiomatic, defining measures based on properties like monotonicity under noise and consistency with pure states, and constructing quantum measures via convex-roof extensions of pure-state functionals.

**Model / system.** The system analyzed is a spin-$j$ quantum state, modeled using the symmetric qubit embedding ($ho_S$ on $N=2j$ qubits). The analysis focuses on the properties of the reduced state $ho_t$ on $t$ qubits.

**Key observables.** Total $t$-anticoherence ($A_T^t$), Quantum $t$-anticoherence ($A_Q^t$), and Classical $t$-anticoherence ($A_C^t$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The order of anticoherence, $t$, and the purity of the state $	ext{Tr}(ho^2_t)$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes the existence of an axiomatic structure for these measures and relies on the ability to separate the total measure into quantum and classical parts.

**Figures summary.** Figure 3 illustrates the decomposition of purity-based AC into quantum and classical parts across different orders ($t$), and Table I summarizes these measures for various representative mixed spin states.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from defining the physical problem (distinguishing AC sources) to establishing axiomatic frameworks for total and quantum measures, constructing explicit measures (purity, distance, fidelity), and finally proving relationships between these measures for various spin systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Anticoherent spin states have isotropic low-order spin moments and are relevant to direction-independent metrology and quantum reference-frame alignment. In contrast to pure states, for mixed states such isotropy may originate either from genuine quantum correlations or from classical statistical mixing. We introduce an axiomatic framework for mixed-state $t$-anticoherence based on the symmetric qubit embedding. We distinguish total $t$-anticoherence, non-decreasing under SU(2)-covariant channels, from quantum $t$-anticoherence, defined as a resource monotone relative to a chosen total measure and constrained to coincide with it on pure states. This yields a classical contribution as their difference. We construct total measures based on reduced-state purity, Hilbert-Schmidt distance, and cumulative multipoles, and we discuss fidelity-based total candidates. We construct quantum counterparts via convex-roof extensions of pure-state functionals tied to bipartite entanglement in the symmetric sector. We provide explicit mixed-state examples, identify states with maximal quantum anticoherence supported on anticoherent subspaces, study robustness under particle loss for different types of states, and characterize the trade-off between purity and the maximal achievable anticoherence order.

</details>

### [Treewidth-Aware Gate Cut Selection for Reducing Transpilation Overhead on Superconducting Quantum Devices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29723v1)

**Authors:** Hana Ebi, Shin Nishio, Takahiko Satoh  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29723v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **3/5**

(A) A quantum circuit is represented as an interaction graph, where nodes correspond to qubits and edges correspond to two-qubit gates. (B) TW2S selects the cut position in two stages. Stage 1 uses a min-fill treewidth ordering to identify candidate edges that lie on sparse inter-community boundaries, and Stage 2 ranks these candidates by edge betweenness centrality with a degree penalty to select the final cut. (C) The selected edge is cut via quasi-probability decomposition (QPD), splitting the original circuit into smaller sub-circuits that can be executed independently on a noisy device and recombined to estimate the target...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29723_figures/2605.29723_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

Right: per-subcircuit ×1.5 (×9 total) strategy. Each cell aggregates over all configurations sharing the same ∆ECR, with R = 5 repetitions per configuration. Green = QPD wins; red = baseline wins.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.29723_figures/2605.29723_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIGURE 3. QPD win rate at M = 10,000 shots in the (∆ECR, |⟨H⟩ideal|) plane for shared (×1, left) and per-subcircuit ×1.5 (×9, right) strategies. Color encodes win rate (green = QPD wins, red = baseline wins). Odd-step configurations cluster near |⟨H⟩ideal| ≈0 (bottom), even-step configurations at |⟨H⟩ideal| ≳2.4 (top).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.29723_figures/2605.29723_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIGURE 4. Mean ∆err as a function of pecr (rows) and pmeas (columns), for M ∈{5K, 100K} and two strategies (shared budget, top; per-subcircuit ×1.5, bottom). Blue dashed box: IBM FakeSherbrooke operating point.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.29723_figures/2605.29723_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIGURE 5. Example of a min-fill elimination trace on an interaction graph. At each step, the vertex with the fewest required fill edges, shown as red vertices, is eliminated, and missing edges are added among its current neighbors, shown as green vertices, to form a clique. The figure illustrates how bags B(v) = {v} ∪N(v) and fill edges F (v) arise during elimination, which are later used to score candidate cut edges in Stage 1.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces TW2S, a novel graph-structural algorithm designed to select optimal two-qubit gates for 'cutting' in quantum circuits to minimize transpilation overhead on superconducting hardware. By leveraging graph metrics like treewidth and betweenness centrality, TW2S identifies structural bottlenecks, leading to significant, quantifiable reductions in required native gates compared to random selection. This positions graph-based analysis as a practical compiler tool for quantum compilation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a crucial compiler-side tool for quantum hardware optimization, directly addressing the gap between abstract quantum circuits and the physical constraints of near-term quantum processors.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge is minimizing the hardware overhead (SWAP gates) incurred during the transpilation of quantum circuits with long-range interactions onto superconducting quantum devices with sparse connectivity.

**Main result.** The proposed TW2S method consistently outperforms random cut selection by identifying structural bottlenecks in the interaction graph, leading to significant reductions in required two-qubit gates ($\Delta 	ext{ECR}$).

**Method.** TW2S is a two-stage, graph-only selection algorithm that first scores edges based on their contribution to the treewidth upper bound (Stage 1) and then ranks candidates using edge betweenness centrality with a degree penalty (Stage 2).

**Model / system.** The work focuses on superconducting quantum devices, using the FakeSherbrooke backend for testing. Benchmarks include synthetic graphs (Grid, Watts-Strogatz, SBM) and the physically relevant J1-J2 transverse-field Ising model.

**Key observables.** $\Delta 	ext{ECR}$ (reduction in ECR gates), Mean Squared Error (MSE) for breakeven analysis, Treewidth, Edge Betweenness Centrality.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The mixing ratio ($\mu = p_{out}/p_{in}$) in SBM graphs, the total shot budget, and the signal strength $|\langle H angle_{	ext{ideal}}|$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The selection process relies solely on graph-structural properties, avoiding backend-specific transpilation calls at selection time. The benefit of cutting is conditional on a breakeven analysis involving signal strength.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the TW2S pipeline (Circuit $	o$ Interaction Graph $	o$ Cut), show $\Delta 	ext{ECR}$ advantages across various graph families (Grid, WS, SBM), and plot the relationship between the mixing ratio ($\mu$) and the structural advantage.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of transpilation overhead, details the QPD mechanism, proposes the two-stage TW2S algorithm using graph metrics (treewidth, betweenness), validates the method on synthetic graphs and the J1-J2 TFIM model, and concludes with a rigorous breakeven analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

On superconducting quantum devices with sparse qubit connectivity, transpilation of long-range two-qubit interactions inserts additional SWAP gates, increasing hardware cost and execution error. Gate cutting via quasi-probability decomposition (QPD) can remove a selected two-qubit gate and thereby reduce routing overhead, but its sampling cost makes cut placement critical. We propose TW2S, a graph-only two-stage gate-cut selection method that operates on the circuit interaction graph without backend-specific transpilation at selection time. Stage 1 analyzes a min-fill elimination trace and scores edges by their contribution to a treewidth upper bound. Stage 2 ranks the resulting candidates by edge betweenness centrality with a degree penalty to identify routing bottlenecks. Across grid, Watts-Strogatz, barbell, and stochastic block model benchmarks transpiled to IBM's FakeSherbrooke backend, TW2S consistently outperforms random cut selection when the interaction graph contains identifiable sparse cuts. The advantage is governed not by absolute graph density but by moderate community structure and accessible inter-community edges. We further derive a mean-squared-error breakeven condition showing that, under a shared total shot budget, QPD is beneficial only when the ECR reduction is large enough and the signal strength is sufficient. Under an expanded per-subcircuit budget the signal-strength requirement is substantially relaxed. In noisy simulations of the J1-J2 transverse-field Ising model, TW2S achieves $Δ$ECR = 47 for n = 8, compared with approximately 9 for random selection, and yields lower estimation error than the uncut baseline in the tested strong-signal regime, with larger gains at increased shot budgets. These results position graph-structural cut selection as a practical compiler-side tool for turning circuit cutting into a targeted routing-reduction strategy.

</details>

### [Inverse generalised spin models of answers to questionnaires](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29739v1)

**Authors:** Arianna Armanetti, Luca Cecchetti, Paolo Sarti, Diego Garlaschelli, Miguel Ibáñez-Berganza  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29739v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

The BEG model accounts better than Ising and BC for the high probability density of subjects close to the mean (small dx) and far from the mean (large dx). Error bars are Wilson score confidence intervals at p = 0.05.</sub>

The Ising and BC models each exhibit two local maxima of the histogram hx′ 1 of the first principal component, the BEG model exhibits three local maxima, and the empirical histogram does not show multi-modal structure.</sub>

Right: histogram of the Euclidean distance to the mean, hdx; the BEG model reproduces the second and third peaks visible in the empirical data, which are absent in the Ising and BC models.</sub>

Left: comparison between the three spin models (Ising, BC, BEG) and the empirical distribution; only the BEG model accounts for the empirical peak of subjects near the origin for small d(M) x . Right: same comparison for the simple models (gauss, gauss-disc, copulas, cat-ind); the copulas and gauss-disc also reproduce the near-origin peak, while the gauss and cat-ind models predict a maximum at a much higher Mahalanobis distance.</sub>

**Summary.** This work applies advanced statistical physics tools, specifically generalized spin models (Ising, BC, BEG), to analyze ordinal data from questionnaires. By treating psychological constructs as emergent properties of interacting variables, the authors show that the BEG model best captures complex structural features like multi-modality and distance distributions, surpassing simpler Gaussian assumptions.

**Why it may be interesting.** While the application is in psychometrics, the mathematical framework—using mean-field theory and spin models to describe emergent collective behavior from local interactions—is directly analogous to modeling phase transitions or complex order parameters in condensed matter systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses how to model complex, ordinal data from psychological questionnaires by treating psychological constructs as emergent properties of interacting variables.

**Main result.** The Blume-Emery-Griffiths (BEG) model systematically outperforms simpler models (like Ising or Gaussian) in capturing the distribution of distances to the mean, suggesting its predictive power for complex data structures.

**Method.** The authors employ maximum entropy principles, mapping the problem onto generalized spin models (Ising, BC, BEG) and using advanced inference protocols like Monte Carlo estimation of likelihood gradients.

**Model / system.** The core system is the generalized spin model, defined by Hamiltonians incorporating linear, quadratic, and biquadratic couplings (BEG). These models are applied to ordinal questionnaire data, allowing for interactions beyond simple pairwise correlations.

**Key observables.** Principal-component histograms, histograms of Euclidean and Mahalanobis distances to the mean, and the model's ability to reproduce multi-modality.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The coupling constants ($\mathbf{h}, \mathbf{J}, \mathbf{K}$) defining the Hamiltonian, and the number of response options ($R$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumptions involve using maximum entropy models to constrain the system, and the necessity of using extended BEG models to maintain gauge invariance.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare item histograms and principal component histograms across various questionnaires, demonstrating the BEG model's superior fit to distance distributions compared to Gaussian or copula models.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by establishing the limitations of traditional psychometrics, introducing generalized spin models (Ising, BC, BEG) as energy-based alternatives, detailing the inference protocols, and finally comparing the models' ability to reproduce empirical structural features like multi-modality and distance distributions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Network psychometrics conceptualises psychological constructs as emergent properties of systems of interacting variables. Energy-based probabilistic models have gained popularity as models of these interactions, but their psychometric application has so far been limited, since most implementations assume binary or ternary responses and rely on limiting inference assumptions. We infer and analyse three generalised spin models of ordinal questionnaire data: the generalised Ising, Blume-Capel (BC), and Blume-Emery-Griffiths (BEG) models. We prove the concavity of the maximum likelihood estimation of the parameters, as well as the gauge invariance of the Ising and BC models. Afterwards, we propose an inference protocol of approximated likelihood maximisation, based on the Monte Carlo estimation of the likelihood gradients. We apply this procedure to eleven psychometric and sociological questionnaires, comparing the inferred spin models against the multivariate Gaussian. We then assess whether the inferred models reproduce the empirical features of the data in terms of principal-component histograms, and histograms of Euclidean and Mahalanobis distances to the mean answer. The multi-modality observed in the histograms of principal components is partially captured by the spin models. This trait of polarisation can be understood, in the light of mean-field theory, as coexistence of stable and metastable phases of the spin models. The BEG model systematically outperforms the other models in capturing the distribution of distances to the mean, while all models underestimate the heavy tails of the Mahalanobis distance. Overall, the analysis witnesses the predictive power of the BEG model, able to account better than others for the abundance of outliers and mean responders, and reveals highly non-linear features of questionnaire data that both Gaussian and spin models fail to account for.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.29071"></a>
### [A hidden bottleneck in classical and quantum linear reservoir computing](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29071v1)

**Authors:** Johannes Nokkala, Federico Centrone, Francesco Arzani  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29071v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `correlated cavity matter` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5** · `quantum optics experiment` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29071_figures/2605.29071_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the linear-reservoir bottleneck. Information processing capacity (IPC) is a task- independent measure of a dynamical system’s ability to retain and transform input information. In linear reservoirs, prepro- cessing may already generate nonlinear features, but the reservoir transformation itself cannot create new expressive power beyond what is present in those features. If memory is carried by the reservoir, the IPC at each fixed delay is bounded by the preprocessing-only IPC, even though capacities from different delays may accumulate in the total IPC. If memory is carried by preprocessing, the total IPC cannot exceed the preprocessing-only IPC.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29071_figures/2605.29071_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. The considered scheme. Memory is classical, which keeps the scheme within reach of experiments. The output is formed from the elements of the covariance matrix of x-quadratures σx. Nonlinearity in input is introduced by the input dependent modulation of squeezed states and again in the de-Gaussification of the reservoir state via photon addition or subtraction on mode 1 (assumed to be deterministic, or post-selected).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.29071_figures/2605.29071_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Breaking the Gaussian limit in the case of a memoryless reservoir and memoryless preprocess- ing. Top panel: total capacity as a function of the num- ber of optical modes N for a purely Gaussian reservoir and for reservoirs augmented by conditional single-photon opera- tions. The dotted curve indicates the Gaussian limit, while the dashed curve indicates the absolute limit set by the num- ber of trained weights. The shaded region is therefore inacces- sible to Gaussian reservoirs but becomes reachable once non- Gaussian operations are introduced. Bottom panel: break- down of the capacity among polynomial degrees. Since the total capacity is the area under this curve, approaching the...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.29071_figures/2605.29071_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Witnessing the non-Gaussianity of the reservoir with finite resources. Excess capacity ∆C is the difference between observed capacity and the Gaussian limit. Top panel: ∆C as a function of ensemble size M. Excess capacity degrades gracefully as statistical noise in the observables increases, but eventually the noise washes it out. Bottom panel: excess capacity when the Gaussian limit is artificially restricted im- proves finite-resource visibility. Unlike elsewhere, here all in- put modes have identical encoding, turning the Gaussian limit constant. This decreases capacity but allows a finite ∆C at lower ensemble sizes. All results are averaged over 80 realiza- tions.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.29071_figures/2605.29071_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Breaking the Gaussian limit when there is memory. Top panel: total capacity as a function of memory length τmax when memory is provided by the preprocessing. Relative to the memoryless case (τmax = 0), allowing multi- ple delays strongly alleviates the high-degree bottleneck and enables the non-Gaussian reservoir to approach the absolute limit much more efficiently. Middle panel: capacity of cross- terms only. When preprocessing provides memory, even the Gaussian case is able to have capacity from nonlinear func- tions featuring multiple delays. This leaves the bound un- changed. Bottom panel: capacity as a function of delay when memory is provided after the quantum reservoir. Here...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work establishes a fundamental bottleneck in the information processing capacity of linear reservoir computers, showing that they are limited by the input features they receive. By applying this to continuous-variable quantum systems, the authors derive a Gaussian bound on the achievable capacity. Crucially, they demonstrate that introducing minimal non-Gaussian operations, such as single-photon subtraction, allows the system to surpass this bound, providing an operational witness for non-Gaussian quantum resources.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a concrete, measurable resource (excess capacity) to witness non-Gaussian processing in photonic quantum systems, which is highly relevant for understanding the limits of quantum computation beyond simple Gaussian operations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper identifies a hidden bottleneck in the information processing capacity (IPC) of linear reservoir computers, both classically and quantum mechanically.

**Main result.** Linear reservoir dynamics cannot create new fixed-delay expressive power beyond what is present in the preprocessed input, establishing a Gaussian bound for CV quantum reservoirs.

**Method.** The authors combine rigorous analytical derivations of information bounds with numerical simulations using finite-ensemble statistics to test the limits of Gaussian vs. non-Gaussian processing.

**Model / system.** The system is continuous-variable (CV) quantum reservoir computing, utilizing bosonic optical modes. The dynamics are analyzed using linear reservoir models, which are extended by introducing non-Gaussianity via conditional single-photon operations.

**Key observables.** Information Processing Capacity (IPC), excess capacity ($\Delta C$), and the covariance matrix ($\sigma$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fixed delay ($	au$), reservoir size ($N$), ensemble size ($M$), and the degree of non-Gaussianity (single-photon operations).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the relevant observables admit a finite-dimensional linear representation, and the finite-resource model uses the Wishart approximation for sample covariance.

**Figures summary.** Figures schematically illustrate the linear-reservoir bottleneck, comparing memoryless vs. reservoir-based capacity, and show capacity scaling with reservoir size ($N$) and memory length ($	au_{max}$) for both Gaussian and non-Gaussian regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the general IPC framework, derives the linear-reservoir bottleneck theorem for both classical and quantum settings, and then applies this to CV quantum reservoirs, demonstrating how single-photon operations break the Gaussian bound.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We identify a hidden bottleneck in the information processing capacity of linear reservoir computers. When the measured features evolve linearly in the reservoir and the output is formed by linear readout with bias, we show that the capacity available at any fixed delay is limited by what is already present in the preprocessed input. Linear reservoir dynamics can therefore redistribute features, but cannot create new fixed-delay expressive power on their own. This limitation is hidden by global capacity measures, since contributions from different delays can accumulate even when each individual delay is strongly constrained. As an experimentally important realization of this general result, we derive the corresponding Gaussian limit for covariance-based continuous-variable quantum reservoirs. Numerical experiments show that experimentally accessible single-photon operations surpass this limit, establishing them as a genuine resource for quantum reservoir computing. The resulting excess capacity also provides an operational witness of non-Gaussian processing in black-box continuous-variable systems under minimal assumptions.

</details>

**Summary.** This work provides a detailed feasibility study for using GEO satellites for Quantum Key Distribution (QKD). It models complex channel impairments like atmospheric loss and background noise, using advanced finite-key security protocols. The results provide actionable design guidelines, emphasizing that strong Adaptive Optics is necessary for practical daytime operation.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on quantum optics/communication, the rigorous application of finite-key security bounds and the detailed modeling of complex, noisy, open quantum channels (atmosphere/space) provide valuable theoretical frameworks applicable to open quantum systems research.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper assesses the end-to-end feasibility of Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) using Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) satellites, focusing on mitigating extreme channel loss and background noise.

**Main result.** The analysis establishes practical operating thresholds and design guidelines, showing that strong Adaptive Optics (AO) correction is crucial for enabling reliable daytime QKD links.

**Method.** The study employs a comprehensive feasibility study using the decoy-state BB84 protocol, incorporating variable-length finite-key security analysis and detailed physical channel modeling.

**Model / system.** The system is a GEO downlink configuration, modeled using a physically realistic channel that accounts for diffraction loss, atmospheric extinction (using libRadtran), and background noise from sky radiance.

**Key observables.** Achievable annual secret-key-yield, finite-size secret-key rate ($\ell/N$), and the noise-click probability ($p_{	ext{noise}}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** GEO altitude (35,786 km), various wavelengths (e.g., 854 nm, 1550 nm), receiver/transmitter apertures ($a_R, a_T$), and AO correction bandwidth ($f_c$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a spherical-Earth model and incorporates specific statistical bounds (e.g., Poisson-Binomial) for security analysis.

**Figures summary.** Figures map the secret-key rate against system loss and noise probability, showing the critical role of AO and the comparison of yields across different wavelengths and geographic locations.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the physical channel impairments (loss, noise) using detailed atmospheric models, applies advanced finite-key security protocols, and synthesizes the results into feasibility maps that guide mission design.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum key distribution (QKD) via geostationary Earth orbit (GEO) satellites offers a compelling route to continuous, continental-scale secure communications. However, operation in this regime entails extreme channel loss and significant background noise, particularly if daylight operation is desired. We present a comprehensive end-to-end feasibility study of a decoy-state BB84 protocol in a GEO downlink configuration, incorporating variable-length finite-key security and tight statistical bounds to expand the achievable positive-key regime. Our analysis encompasses the principal receiver architectures relevant to downlink QKD and employs a physically realistic channel model that captures the dominant loss and noise mechanisms. We evaluate performance across rural, urban, and coastal environments at multiple wavelengths, including visible Fraunhofer absorption minima and the telecom band. Using historical cloud data across Europe, we forecast the annual secret-key yield across the continent. Through a systematic exploration of the high-dimensional parameter space, we identify key trade-offs and performance bottlenecks that determine feasibility. These results establish practical operating thresholds and provide actionable design guidelines for future GEO-QKD missions.

</details>

**Authors:** Masari Watanabe, Hirofumi Nishi, Taichi Kosugi, Shigekazu Hidaka, Ryo Sakurai, Yu-ichiro Matsushita  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30143v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5**

The H2 electronic structure provides the Born–Oppenheimer potential and Hellmann–Feynman force, which are embedded as an R-dependent KvN force kick. Repeated KvN–Langevin updates prepare a canonical nuclear phase-space state, from which two observables are read out: the Raman-active vibrational density of states by quantum phase estimation and the transition-state-theory rate constant from a canonical flux expectation.</sub>

(b) Implementation of the many-particle UNVE(∆t) block, showing the drift–kick–drift structure based on a second-order symmetric Suzuki–Trotter decomposition, UNVE(∆t) ≃U1(∆t/2) U2(∆t) U1(∆t/2). (c) PITE/cos-filter implementation of UPITE(σH), representing the diffusion block in the momentum Fourier space.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.30143_figures/2605.30143_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

(b) Ground-state PES relative to its minimum, compared with the FCI/STO-3G reference. This representation supplies V (R) and F(R) for the KvN force kick used in the molecular- dynamics simulations.</sub>

An initial wave packet on the dissociation side moves toward the bond-length region and relaxes under the KvN–Langevin thermostat. (g) Mean internuclear distance ⟨R⟩(t). (h) Kinetic temperature Tkin(t), with Tphys = 947 K and the corrected internal temperature Tint = 942 K. (i) Kullback–Leibler divergence DKL(ρ(t)∥ρeq) from the canonical reference distribution.</sub>

(b) QPE is applied to |α±⟩using the Hamiltonian KvN time evolution, and the flag and phase ancilla measurements give the branch-resolved fre- quency distributions.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops a quantum-circuit framework to simulate molecular dynamics in the canonical ensemble using a Langevin thermostat. It encodes the classical phase-space density as a Koopman-von Neumann wave function, allowing for the preparation of equilibrium states on a quantum computer. The protocol successfully calculates key physical observables, such as the VDOS and TST rate constant, for the H2 molecule.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, circuit-level protocol for quantum-classical hybrid molecular dynamics, directly linking quantum computation to fundamental statistical mechanics problems like canonical ensemble preparation and rate theory.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To construct a quantum-circuit framework for performing finite-temperature molecular dynamics in the canonical ensemble (NVT) using a Langevin thermostat.

**Main result.** The authors successfully demonstrate an end-to-end quantum protocol that prepares the canonical phase-space state and uses it to calculate both the vibrational density of states (VDOS) and the Transition-State-Theory (TST) rate constant for the H2 molecule.

**Method.** The dynamics are mapped onto a quantum circuit sequence that decomposes the Langevin evolution into Hamiltonian, friction, and diffusion blocks, with the diffusion block implemented via Probabilistic Imaginary-Time Evolution (PITE).

**Model / system.** The system is the H2 molecule, modeled using the Born-Oppenheimer approximation. The dynamics are governed by the Langevin equation, whose Fokker-Planck evolution is encoded in the Koopman-von Neumann (KvN) wave function.

**Key observables.** Vibrational Density of States (VDOS) via Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) and the Transition-State-Theory (TST) rate constant.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Target physical temperature ($T_{	ext{phys}}$), friction coefficient ($\gamma$), and the time step ($\Delta t$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Born-Oppenheimer approximation is retained, and the quantum simulation requires an analytical correction to account for the leading-order temperature bias introduced by the PITE-realized cosine filter.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 summarizes the workflow from electronic structure to VDOS (via QPE) and TST rate constant. Figure 8 compares finite-particle phase-space samples to the analytically encoded canonical KvN density.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the mathematical derivation of the Langevin dynamics, maps the evolution onto a quantum circuit structure (decomposing the Liouville flow), and then demonstrates the application of this circuit pipeline to calculate both dynamical (VDOS) and static (TST) properties.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We construct a quantum-circuit framework for finite-temperature molecular dynamics in the canonical ensemble (NVT) with a Langevin thermostat, connecting canonical state preparation to subsequent physical-property readouts. The classical nuclear phase-space distribution is encoded as a Koopman--von Neumann (KvN) wave function, and canonical state preparation is formulated as Langevin-type Fokker--Planck relaxation. The Hamiltonian Liouville flow, momentum friction, and momentum diffusion are decomposed into separate circuit blocks. The friction block is represented by a symmetrized momentum-space dilation, whereas the diffusion block is implemented as a cosine filter realized by probabilistic imaginary-time evolution (PITE). We analytically quantify the leading-order temperature bias caused by replacing the Gaussian diffusion kernel with this PITE-realized cosine filter. This analysis yields an internal-temperature correction that targets the desired physical equilibrium distribution. As a proof-of-concept demonstration connecting quantum chemistry to KvN nuclear dynamics, we study the H$_2$ molecule. Numerical simulations show relaxation from a nonequilibrium phase-space distribution to a canonical KvN state. From this canonical state, we demonstrate two complementary readouts: a dynamical quantum-phase-estimation readout of the vibrational density of states associated with the H--H stretch coordinate and a static canonical evaluation of the transition-state-theory (TST) rate constant. This work demonstrates, in a minimal molecular system, a circuit-level protocol that connects Langevin canonical state preparation to physical-property calculations, providing a concrete step toward quantum--classical hybrid molecular dynamics on quantum computers.

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical paper determines the critical exponents for the planar random-cluster model at $q=4$ by relating it to the four-state Potts model. It employs advanced mathematical tools, including the Gaussian Free Field and sophisticated coupling techniques, to derive precise asymptotic scaling laws for key physical observables. The work provides a deep mathematical understanding of critical phenomena in 2D lattice models.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on classical statistical mechanics, the rigorous mathematical techniques involving conformal field theory, GFF, and scaling limits are highly relevant to understanding critical phenomena in quantum many-body systems and quantum field theories.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to extract the critical exponents for the planar random-cluster model when the cluster-weight $q=4$, and to determine the corresponding critical behavior of the four-state Potts model.

**Main result.** The authors derive specific critical exponents for various thermodynamic quantities (free energy, susceptibility, correlation length) for both models, establishing precise scaling laws in the critical regime.

**Method.** The analysis heavily utilizes advanced techniques including the Baxter-Kelland-Wu coupling, relating the model to the Gaussian Free Field (GFF), and employing rigorous bounding techniques from large deviation theory.

**Model / system.** The primary system is the planar random-cluster model with $q=4$, which is mathematically equivalent to the four-state Potts model on the square lattice ($\mathbb{Z}^2$). The analysis focuses on the critical point ($p_c=2/3$ for the random-cluster model).

**Key observables.** Free energy ($f$), susceptibility ($\chi$), correlation length ($\xi$), and various connection/crossing probabilities ($\pi_1, \pi_2, \Delta$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The critical parameters include the cluster-weight $q=4$, the critical probability $p_c=2/3$, and the critical inverse temperature $eta_c$ for the Potts model.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivations rely on the convergence of the height function to the GFF, the quasi-multiplicativity of certain probabilities, and the use of complex coupling/bounding arguments involving geometric domains (e.g., flower domains).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the geometric structures used in the proofs, such as the double four-petal flower domain and various loop configurations contributing to the correlation functions.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the connection between the models and the GFF, then deriving general scaling laws for correlation functions using M-formulas and bounding techniques, and finally applying these tools to derive the specific critical exponents for the observables.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Using the Baxter-Kelland-Wu coupling and the convergence of the height function of the six-vertex model to the Gaussian Free Field, we extract critical exponents for the planar critical random-cluster model at $q=4$, and the planar four-state Potts model.

</details>

**Authors:** Atanu Bhunia, Saronath Halder, Preeti Parashar, Ritabrata Sengupta  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29775v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `measurement-induced transitions` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

**Summary.** This theoretical paper rigorously analyzes the concept of 'quantum nonlocality without entanglement' by studying how sets of orthogonal product states behave under local measurements. The core finding is that the structural completeness of the state set dictates its robustness: complete bases resist local indistinguishability, whereas incomplete bases can undergo 'activation' to become locally indistinguishable.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides fundamental structural theorems about the boundaries of quantum information processing, specifically characterizing when local measurements can destroy the ability to distinguish quantum states, which has implications for quantum cryptography and resource theories.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the conditions under which quantum nonlocality can be activated—transforming a locally distinguishable set of states into a locally indistinguishable one—without relying on entanglement.

**Main result.** Completeness of an orthogonal product basis prevents activation via local projective measurements, while incompleteness allows for such activation, providing a structural understanding of local-to-nonlocal transitions.

**Method.** The authors use rigorous mathematical proofs based on analyzing the constraints imposed on local measurement operators (LOCC/LPCC) when distinguishing or failing to distinguish specific sets of orthogonal product states.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on orthogonal product states in composite quantum systems, particularly in bipartite and multipartite Hilbert spaces, analyzing the behavior of these states under local operations.

**Key observables.** Local distinguishability, local indistinguishability, and the structural property of 'completeness' of the product basis.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The dimensionality of the local Hilbert spaces (e.g., $\mathbb{C}^3 \otimes \mathbb{C}^3$ or $\mathbb{C}^3 \otimes \mathbb{C}^6$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often restricts itself to local projective measurements (LPCC) rather than general POVMs, and the theorems rely on the initial distinguishability of the state set.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the structural difference between complete (Fig. 2) and incomplete (Fig. 3) product bases regarding how post-measurement states are constrained, and show specific state representations in $\mathbb{C}^3 \otimes \mathbb{C}^3$ (Fig. 4).

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the general framework of local distinguishability and activation. It then proves that complete bases are robust against activation (Theorems 7, 8, 9). Subsequently, it contrasts this by analyzing specific incomplete sets ($S_2$) that *are* activable, concluding that 'incompleteness' is necessary for activation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

A set of orthogonal product states is said to exhibit "quantum nonlocality without entanglement" if it is locally indistinguishable, i.e. no sequence of local operations and classical communication (LOCC) can perfectly discriminate the states. Building on this foundational idea, recent studies have highlighted the phenomenon of "genuine activation of hidden nonlocality", where a set of initially distinguishable orthogonal states becomes locally indistinguishable through orthogonality-preserving LOCC transformations. In this letter, we establish that any complete orthogonal product basis that is initially locally distinguishable remains so under all orthogonality-preserving local projective measurements, thereby ruling out activation via orthogonality-preserving local projective measurements and classical communication. We further introduce and formalise the notions of "strongly local sets", namely locally distinguishable sets that remain non-activable under all bipartitions. Interestingly, the study of "local activability" of distinguishable sets is useful to characterise the boundary between LOCC distinguishability and its irreversible loss in multipartite systems. Our results provide a rigorous structural understanding of local-to-nonlocal transitions in quantum state discrimination.

</details>

**Authors:** E. S. Benilov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30084v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Curve (t) is computed theoretically, using the EV model (36)–(41); curve (e) corresponds to the empirical formula of Ref. [22]. The latter strictly applies to the range between the triple and critical points, so its exten- sion into the supercooling region is shown by a dotted line.</sub>

**Summary.** This work applies the Enskog-Vlasov kinetic equation to model the supercooling of liquids, predicting that the surface tension diverges at the spinodal temperature ($T_s$). The analysis uses stability criteria derived from the kinetic model to map out the instability region, offering a theoretical understanding of the liquid-to-solid transition.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous application of a kinetic equation like EV to predict a fundamental thermodynamic singularity (surface tension divergence) provides a theoretical framework for understanding phase instability in dense matter, which is relevant to non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the supercooling of liquids, focusing on determining the spinodal temperature and analyzing the behavior of the liquid-vapor interface as the liquid approaches instability.

**Main result.** The Enskog-Vlasov model predicts that the surface tension of a supercooled liquid diverges at the spinodal temperature ($T_s$), which is linked to the emergence of oscillatory waves at the interface.

**Method.** The study employs the Enskog-Vlasov (EV) kinetic equation, combining Enskog's collision integral with a Vlasov-style description of the van der Waals force, and uses stability analysis on the resulting equations.

**Model / system.** The system is supercooled fluids (e.g., Argon, $	ext{N}_2$, $	ext{H}_2	ext{O}$). The model is the Enskog-Vlasov (EV) kinetic equation, which is calibrated using macroscopic parameters like critical and triple-point values.

**Key observables.** Spinodal temperature ($T_s$), surface tension ($\gamma$), density profile $n(z)$, and the divergence of $\gamma$ at $T_s$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Molecular diameter ($D$), van der Waals constant ($a$), Korteweg constant ($K$), and the ratio $\Delta T/T_{tp}$ for different cooling paths (isobaric vs. isochoric).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes a nonrotational EV description and relies on series expansions (e.g., up to $L=5$) and phenomenological calibration against empirical data.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the divergence of surface tension ($\gamma$) vs. temperature ($T$) near $T_s$, and stability portraits in the density-temperature plane showing the spinodal boundaries for various fluids.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the EV model, calculates thermodynamic properties and the liquid-vapor interface structure, analyzes stability using wave theory to find the spinodal curve, and compares results across multiple fluids.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

A model combining Enskog's collision integral for dense fluids with a Vlasov-style description of the van der Waals force is applied to supercooling. First, the spinodal temperature $T_{s}$ is calculated, at which a liquid becomes unstable to small perturbations and transitions to solid. In particular, it turns out that isochoric cooling allows one to reach a lower temperature than isobaric cooling. Second, the surface tension of a supercooled liquid-vapor interface is shown to diverge at $T_{s}$. The singularity is caused by an oscillatory region emerging on the liquid side of the interface as $T\rightarrow T_{s}$; it develops because the liquid approaches instability, and the interface starts radiating (so far, evanescent) waves. At $T=T_{s}$, the waves cease to be evanescent and the oscillatory region extends to infinity -- hence, the singularity of the surface tension. Since this effect has a clear physical interpretation, it should occur regardless of the model and approximations under which it was obtained. This and the other results of the paper are illustrated using argon and several other fluids.

</details>

### [Hidden Ising models from the generalized Yang-Baxter equation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30007v1)

**Authors:** Akash Sinha, Somnath Maity, Pramod Padmanabhan, Vladimir Korepin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30007v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.30007_figures/2605.30007_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Action of the Hamiltonian density of (8).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.30007_figures/2605.30007_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4: Thermodynamic properties of the hidden critical (g = 1) transverse-field quantum Ising model for a system size N = 200, with kB = 1 in natural units, are shown as functions of temperature T: (a) free-energy density f(T), (b) thermal entropy per site s(T), and (c) heat capacity per site c(T). The results are obtained using the energy spectrum given in Eq. (49).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.30007_figures/2605.30007_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

**Summary.** This paper develops a general framework to analyze multi-site spin models that behave like Transverse Field Ising Models (TFIMs). By leveraging the generalized Yang-Baxter equation and the QISM, the authors prove the system is effectively free-fermionic, leading to a highly degenerate spectrum. This provides a powerful tool for understanding integrability in complex quantum spin chains.

**Why it may be interesting.** The connection between complex, seemingly interacting spin models and exactly solvable free-fermionic structures via advanced integrability techniques (gYBE) is highly relevant for understanding quantum critical points and exotic phases in condensed matter systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To construct and analyze multi-site interaction spin systems that are effectively Transverse Field Ising Models (TFIMs) under the hood, by utilizing the local algebra structure.

**Main result.** The system's spectrum is shown to be free-fermionic, exhibiting a huge degeneracy sourced by local conserved quantities, and the model's integrability is established via the generalized Yang-Baxter equation.

**Method.** The analysis employs the Quantum Inverse Scattering Method (QISM) adapted to a multi-site generalization of the Yang-Baxter equation (gYBE) and utilizes the boost operator formalism to derive conserved charges.

**Model / system.** The system is a one-dimensional spin-1/2 Hamiltonian with multi-site interactions, whose local algebra resembles that of the TFIM. It is mapped to a Majorana fermion system whose Hamiltonian is shown to be free-fermionic in an effective single flavor.

**Key observables.** Residual thermal entropy density $s(0) = 2 \ln 2$ at zero temperature, and the free energy density $f(T)$ in the thermodynamic limit.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The parameters $\zeta, \eta, 	ilde{\zeta}, 	ilde{\eta}$ govern the local interactions, and the analysis is sensitive to the boundary conditions (open vs. closed chains).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation relies on the existence of a multi-site generalization of the Yang-Baxter equation and the ability to map the complex spin interactions to an effective free-fermionic Majorana chain.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the action of the Hamiltonian density on the lattice and the structure of the conserved charges derived from the transfer matrix.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically builds the model by first establishing the local algebra, then mapping it to a Majorana fermion Hamiltonian, proving its equivalence to a TFIM, and finally using the QISM/gYBE framework to derive conserved charges and calculate thermodynamic properties.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce a one dimensional spin $\frac{1}{2}$ Hamiltonian with multi-site interactions, but still local. The algebra of its Hamiltonian densities resembles that of the transverse field Ising model. Using this fact we show that its spectrum is free-fermionic but with a huge degeneracy for each level. The source of the degeneracy is a set of local conserved quantities that act like a classical background field for the quantum system. The thermodynamics of this system is contrasted with the standard Ising model. At the gapless points in the energy spectrum, we show that this system can be derived from the quantum inverse scattering method adapted to a multi-site generalization of the Yang-Baxter equation as introduced by E. Rowell and Z. Wang. The $R$-matrix is constructed using generators of extraspecial 2-groups. This helps us extract all the conserved charges and lay the framework for a general mechanism to generate such multi-site interaction spin systems that are transverse field Ising models under the hood. A remark on how to obtain P. Fendley's free-fermion in disguise models in this formalism is also included.

</details>

### [Measuring anyon dispersion with tunneling probes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29017v1)

**Authors:** Taige Wang, T. Senthil  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29017v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

A weak impu- rity transfers momentum q to one constituent, and the inter- ference between amplitudes with and without this scattering produces the q component of the conductance map. (b) Nu- merical fixed-q line cut for the aaa sector. The first dashed line marks the clean activation threshold 3∆a, while the sec- ond marks the on-shell QPI branch ωaaa(q). The indicated powers are those of ∂2 V (dI/dV ) for Laughlin ν = 1/3.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29017_figures/2605.29017_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Fourier-transform STS signals from the full numerical convolution kernel. (a) dI/dV for the ba sector. The two dashed guides correspond to scattering b and scattering a. (b) First energy derivative of the same ba signal. (c) dI/dV for the aaa sector. (d) Second energy derivative of the aaa signal. The dashed guide is ωaaa(q) = 3∆a + q2/(8Ma).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<sub>FIG. 3. QTM. (a) A monolayer graphene (MLG) QTM probe forms a planar tunnel junction with the FCI sam- ple. The twist angle selects the injected electron momentum K. (b) dI/dV and ∂2 V (dI/dV ) for the aaa contribution to A+ e,FCI(K, eV ) in the small-pocket, elastic-tunneling limit.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper details methods to spectroscopically measure the dispersion of anyons, fractionalized quasiparticles, in topological materials like Fractional Chern Insulators. It utilizes both Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy (QPI) and Quantum Twisting Microscopy (QTM) to analyze the singularity structure of tunneling spectra. The distinct power laws observed in these measurements allow researchers to differentiate and quantify the energy and momentum characteristics of various anyon excitation types.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful experimental roadmap for characterizing exotic quasiparticles in strongly correlated topological phases, linking abstract topological concepts (anyons) to measurable spectroscopic signatures.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to develop spectroscopic techniques to measure the dispersion relation of anyons, which are fractionalized quasiparticles, in topological systems like Fractional Chern Insulators (FCI).

**Main result.** By analyzing the power-law singularities in quasiparticle interference (QPI) patterns and measuring fixed-momentum thresholds via quantum twisting microscopy (QTM), the distinct dispersions of compact excitations, bound anyon molecules, and unbound continua can be separated and quantified.

**Method.** The study combines theoretical derivations of weak-scattering singularities using Green's functions with analysis of experimental signatures from Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy (STS) and Quantum Twisting Microscopy (QTM).

**Model / system.** The physical system under study is the Fractional Chern Insulator (FCI) or related topological matter, which hosts anyons. The analysis models the low-energy dispersion of these mobile anyons, which dictate the ground state of doped systems.

**Key observables.** The primary observables are the power-law exponents of the singularity structure in the differential conductance ($	ext{d}I/	ext{d}V$) and the fixed-momentum thresholds measured by QTM.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Key parameters include the topological filling fraction ($
u$), the momentum transfer ($q$), and the energy detuning ($\omega - E_{branch}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on local expansions near isolated minima and assumes that matrix-element constraints do not alter the fundamental on-shell branch points.

**Figures summary.** FIG. 1 illustrates the QPI geometry; FIG. 2 shows convolution kernels for bound and continuum sectors; FIG. 3 details the QTM setup and spectral features for the three-anyon continuum; TABLE I and TABLE II summarize the expected power laws for different excitation sectors.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the physical context (anyons in FCI), then detailing the theoretical framework for analyzing spectral singularities (QPI/QTM), deriving the specific mathematical forms for different excitation types (e, ba, aaa), and finally presenting the comparative analysis of the two measurement techniques.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Anyons are usually characterized by their topological data and their fractional quantum numbers under global symmetries. In lattice systems such as fractional Chern insulators (FCI), they are also mobile quasiparticles. Their motion controls the possible ground states of the dilute anyon gas obtained by doping an FCI, including possible superconducting states. We show how tunneling probes can measure this motion. In scanning tunneling spectroscopy, weak disorder produces spatially oscillating quasiparticle-interference patterns whose branches reveal the dispersion of fractionalized constituents. In quantum twisting microscopy, planar momentum-conserving tunneling selects the total momentum of the injected electron, so the continuum thresholds of fractionalized electron spectra encode the dispersion of the constituent anyons. The resulting spectra distinguish compact electron-like excitations, bound anyon molecules, and unbound anyon continuum.

</details>

**Summary.** This work develops an advanced theoretical framework using an equivalent Wishart Ansatz to analyze the generalization performance of deep Bayesian neural networks in the proportional-width limit. By reducing the complex dynamics to a few scalar order parameters, the authors provide a powerful, testable prediction for how deep learning models learn complex data structures. The results are validated by comparing theory against extensive MCMC sampling experiments.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous mathematical framework developed for analyzing the statistical mechanics of deep learning models provides powerful analogies to disordered systems, particularly those involving complex energy landscapes or quenched disorder.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of extending theoretical understanding of generalization performance in deep, non-linear, multi-layered neural networks to the proportional-width regime.

**Main result.** The authors propose an effective Wishart Ansatz that allows predicting generalization performance by encoding strong representation learning in a small number of scalar order parameters, showing good agreement with sampling experiments.

**Method.** The core method involves using an equivalent Wishart Ansatz to perform a large deviation analysis on the partition function of Bayesian MLPs, extending this to CNNs via a hierarchical local kernel renormalization mechanism.

**Model / system.** The system studied is Bayesian Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on high-dimensional data. The analysis focuses on the proportional-width regime where training set size and network width grow together.

**Key observables.** Generalization performance (Training Loss and Generalization Error), order parameters, and the renormalized NNGP kernel.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Proportional-width regime ($P \sim N$), network depth ($L$), and the task-kernel overlap ($M_{yy}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is the validity of the Equivalent Wishart Ansatz, which approximates the dominant stochastic fluctuations of the hierarchical empirical kernels.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare learning curves (Train Loss vs. Gen. Error) derived from the EWA theory against MCMC samples from the Bayesian posterior for MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Gaussian data, demonstrating quantitative agreement.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from the general problem of deep network generalization in the proportional limit, introduces the EWA for MLPs, extends the theory to CNNs, and validates the approach by comparing theoretical predictions against extensive sampling simulations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The scaling limit where both the size of the training set $P$ and the width $N$ of a deep neural network grow at the same rate, the so-called proportional-width regime, has been intensely studied for shallow, single-hidden-layer networks. However, extending these non-perturbative results from shallow architectures to deep non-linear networks has proven very challenging. Here we present an effective approximate approach to predict the generalization performance of Bayesian multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) of fixed depth $L$ on arbitrary high-dimensional data. We propose an equivalent Wishart Ansatz to capture the dominant stochastic fluctuations of the hierarchical empirical kernels of MLPs. This allows us to perform a large deviation analysis for the partition function of MLPs in the proportional limit, expressed in terms of a renormalized NNGP kernel. In this description, even strong representation learning in the proportional limit is encoded in at most $L$ scalar order parameters, determined self-consistently. Extending the approach to convolutional architectures (CNNs), we identify a hierarchical local kernel renormalization mechanism, which allows to quantify more complex data-dependent transformations of the large-width kernel in CNNs due to finite-width effects. We test our effective theory against sampling experiments from the Bayesian posterior of finite deep neural networks with depths $L \sim O(10)$ and $P\sim O(10^3)$ on classic benchmark datasets, finding overall very good agreement together with two distinct types of systematic deviations.

</details>
