<a id="paper-2605.30108"></a>
### [Asymptotic magic state distillation with almost linear rate](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30108v1)

**Authors:** Koki Ehara, Ryuji Takagi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30108v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.30108_figures/2605.30108_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Overview of the distillation protocol. Step 1 is the pre- distillation step, which generates intermediate magic states from the initially prepared magic states so that they can withstand the distillation performed in Step 2. After that, the resulting states are processed in Step 2 to achieve 𝜀out →0.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.30108_figures/2605.30108_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Numerical plots for finite-size magic state distillation. The vertical axis shows the distillation rate, while the horizontal axis shows the exponent of the output error. The purple triangles represent repeated applications of the 15 →1 pre-distillation protocol, and the blue circles are obtained by subsequently applying our protocol. A blue label (𝑝, 𝑟) indicates that 𝑟rounds of our protocol are performed after 𝑝rounds of 15 →1 pre-distillation. The red dashed line indicates the rate of magic state distillation with constant overhead. For detailed information such as the coordinates of the points in this figure, see Appendix C.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper analyzes the fundamental trade-offs in magic state distillation, comparing the overhead exponent ($\gamma$) to the asymptotic distillation rate. The authors prove that these two quantities are generally decoupled, constructing protocols that maintain a large overhead exponent while achieving near-linear distillation rates. This has significant implications for designing resource-efficient quantum computation protocols.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a deep theoretical understanding of resource trade-offs in quantum computation, showing that resource scaling metrics (like $\gamma$) do not fully dictate achievable performance (like the rate) in complex error correction tasks.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the relationship between the overhead exponent ($\gamma$) and the asymptotic distillation rate in magic state distillation protocols.

**Main result.** They demonstrate that the asymptotic distillation rate is not constrained by the overhead exponent in the sublinear rate regime, presenting protocols with $\gamma > 1$ that achieve rates arbitrarily close to the linear rate.

**Method.** The authors analyze the resource scaling by constructing protocols based on error checking using measurements of logical Clifford operators and applying techniques from quantum error-correcting codes.

**Model / system.** The system is the magic state distillation protocol, which purifies noisy magic states ($ho$) using Clifford operations and quantum error-correcting codes (e.g., weakly self-dual CSS codes).

**Key observables.** Overhead exponent ($\gamma$), asymptotic distillation rate ($r_n/n$), and output error rate ($\epsilon_{	ext{out}}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The analysis focuses on regimes where the rate is sublinear, contrasting with the known special case where $\gamma=0$ implies a linear rate.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that Clifford operations can be implemented fault-tolerantly, allowing error suppression to be negligible compared to non-Clifford gate errors.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 compares the performance of their protocol (blue points) against other methods (purple points) across various output error rates, showing superior rates in certain regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the theoretical framework by defining the two key metrics ($\gamma$ and rate), presents the construction of a specific Hadamard-test-type protocol using outer and inner codes, and finally proves the separation between the two metrics by analyzing the scaling laws.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The overhead exponent -- characterizing the scaling of the number of noisy magic states with respect to the target distillation error -- has been a central quantity to benchmark magic state distillation protocols. On the other hand, a related but less investigated quantity motivated by an information-theoretic viewpoint is the asymptotic distillation rate, the largest ratio of output to input magic states such that error vanishes asymptotically. These two quantities are tightly related in the specific case -- the overhead exponent is zero if and only if the asymptotic distillation rate is linear. However, their relationship in other regimes has been unclear. Here, we show that their quantitative relation is generally not robust, by presenting a family of magic state distillation protocols with an overhead exponent not close to zero -- in fact, larger than one -- that still achieves the asymptotic rate arbitrarily close to the linear rate. This implies that the distillation rate is not constrained by the overhead exponent within the sublinear rate regime. Notably, our protocol is based on error checking by measurements of logical Clifford operators, which underlies the recent magic state cultivation protocol, suggesting the potential of this mechanism for asymptotic magic state distillation.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.28936"></a>
### [Hardware-Tailored Resource Estimation for Magic-State Distillation on Silicon Spin Qubits](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28936v1)

**Authors:** Songqinghao Yang, Christopher K. Long, Rubén M. Otxoa, Prakash Murali, Crispin H. W. Barnes, David R. M. Arvidsson-Shukur  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28936v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.28936_figures/2605.28936_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Resource-estimation pipeline. From top to bottom: A target quantum application is compiled into a logical circuit based on the choice of (1) logical operations, (2) QEC code, and (3) MSD protocol. The logical circuits are compiled into physical circuits by considering specific hardware architectures. The relevant QEC and MSD overheads depend on a hardware-specific noise model. The physical circuits are compiled into a sequence of hardware-tailored control pulses using pulse design. We use GRAPE to optimize the controls, thereby compressing the pulse sequence and potentially reducing errors and runtime. Finally, we produce theoretical resource metrics—space-time volume,...</sub>

**Summary.** This work provides a comprehensive resource analysis for achieving fault-tolerant quantum computation using silicon spin qubits. By modeling realistic, non-Markovian noise and comparing various error-correcting codes and qubit layouts, the authors quantify overheads. The results demonstrate significant resource savings through optimized control pulses and code tailoring, guiding the path toward scalable quantum processors.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed modeling of non-Markovian noise and the systematic comparison of resource costs across different physical architectures (sparse vs. dense) provides crucial, actionable guidelines for designing scalable quantum hardware.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper performs a detailed resource analysis to estimate the physical resources required for generating high-fidelity logical magic states on silicon spin-qubit platforms for fault-tolerant quantum computation.

**Main result.** Optimizing control pulses can reduce magic-state distillation overhead by 42%, and silicon-tailored biased error-correcting codes can achieve a threefold reduction in physical footprint compared to the surface code.

**Method.** The authors combine bottom-up and top-down methodologies, constructing a hardware-level noise model incorporating $1/f$ non-Markovian noise to estimate resource overheads across different qubit architectures.

**Model / system.** The physical system is silicon spin-qubits, analyzed across three architectural models: shuttling-based SpinBus, dense nearest-neighbor, and hybrid patched layouts. The analysis compares surface, color, and biased error-correcting codes.

**Key observables.** Resource overheads (space-time volumes, physical-qubit overhead, runtime), target logical error rates/fidelities, and physical resource requirements.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coherence times ($T_1, T_2^*$), gate fidelities (e.g., $99.9\%$), and noise parameters characterizing the silicon Hamiltonian.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that current small-scale experimental results can scale up, and the resource estimates are upper bounds based on current hardware parameters.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the resource-estimation pipeline, compare physical-qubit footprints for different algorithms across architectures, and show the space-time volume overhead for magic-state distillation factories.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a resource-estimation pipeline: starting from the application $ightarrow$ logical circuit $ightarrow$ physical circuit $ightarrow$ hardware-tailored control pulses $ightarrow$ resource metrics. It also inverts this process by fixing target fidelities to constrain hardware performance.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a resource analysis for generating high-fidelity logical magic states on silicon spin-qubit platforms. We consider a range of architectures, including a shuttling-based SpinBus design, a dense nearest-neighbor layout, and a hybrid scheme with shuttling-connected patches. We compare surface, color, and biased error-correcting codes, and analyze the $5\to1$ and $15\to1$ magic-state distillation protocols. Our approach combines bottom-up and top-down methodologies. We construct a hardware-level noise model based on a silicon-processor Hamiltonian with realistic parameters and $1/f$ non-Markovian noise, enabling estimation of physical resources required to reach target logical error rates. These results are propagated to system-level overheads for applications including spin dynamics, integer factorization, and quantum chemistry. Conversely, we fix target logical fidelities and derive corresponding constraints on hardware performance. Our framework enables systematic evaluation of resource-reduction strategies. We find that optimized control pulses reduce magic-state distillation overhead by 42\% compared to standard gate implementations. In addition, silicon-tailored biased error-correcting codes achieve an approximately threefold reduction in physical footprint relative to the surface code, even without physical-bias-preserving operations.

</details>

### [On certain combinatorial expressions of TASEP transition probabilities](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29520v1)

**Authors:** Lorenzo Vito Dal Zovo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29520v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

</details>

Panel b) represents the path in the 2-sites TASEP configuration space in bijection with the standard Young tableaux of panel a).</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.29520_figures/2605.29520_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

**Summary.** This paper provides a deep combinatorial framework for calculating the time evolution of the TASEP on a finite lattice. It achieves this by showing that transition probabilities can be expressed as signed sums over generating functions derived from generalized Young tableaux. This offers a powerful, non-equilibrium mathematical interpretation analogous to known steady-state results.

**Why it may be interesting.** The connection between non-equilibrium statistical mechanics models (like TASEP) and deep combinatorial structures (like Young tableaux) provides powerful mathematical tools for analyzing open quantum systems dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To derive a combinatorial and order-theoretic interpretation for the finite-time transition probabilities of the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process (TASEP) with open boundary conditions.

**Main result.** The transition probability matrix elements are expressed as signed sums involving exponential generating functions associated with generalized Young tableaux counts.

**Method.** The authors relate the time evolution operator $e^{Ht}$ to counting paths (words in local generators) and map these counts bijectively to enumerations involving multisets of cells in Young diagrams.

**Model / system.** The system is the TASEP, a model of hard-core particles hopping on a 1D lattice with open boundaries. The dynamics are governed by a Continuous-Time Markov Chain (CTMC) whose transition matrix is $P(t) = e^{Ht}$.

**Key observables.** Finite-time transition probabilities $\langle P(t) angle_{ij}$ and the associated exponential generating functions $F_D(t)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The time parameter $t$, the lattice size $N$, and the boundary conditions (open).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on decomposing the local generators and assuming absolute convergence to rearrange the infinite sums into the final combinatorial form.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate diagrams in $\mathcal{D}_{N+1}^{\eta, \xi}$ and show connections between TASEP paths and standard Young tableaux counts.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the combinatorial equivalence for transition sequences to generalizing this to the full transition matrix, culminating in a final summation formula for $\langle P(t) angle_{ij}$ over specific diagram families.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study combinatorial structures arising from finite-time transition probabilities of the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process with open boundary conditions. While much of the existing combinatorial theory regarding the TASEP concerns the steady-state distribution, we focus instead on the transient dynamics. We first show that the enumeration of transition sequences between two configurations of the open TASEP is equivalent to the enumeration of standard Young tableaux of a family of non-classical shapes which have been of recent interest in the combinatorial literature. This extends to the open-boundary setting the correspondence between the TASEP with periodic boundaries and cylindric tableaux.   We then introduce a family of tableau-like objects associated with Young diagrams in which repetitions of cells are allowed, subject to the partial order induced by the diagram. For each diagram, we collect the numbers of these objects into an exponential generating function. We prove that the entries of the homogeneous open TASEP transition matrix can be expressed as signed sums of such generating functions over suitable families of diagrams. This gives a combinatorial and order-theoretic interpretation of finite-time transition probabilities for the open TASEP, analogous to the combinatorial mappings known for steady-state probabilities.

</details>

For the two CNN controls with similar parameter counts to the QNN, both χ and observed transmission are effectively zero, indicating that the hidden and public tasks are functionally decoupled. For the narrow MLP, χ ≈0.11 and the observed trans- mission ratio is 0.41±0.16, implying a weak but nonzero poison component visible through the MNIST channel. For the representative QNN, χ ≈0.73 and the observed transmission ratio is 0.95 ± 0.05, showing that the...</sub>

**Summary.** This work extends the concept of subliminal learning to quantum machine learning, comparing how quantum neural networks (QNNs) transfer hidden knowledge compared to classical models. The study finds that QNNs are significantly better at retaining and transmitting this hidden information through restricted public interfaces. This has direct implications for the security assessment of quantum model supply chains.

**Why it may be interesting.** The comparison between QNNs and classical models in information transfer provides a concrete, quantifiable measure of how quantum representations might encode and leak hidden information, which is relevant to understanding quantum state leakage or robustness.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates 'subliminal learning,' which is the transfer of latent, unobserved knowledge from a 'teacher' model to a 'student' model through a limited public interface.

**Main result.** Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) are shown to retain significantly more hidden-task information during distillation compared to classical networks, especially when the public interface is restricted.

**Method.** The study compares two distillation pathways—an auxiliary channel and a restricted task channel—by analyzing the cross-task susceptibility ($\chi$) and the magnitude of the recoverable teacher drift ($\Delta	heta_{pub}$).

**Model / system.** The models include Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) operating on qubits via amplitude encoding and variational circuits, benchmarked against classical MLPs and CNNs.

**Key observables.** Cross-task susceptibility ($\chi$), student/teacher transmission ratio, and the magnitude of the teacher drift ($\|\Delta	heta_T\|$)

**Important parameters / regimes.** Teacher drift magnitude ($\|\Delta	heta_T\|$); the structure and rank deficiency of the public Jacobian matrix ($J$); the type of distillation channel (auxiliary vs. task).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on first-order local linear approximations around the shared initialization point for interpreting drift dependence.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the transmission ratio and susceptibility ($\chi$) for both auxiliary and task channels across MLP and QNN architectures, demonstrating the QNN's superior ability to preserve hidden signals.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the subliminal learning framework, analyzes the two distillation channels (auxiliary/task), develops a unified geometric picture based on teacher drift and cross-task susceptibility, and concludes with implications for quantum model security.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Machine learning models can inherit hidden behavioral traits through innocuous public interfaces, a phenomenon known as subliminal learning. Here we extend this framework to quantum models and study two distillation pathways: an auxiliary channel on random inputs and a restricted task channel in which the student matches a public supervised output while the hidden behavior resides on a disjoint task. Both classical and quantum neural networks (QNNs) exhibit efficient auxiliary-channel subliminal learning, but the task channel shows strong architecture dependence. Classical neural networks transmit little hidden-task information through the public-task interface, whereas QNNs retain most of the hidden-task signal. We show that a unified geometric picture explains both regimes: transmission is controlled by the teacher drift magnitude together with the fraction of hidden-task-relevant drift that remains visible through the public interface. These results identify a concrete security concern for quantum model supply chains and suggest a controlled route for hidden-information transfer in quantum information processing.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how different methods (mappings) can encode the complex Hamiltonian of atomic nuclei into a quantum circuit for use with the VQE algorithm. By testing SD, pnSD, and cSD mappings on $^{10}	ext{B}$ and $^{12}	ext{C}$, the authors compare qubit efficiency, hardware performance, and energy accuracy. The findings guide the choice of mapping, suggesting cSD is best for qubit count but SD showed best hardware performance for the tested state.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the resource scaling challenge in applying quantum algorithms to complex many-body physics, providing concrete benchmarks for mapping strategies that are crucial for near-term quantum hardware.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper compares different qubit-mapping strategies to study the ground state structure of atomic nuclei using the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) approach.

**Main result.** The SD mapping yielded the best post-error mitigated result for the $^{10}	ext{B}$ ground state on hardware (0.21% error), while the cSD mapping was shown to be the most qubit-efficient for scaling to complex nuclei.

**Method.** The study employs VQE on a noisy simulator and quantum hardware, comparing three mappings (SD, pnSD, cSD) to encode the nuclear Hamiltonian into qubit circuits.

**Model / system.** The physical system is nuclear structure, modeled using the shell model description for mid p-shell nuclei ($^{10}	ext{B}$ and $^{12}	ext{C}$). The Hamiltonian is represented in either a many-body particle basis or a Slater determinant (SD) basis.

**Key observables.** Ground state energy (percent error relative to exact result), wavefunction fidelity, and quantum resource counts (qubits, circuit depth).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The nuclei studied are $^{10}	ext{B}$ and $^{12}	ext{C}$. The resource comparison highlights the qubit count (SD: 26 qubits vs. cSD: 5 qubits for $^{10}	ext{B}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The comparison assumes the applicability of VQE and the effectiveness of error mitigation techniques (like ZNE) on current quantum hardware.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the single-particle states, qubit assignments for the three mappings, and comparisons of energy errors ($\Delta E$) and wavefunction fidelities ($F$) across different noise regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem by comparing three mappings (SD, pnSD, cSD) for VQE. It details the resource comparison (qubits, depth) and presents results on both noisy simulators and quantum hardware, concluding with an assessment of mapping utility for scaling.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we compare three qubit-mapping strategies to study the structure of the nuclear ground state within the shell model description employing the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) approach. Although the initial point for different mappings is a Hamiltonian matrix in many-body particle basis or Slater determinant (SD) basis, the structure of the trial wavefunction and resource counts are different for each mapping. These three mappings are tested for a mid $p$-shell nucleus $^{10}$B and compared the quantum resources required to find the ground state for each mapping. Further, we extend the qubit-efficient mapping to study the ground state of one more mid $p$-shell nucleus $^{12}$C. We run circuits up to 26-qubits representing their ground states on a noisy simulator (IBM's FakeFez backend) and quantum hardware ($ibm\_fez$). The best post-error mitigated results from the hardware for $^{10}$B ground state is obtained following SD to qubit mapping with a percent error of 0.21 \%. The percent errors for the same state following cSD and pnSD mapping are 3.37 and 8.88 \%, respectively. On the other hand, following the cSD mapping, the post-error mitigated ground state energy of $^{12}$C is 6.82 \% away from the exact result. We further evaluate the fidelity of the VQE wavefunctions obtained from hardware with respect to the shell model wavefunctions for the cSD mapping. This cSD mapping can be useful for scaling the VQE algorithm for complex nuclei across different mass regions in terms of qubit efficiency.

</details>

### [Theory of distribution skewness effect on polydisperse random close packing](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30185v1)

**Authors:** Vinay Vaibhav, Carmine Anzivino, Alessio Zaccone  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30185v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5**

<sub>FIG. 1. Variation of random close packing fraction with polydispersity and skewness. ϕRCP as a function of (a) polydispersity δ for different values of skewness S, as indicated, and (b) as a function of skewness S for different values of polydispersity δ, as indicated. Solid lines correspond to the analytical prediction ϕRCP(δ, S) obtained from the three–moment theory (see text and Supplementary Information [32]), while symbols denote the same function evaluated for different continuous size distributions: beta (open circles), generalized gamma (open squares), log-normal (closed triangles), and truncated power-law (closed diamonds). Symbols color correspond to the case of fixed S in (a) and...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a theoretical framework to calculate the random close packing density of polydisperse hard spheres by incorporating the effects of size distribution skewness and polydispersity. Using an extension of the Percus-Yevick equation, the authors derive closed-form predictions that show the results collapse onto master curves, providing a quantitative link between particle size statistics and jamming density.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on classical packing, the rigorous statistical mechanical treatment of density functionals and the use of moment expansions for complex systems are relevant to understanding phase behavior in many-body quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the random close packing density ($\phi_{RCP}$) of polydisperse hard sphere systems, focusing on how particle size distribution moments, particularly skewness ($S$), influence this density.

**Main result.** The authors derive a closed-form theoretical prediction for $\phi_{RCP}(\delta, S)$ that shows all size distributions sharing the same moments collapse onto a master curve, agreeing with existing numerical results.

**Method.** The study employs a theoretical framework based on the equilibrium model of crowding, extending the Percus–Yevick virial equation of state for mixtures using a three-moment approximation.

**Model / system.** The system modeled is polydisperse hard spheres, which can be extended to bidisperse mixtures. The theory relates the packing density to the moments of the particle diameter distribution.

**Key observables.** Random close packing density ($\phi_{RCP}$), dependence on polydispersity ($\delta$) and skewness ($S$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Polydispersity ($\delta$), Skewness ($S$), and the moments of the diameter distribution ($\langle\sigma^nangle$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumptions include using a truncatable three-moment description of the mixture equation of state and approximating local crowding by the equilibrium state.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates $\phi_{RCP}$ dependence on $\delta$ (fixed $S$) and $S$ (fixed $\delta$) for various distributions, showing collapse onto the analytical prediction. Figure 2 compares analytical predictions across different distributions against literature numerical estimates.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the theoretical framework by extending the PY equation of state, derives the closed-form solution for $\phi_{RCP}(\delta, S)$, corrects the theory for asymptotic limits, and then validates the predictions by comparing them against numerical results for various size distributions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the random close packing density, $φ_\textrm{RCP}$, of polydisperse hard sphere systems using a theoretical framework based on the equilibrium model of crowding. We derive a closed-form solution for $φ_\textrm{RCP}$ in terms of the moments of the diameter distribution, enabling an analytical exploration of the effects of polydispersity ($δ$) and skewness ($S$) on packing density. For a binary mixture, it is possible to explore a broader range of dependence of $φ_\textrm{RCP}$ on $δ$ for a given $S$ or on $S$ for a given $δ$. We show that the dependencies of $φ_\textrm{RCP}$ on skewness for a variety of continuous distributions collapse onto a theoretical master curve obtained for the binary mixture case. By correcting the theory so that it obeys known exact limiting behaviours for extreme size asymmetry, our analytical predictions not only agree with previously obtained numerical results, but also predict previously unexplored regions of the $φ_\textrm{RCP}$ parameter space.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Giant Photon Superbunching from Weak Nonlinearity](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29596v1)

**Authors:** You Wang, Xu Zheng, Timothy C. H. Liew, Y. D. Chong  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29596v1>

The fixed model parameters are Ui = 4.7 × 10−6 µeV, J = J′ = 1 µeV, J′′ = 0.5 µeV, γi = 2 µeV, and F = 200 µeV.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29596_figures/2605.29596_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Characterization of superbunching solutions. (a) Plot of g(2)(0) and mean photon number ⟨n⟩at the optimal super- bunching point for each drive amplitude F. These results are obtained by solving the quantum master equation nu- merically. (b) Similar to (a), but plotting ⟨n⟩versus g(2)(0) at each optimal superbunching point (dots), along with the predicted scaling relation g(2)(0) −2 ≈C/⟨n⟩with C cali- brated to the numerical data point at F = 250 µeV. (c) Time- delayed second-order correlation g(2)(τ) at an optimal super- bunching point with g(2)(0) ≈135. In all subplots, we fix ∆= 0.0415 µeV and γ = 2 µeV.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper predicts that giant photon superbunching can be engineered in weakly nonlinear coupled photonic cavities, circumventing the need for strong nonlinearities. By analyzing the quantum fluctuations in a four-mode ring resonator system, the authors show how fine-tuning couplings can force the system into a zero-displacement regime, leading to high correlation values ($g^{(2)} \approx 135$) at practical emission rates.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems, as it provides a concrete, weakly nonlinear mechanism for generating non-classical light states (superbunching) suitable for integrated photonics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of achieving giant photon superbunching ($g^{(2)}(0) \gg 2$) in photonic systems that rely on weak nonlinearities, moving beyond the historical requirement for strong nonlinearities.

**Main result.** Giant superbunching ($g^{(2)}(0) \approx 135$) is predicted and demonstrated theoretically in a four-cavity system, showing that this mechanism is highly compatible with integrated photonic platforms.

**Method.** The analysis involves deriving an effective Hamiltonian for quantum fluctuations, linearizing the system dynamics, and solving the resulting quantum Langevin equations in the frequency domain.

**Model / system.** The model is a system of $N$ coupled photonic resonators (specifically four-mode ring resonators) containing Kerr nonlinear media, governed by a Hamiltonian and a master equation.

**Key observables.** The second-order correlation function $g^{(2)}(0)$, the mean photon flux $\langle n_i angle$, and the emission rate $R_{	ext{em}}$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The system requires fine-tuning inter-cavity couplings ($J_{jk}$) and detunings ($\Delta_j$) to achieve the zero-displacement condition ($\alpha_i = 0$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on linearizing the effective Hamiltonian by retaining only quadratic terms in the fluctuation operators ($\hat{d}_j$).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the four-mode schematic, potential realizations using coupled ring resonators, and numerical plots showing the sharp peak in $g^{(2)}(0)$ as a function of detuning or coupling perturbation.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the general theoretical framework (Hamiltonian, fluctuation operators) to deriving the effective dynamics, solving the resulting quantum Langevin equations, and finally applying the results to a specific four-cavity ring configuration to confirm the superbunching mechanism.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Photon superbunching, which occurs when the second-order correlation satisfies $g^{(2)}> 2$, is typically associated with strong optical nonlinearities or collective multi-photon emission processes. We predict that extreme superbunching can also arise in systems of weakly-nonlinear photonic cavities, via the creation of a squeezed vacuum through interference engineering by fine-tuning inter-cavity couplings and drive parameters. We present numerical calculations indicating that a system of four photonic resonators containing representative Kerr media can achieve $g^{(2)}(0) = 135$ with a $80\,\text{kHz}$ emission rate. Unlike earlier superbunching schemes, this mechanism is highly compatible with integrated photonic platforms constructed using conventional optical media.

</details>

**Summary.** This work analyzes how shared common noise can synchronize the collective behavior of multiple groups of uncoupled oscillators. Using phase density mapping, the authors analytically prove that this group-level synchronization occurs even without direct coupling between the groups. This mechanism is significant for understanding emergent order in complex, noisy physical systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The mechanism of noise-induced collective behavior in uncoupled systems, particularly the emergence of global order from shared noise, provides insights into complex dynamics relevant to open quantum systems and pattern formation.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how common noise can induce group-level synchronization between multiple uncoupled groups of limit-cycle oscillators.

**Main result.** Group-level synchronization is robustly achieved when groups share a common noise source, even in the absence of direct inter-group coupling. This synchronization is analytically explained via phase density evolution mapping.

**Method.** The study employs numerical simulations of stochastic differential equations (SDEs) and derives analytical explanations using phase density evolution mappings and overlap fraction analysis.

**Model / system.** The system consists of multiple groups of coupled or uncoupled limit-cycle oscillators, modeled by phase dynamics governed by SDEs incorporating common and local noise components.

**Key observables.** Complex Kuramoto order parameter ($Z_g$), group-level synchronization measure ($d_{12}(t)$), and the overlap fraction of phase densities ($F^c_{\omega,n}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Noise strength ($\sigma$), noise correlation coefficient ($c_{in}$), intra-group coupling strength ($K$), and natural frequency heterogeneity ($\sigma_\omega$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on weak coupling and weak additive white noise, allowing reduction to a phase-only system, and uses approximations like the Euler-Maruyama method for discretization.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the time evolution of order parameters ($R(t)$) and phase density functions ($f_{g,n}(	heta)$) for various coupling and noise regimes, demonstrating convergence and overlap increase.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the synchronization phenomenon across four regimes: uncoupled nonidentical, coupled identical, coupled nonidentical, and the general case, using both time-domain order parameters and phase-space density evolution.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate group-level synchronization between oscillator groups induced by common noise in the absence of inter-group coupling. Each group receives a common noise shared by all its oscillators and independent local noise inputs to individual oscillators. The same common noise is applied to all groups. The system is studied with both identical and nonidentical oscillators, and with and without intra-group coupling. In the nonidentical case, natural frequencies are drawn from the same distribution for both groups, making them statistically equivalent. Through numerical simulations of this system, we find that the degree of synchronization within each group, measured by the absolute value of a complex Kuramoto order parameter, typically shows significant temporal fluctuations. Importantly, the complex order parameters representing the collective oscillations of the groups synchronize when the groups are driven by the same common noise. By deriving a phase density evolution mapping, we analytically explain how this group-level synchronization is achieved in the absence of intra-group coupling.

</details>

### [On the question of noise as a resource in quantum computing](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30026v1)

**Authors:** J. Montes, F. Borondo, Gabriel G. Carlo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30026v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This theoretical work proposes that non-unital noise can be harnessed as a resource in quantum computing. By analyzing the geometric expansion of the state space manifold, the authors show that controlled noise accelerates the approach of quantum states toward maximal randomness (Haar-like behavior). This suggests a pathway for designing quantum algorithms that intentionally utilize dissipation for computational advantage.

**Why it may be interesting.** The concept of noise acting as a resource is highly relevant to understanding decoherence in NISQ devices, suggesting that controlled dissipation can be engineered to speed up quantum information processing tasks.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether noise, typically seen as detrimental, can actually function as a resource to accelerate the approach of quantum states towards the maximally mixed, Haar-random state.

**Main result.** Non-unital noise, specifically amplitude damping, induces a geometric expansion in the state space manifold, causing the distance to the Haar measure to decay faster than in the purely unitary case at finite circuit depths.

**Method.** The authors employ geometric analysis on the Bloch sphere, deriving the local area expansion factor ($\Lambda$) of the effective map induced by the noise channel.

**Model / system.** The analysis uses a minimal 1-qubit model to derive intuition, extending to $n$-qubit systems evolving under local amplitude damping noise combined with a universal gate set like {H, T, CNOT}.

**Key observables.** Local area expansion factor ($\Lambda$), distance to Haar measure ($D(t)$), and the effective nonlinear map $F_\gamma: S^2 	o S^2$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Noise strength ($\gamma$), circuit depth ($t$), and the number of qubits ($n$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on approximating the evolution by tracking the dominant pure state after noise application, and the speedup is limited by excessive noise which biases the state towards the zero state.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the local area expansion factor ($\log|\Lambda|$) across the Bloch sphere, and show the decay of the distance to Haar ($D(t)$) for both unitary and noisy evolution, demonstrating the noise-induced speedup.

**Paper structure.** The paper first develops the geometric mechanism using a 1-qubit model (AD channel) to define the effective map and local expansion factor. It then applies this analysis to multi-qubit systems, comparing the decay of the distance to Haar ($D(t)$) for noisy vs. unitary evolution to show the resource potential of noise.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Noise is usually regarded as the main obstacle to achieving a scalable quantum advantage, but recent evidence in quantum reservoir computing [L. Domingo, F. Borondo, and G. G. Carlo. Taking advantage of noise in quantum reservoir computing, Scientific Reports, 13:8790, 2023] suggests that certain channels can, in appropriate regimes, improve performance by enriching the reservoir's effective dynamics. Motivated by this idea we propose a geometric mechanism to explain how non-unital noise applied together with a universal gate set leads to a faster approach to Haar-like distributions of the final states. We find that noise of this kind induces an effective volume expansion on the manifold of pure states. In order to intuitively understand this we use a minimal 1 qubit model where we take the amplitude damping channel and combine it with a renormalization rule that associates to each resulting mixed state a representative pure state. This composition defines a globally expanding nonlinear map on the space of pure states. We analytically derive the local area expansion factor and identify the global expansion threshold. Finally, we combine amplitude damping with the G3 = {H, T, CNOT} universal gate set to show how the approach to Haar-like behavior is faster in an appropriate parameter region. This leads us to propose noise as a possible resource in future quantum algorithms.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a comprehensive theoretical framework for analyzing adiabatic quantum dynamics, proposing methods that bypass the need to simulate time-dependent Hamiltonians directly. By developing generalized adiabatic theorems for processes like randomisation and adapted scheduling, the authors achieve optimal scaling for key quantum algorithms such as QLSP. This advances the theoretical understanding of fault-tolerant quantum computation protocols.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides rigorous, mathematically advanced tools to analyze quantum computation protocols, offering alternative, potentially more efficient, pathways than direct time evolution simulation for state preparation.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper develops alternative theoretical frameworks to achieve the goal of adiabatic quantum computing—tracking an eigenstate as a Hamiltonian changes—without incurring the computational overhead of simulating time-dependent Hamiltonian evolution.

**Main result.** It establishes general theorems and provides concrete algorithms for achieving optimal scaling in solving problems like the Quantum Linear Systems Problem (QLSP) by utilizing techniques like Poisson-distributed randomisation and adapted time schedules.

**Method.** The methodology involves deriving 'adiabatic' theorems for non-standard evolution processes, employing spectral theory, contour integration, and analyzing the fidelity bounds derived from master equations or generalized differential equations.

**Model / system.** The core model is a quantum system governed by a time-dependent Hamiltonian H(s) evolving from an initial state to a final eigenstate. The analysis extends to unitaries generated via Poisson processes or Trotter approximations.

**Key observables.** Infidelity ($1 - 	ext{Tr}(P(1)ho(1))$), Fidelity ($	ext{Tr}(ho(1)P(1))$), and the spectral gap $g(s)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The evolution time $T$, the gap function $g(s)$, and the condition number $\kappa$ (for QLSP).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumptions include the differentiability of Hamiltonians, the existence of spectral projectors, and the ability to define appropriate integration contours in the complex plane.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the spectrum of the Hamiltonian, the necessity of adapted time schedules due to non-constant gaps, and the integration contours used in the mathematical proofs.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from general frameworks for deriving adiabatic theorems (Theorem 1) to specific implementations using adapted schedules (Theorem 4), stochastic processes (Poisson unitaries, Theorem 6), and finally applies these tools to optimize algorithms like QLSP and Grover search.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In adiabatic quantum computing the aim is to track an eigenstate as the Hamiltonian changes. In the usual setup this is achieved using the natural time-dependent Hamiltonian evolution of the system and the main technical tool is the adiabatic theorem. We propose several alternative processes that achieve the same goal, but can easily be implemented on a gate-based quantum computer without the overhead of simulating time-dependent Hamiltonian evolution. We give a general framework for deriving `adiabatic' theorems for these processes.   As an application, we give various algorithms for solving the Quantum Linear Systems Problem (QLSP) with optimal scaling in the condition number. One of these algorithms was previously developed in [Cunningham, Roland 2024] and another can be seen as a randomised version of the discrete adiabatic algorithm of [Costa et al. 2022].   We also describe versions of Trotterisation in our framework, which allows several results from [An et al. 2025] to be reproduced in a randomised setting. In particular, bounds on the Trotter error in terms of the fidelity are obtained that are asymptotically better than the standard bounds.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.28925"></a>
### [A local description of strong symmetries and strong-to-weak symmetry breaking in quantum many-body systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28925v1)

**Authors:** Ruizhi Liu, Jinmin Yi, Dominic V. Else  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28925v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.28925_figures/2605.28925_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. The graphical construction of a bath evolution that sends a state with strong symmetry to one with only weak symmetry, see the text.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops a rigorous, local mathematical framework to redefine and diagnose strong symmetries and the process of strong-to-weak symmetry breaking in quantum many-body systems. By utilizing operator algebras in the infinite volume limit, the authors establish that strong symmetry is equivalent to a state being 'charge-coherent.' This provides a powerful, local tool for understanding symmetry constraints in complex quantum matter.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides essential mathematical machinery for characterizing phases of matter in open or mixed quantum systems, directly addressing the conceptual gap between symmetry breaking diagnostics and local observables.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to provide a mathematically rigorous and local description for strong symmetries and the process of strong-to-weak symmetry breaking (SW-SSB) in quantum many-body systems.

**Main result.** The authors establish that strong symmetry is equivalent to the state being 'charge-coherent' in the infinite volume limit, providing a local diagnostic tool for previously non-local phenomena.

**Method.** The methodology relies heavily on the algebraic approach using local operator algebras ($C^*$-algebras) to define states and symmetries in the thermodynamic limit, avoiding the difficulties of infinite tensor products.

**Model / system.** The focus is on general quantum many-body systems, particularly in mixed (non-pure) states, analyzed on an infinite lattice ($\Lambda$). The framework is general enough to apply to quantum spin chains.

**Key observables.** Strong symmetry, weak symmetry, charge coherence, fidelity, and the anomaly index (an element in $H^3(G, U(1))$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The infinite volume limit (thermodynamic limit), the nature of the symmetry group $G$ (compact), and the locality radius $r_\alpha$ of the symmetry action.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that distinguishing phases of matter should be possible via the expectation values of local operators in the thermodynamic limit. The algebraic approach is necessary because the Hilbert space formalism fails in infinite systems.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the limitations of previous non-local formulations, then introducing the rigorous algebraic framework for local observables, defining strong symmetry via charge coherence, and finally applying this to derive constraints like LSM-type anomalies.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In mixed states of quantum systems, symmetries come in two types: strong and weak. Furthermore, it has been argued that in quantum many-body systems, strong symmetries can be "spontaneously broken" down to weak symmetries. An issue is that as previously formulated, such "strong-to-weak symmetry breaking" appears to be a fairly non-local effect. In this paper, we show how to understand and diagnose strong symmetries and strong-to-weak symmetry breaking in an explicitly local way. Our main technical tool is a rigorous definition of strong symmetry in the limit of infinite volume, which generalizes the conventional finite-volume definitions, and for which we give several equivalent formulations, including one involving the concept of "local charge coherence". Finally, we introduce von Neumann systems, which in infinite-volume symmetries are intermediate between strong and weak symmetries. We derive a Lieb-Schultz-Mattis type anomaly constraint for von Neumann symmetries (and therefore, in particular, strong symmetries) in quantum spin chains.

</details>

### [Community detection in subject-subject networks from psychometrics data](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29749v1)

**Authors:** Arianna Armanetti, Luca Cecchetti, Eiko Fried, Diego Garlaschelli, Miguel Ibáñez-Berganza  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29749v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29749_figures/2605.29749_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Two-dimensional PCA projections of subject similarity matrices for three synthetic datasets, coloured by the communities detected by the community detection algorithm. The equal-factors and dominant-factor datasets are drawn from a single homogeneous population (no planted community structure); mixture-4 contains four equally-sized subgroups with distinct response profiles. Top row: similarity computed directly in the M-dimensional item-response space. Bottom row: similarity computed in the F-dimensional factor-score space. Community detection is done in both cases maximising the modularity of the similarity matrix after removal of its leading eigenvector of the similarity matrix...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work proposes a robust, network-theoretic framework to detect hidden community structures in psychometric data by analyzing subject-subject similarity networks. It overcomes limitations of traditional methods by projecting data into a factor-score space and using a sophisticated null model derived from spectral analysis. The resulting pipeline provides a principled, multi-metric test for genuine population heterogeneity in complex datasets.

**Why it may be interesting.** While the application domain is psychology, the underlying mathematical machinery—using spectral methods, community detection, and multi-observable significance testing on high-dimensional correlation/similarity matrices—is highly relevant to analyzing complex, structured data in condensed matter physics, such as identifying phase boundaries or distinct magnetic phases from correlation functions.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of identifying genuine population heterogeneity (subgroups) within psychometric data, moving beyond the limitations of traditional methods like Latent Class Analysis (LCA). It seeks a robust, network-theoretic approach to detect underlying modular structure.

**Main result.** The proposed pipeline, which uses community detection on similarity networks derived from factor-score projections and assesses significance using four complementary observables, successfully isolates a small subset of psychometric scales exhibiting genuine, interpretable modular structure.

**Method.** The core method involves projecting high-dimensional item-response data onto a low-dimensional factor-score space, constructing a similarity network, and then applying community detection (e.g., Leiden) while rigorously testing the significance of the detected partition against a resampling null model.

**Model / system.** The system is subject-subject similarity networks derived from psychometric data (Likert scale responses). The analysis models the data structure by first fitting a factor analysis model to obtain factor scores, which are then used to compute the similarity matrix for community detection.

**Key observables.** Modularity (Q), differential entropy of the eigenvector point cloud, overlap of within/between-community similarity histograms, and the maximum eigenvalue ($\lambda_0$) used for null model subtraction.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The number of latent factors ($F$), the number of resamplings ($T=150$), and the choice of neighborhood scales ($k_{low} \sim 0.1 N$ and $k_{high} \sim 0.8 n_{comm}$) for entropy estimation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes that genuine structure can be separated from artifacts caused by item redundancy (addressed via factor-score projection) and global response tendencies (addressed via spectral null model subtraction).

**Figures summary.** Figures summarize the performance of the four significance metrics across synthetic datasets, showing how the metrics correctly identify the null case and quantify the departure from null as cluster separation increases. The analysis also compares the CD approach against LCA performance.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first identifying the limitations of LCA, then proposing the network-theoretic alternative involving factor-score projection and spectral null modeling. It details the four complementary significance observables, validates the pipeline on synthetic data, and finally applies it to 14 real psychometric scales.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Identifying subgroups of respondents in psychometric data is traditionally addressed with Latent Class Analysis, which requires the number of classes to be specified a priori and can perform poorly when strong inter-item correlations violate local independence assumptions. We propose a network-theoretic alternative based on community detection in subject-subject similarity networks. To suppress the systematic artifacts induced by the factor structure of the items, the similarity is computed in a low-dimensional factor-score space and the null model for modularity maximisation is obtained by removing the leading (global) mode of the similarity matrix, rather than via the standard Newman--Girvan model. The significance of a detected partition is then assessed against a column-wise resampling null through four complementary observables: the modularity, the differential entropy of the eigenvector point cloud at two neighbourhood scales, and the overlap of the within- and between-community similarity histograms. On a synthetic benchmark with controlled mixture signal, all four metrics correctly identify the homogeneous case as null-compatible -- including the demanding regime of a dataset dominated by a single factor -- and exhibit a graded departure from the null as the cluster separation grows. Applied to 14 widely used psychometric scales, the pipeline isolates a small group of datasets supporting a genuine and directly interpretable modular structure, while the remaining scales fall either in a mixed-signal regime or in one compatible with a single homogeneous community. The significance analysis is independent of the specific community-detection algorithm and provides an operational way to test for modular subject-level structure in questionnaire data.

</details>

### [Records, drift, and the longest increasing subsequence of biased Gaussian random walks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29185v1)

**Authors:** J. Ricardo G. Mendonça  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29185v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29185_figures/2605.29185_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29185_figures/2605.29185_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Effective exponent θeff(p,n) = ∆log⟨Ln⟩/∆logn, evaluated at the geometric midpoint of consecutive walk lengths. For every sampled p &gt; 1/2, the curves rapidly approach θ = 1. The symmetric case p = 1/2 remains below unity, consistent with the finite-variance √n logn regime.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.29185_figures/2605.29185_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

The red dashed curve is the theoretical prediction (24), and the gray dotted line is the high-drift reference a = p. At p = 1/2, the plotted points are finite-n ratios; the limiting linear coefficients vanish. Standard errors are smaller than the markers.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.29185_figures/2605.29185_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.29185_figures/2605.29185_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Quantile–quantile plot of the standardized LIS length (Ln − ⟨Ln⟩)/sd(Ln) at n = 106 against the standard Gaussian quantile. At p = 1/2, the distribution is right-skewed and fat-tailed, consistent with the lognormal-like bulk reported for symmetric walks. For the sampled p &gt; 1/2, the points follow the Gaussian reference line within Monte Carlo scatter.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work analyzes the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) of a biased Gaussian random walk, finding that for positive drift, both the LIS length and the record count grow linearly with time. The paper highlights a sharp transition in statistical behavior at the symmetric point ($p=1/2$), where the LIS distribution changes from lognormal-like to Gaussian, providing deep insights into how drift affects extreme path statistics.

**Why it may be interesting.** While not directly quantum, the study of extreme statistics (like LIS) in random processes is foundational to understanding fluctuations in complex systems, which has analogues in quantum measurement statistics or disordered quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the growth rate and mechanism of the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) for a biased Gaussian random walk, contrasting it with the well-studied symmetric case.

**Main result.** For positive drift ($p>1/2$), the mean LIS length grows linearly ($\langle L_n(p) angle \sim a(p)n$), while the record count also grows linearly ($\langle R_n(p) angle \sim \lambda(p)n$).

**Method.** The analysis combines numerical simulations of the random walk with asymptotic analysis, comparing the LIS growth to established results for record processes.

**Model / system.** The model is a biased Gaussian random walk $X_k = X_{k-1} + \xi_k$, where the bias is controlled by $p = \mathbb{P}(\xi>0)$, leading to a drift $\mu_p$.

**Key observables.** Mean LIS length ($\langle L_n(p) angle$), mean record count ($\langle R_n(p) angle$), and the fluctuation excess $a(p) - \lambda(p)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The bias parameter $p$, the symmetric point $p=1/2$, and the drift $\mu_p$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the existence of linear asymptotic growth rates for both LIS and record counts when $p>1/2$.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the rapid transition of the effective exponent $	heta_{eff}$ to 1 for $p>1/2$, plot the linear coefficients $a(p)$ and $\lambda(p)$ vs. $p$, and show the distribution changes from lognormal-like at $p=1/2$ to Gaussian for $p>1/2$.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes the problem by contrasting biased vs. symmetric random walks. It derives the linear scaling for $p>1/2$, analyzes the record process using Spitzer's formula, and then examines the singular behavior near the symmetric point $p=1/2$, concluding with a discussion of the non-record contribution.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The longest increasing subsequence (LIS) of a random walk has so far been studied mainly for zero-mean, symmetric step increments. We numerically investigate the LIS of biased Gaussian random walks, with unit-variance increments and positive drift $μ_{p} = Φ^{-1}(p)$, where $p = \mathbb{P}(ξ>0)$. In contrast with the symmetric case, we find that for every fixed $p>1/2$ the mean LIS length grows linearly, $\langle L_{n}(p)\rangle \sim a(p)n$, with $a(p)$ increasing from $0$ at $p=1/2$ to $1$ as $p \to 1$. The record count is also linear, with coefficient $λ(p)$ given by Spitzer's formula for the mean ascending ladder epoch, and the LIS becomes increasingly aligned with this record skeleton as $p$ grows. At the symmetric point $p=1/2$, the record skeleton collapses to the Sparre Andersen $\sqrt{n}$ scale, while the LIS returns to the symmetric finite-variance $\sqrt{n}\log{n}$ regime. Near this limit, the excess $a(μ_{p})-λ(μ_{p})$ vanishes more slowly than linearly in the drift, although our data do not resolve a single power law. The empirical distribution of $L_{n}$ also changes across the singular point, from lognormal-like at $p=1/2$ to fluctuations consistent with Gaussian behavior for every sampled $p>1/2$.

</details>

### [Analytical model for structured light propagation through a turbulent atmosphere](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30304v1)

**Authors:** Konstantin Kravtsov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30304v1>

The same problem in the split-step representation: the channel is divided into a large number of random and independent chunks. Each chunk is modeled as a zero-thickness phase screen (PSi) and a free propagation region defined by the propagator ˆP. The resulting field in the end of the channel is decomposed into spatial modes to analyze their statistics. Our key proposal is to use the depicted optical-power-versus-spatial-mode encod- ing, which turns ˆP into the identity operator;...</sub>

</details>

Distribution of power among mode groups as a func- tion of N for 3 channels with fundamental-mode input and characterized by interaction strengths |Λ00L| = 0.1, 1.0, and 3.0, which is equivalent to Fried parameter values of 204 mm, 51.3 mm, and 26.6 mm respectively. Based on 120 lowest- order LG modes. The total power in these modes is 0.996, 0.95, and 0.8 respectively, while the rest is dissipated to even higher order modes.</sub>

Theoretical prediction, which does not depend on the channel length unless the beam diameter varies, is based on 28 lowest- order HG modes. Modes are presented in the following order: (0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (0,2), (1,1), (2,0), etc.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents an analytical model for structured light propagation through atmospheric turbulence, treating the interaction as a mode-coupling problem. It derives the power evolution using a matrix exponential solution based on the spectral overlap between turbulence and mode acceptance. The framework is valuable for predicting modal crosstalk in free-space optical links under various turbulence strengths.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful, analytically tractable tool for understanding modal cross-talk and energy redistribution in complex wave propagation environments, which has parallels in open quantum systems where environmental coupling leads to mode mixing.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper develops an analytical framework to model how structured light propagates through a turbulent atmosphere, specifically quantifying the power depletion from the original mode and its redistribution into neighboring spatial modes.

**Main result.** The power transfer rate scales linearly with propagation distance in a uniform channel, allowing for an exact solution via a matrix exponential. The model confirms scaling laws for mode coupling based on the mode order.

**Method.** The authors employ a split-step approach combined with a mode-based optical field representation, treating the channel effect as a cumulative action of infinitesimal phase screens.

**Model / system.** The system is free-space optical communication through a turbulent atmosphere. The model uses orthogonal mode sets (HG and LG) and derives coupling rates ($\Lambda_{ab}$) from the spatial spectral overlap between the turbulence spectrum and the mode acceptance spectrum.

**Key observables.** Average power in each spatial mode, power transfer rate, and the Rytov parameter ($\sigma_R^2$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Propagation distance ($L$), Fried parameter ($r_0$), and the turbulence spectrum (e.g., Kolmogorov scaling).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes the neglect of relative phases between spatial modes and is most accurate for weak to moderate turbulence levels.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the physical setup and the split-step representation, showing power distribution comparisons between theoretical predictions and numerical simulations for various turbulence strengths.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds the model using the split-step approach, derives the mode coupling matrix ($\Lambda_{ab}$) based on phase perturbation spectra, and presents the final evolution as a matrix exponential solution, validating results against simulations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a straightforward analytical framework for the propagation of spatial light modes through a turbulent atmosphere. Built upon the split-step approach with the mode-based optical field representation, it directly assesses how turbulence-induced phase fluctuations deplete the optical power in the original mode and re-distribute it into neighboring spatial modes. Importantly, this power transfer scales linearly with the propagation distance in a uniform channel, yielding a simple solution for arbitrary distances in the form of a matrix exponential. The transfer rate is determined by the spatial spectral overlap between the turbulence spectrum and the acceptance spectrum for a pair of interacting spatial modes. The model predicts the average power in each spatial mode and is exact when a single mode strongly dominates all others. Our predictions show reasonably good agreement with simulations up to medium-to-strong turbulence levels. The model also confirms the scalings with mode order previously known as empirical observations.

</details>

### [Dynamical Casimir photons from rotation of a nonspherical particle](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29883v1)

**Authors:** Guilherme C. Matos, Lucas Bianchi, Jeremy N. Munday, François Impens, Reinaldo de Melo e Souza, Paulo A. Maia Neto  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29883v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **1/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29883_figures/2605.29883_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Dynamical Casimir Effect (DCE) radiation from a rotating spheroid. The spheroid rotates at angular velocity Ωabout the z-axis, which is orthogonal to its symmetry axis (along the x direction). The particle’s anisotropic polarizabil- ity enables rotational sideband mixing of vacuum fluctuations, driving the emission of photon pairs that satisfy the condition ω1 + ω2 = 2Ω.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29883_figures/2605.29883_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. DCE emission of a prolate BST spheroid rotating about an axis orthogonal to its symmetry axis. (a) Emission spectrum for (from top to bottom) Ω/ωT = 10, 1.9, 1.3, 0.5, where ωT /(2π) = 0.91 GHz is the BST resonance frequency. (b) Enhancement ratio (with respect to the quasi-static limit) Γ/Γqs versus rotational frequency Ω/(2π).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.29883_figures/2605.29883_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. DCE emission from a spheroid at fixed tip velocity vb = Ωr∥= 1.5 × 105 m/s. (a) Emission rate as a function of the major semi-axis r∥for several eccentricities e. The crossover from the small-size limit (high frequencies) to the large-size one (low frequencies) takes place at r∥∼vb/ωT = 26 µm. The range shown spans rotational frequencies Ω/(2π) from 24 GHz to 0.24 GHz. (b) Emission rate as a function of the eccentricity e for different values of r∥. Each curve is normalized by its maximum value.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical study calculates the rate of dynamical Casimir photon emission generated by a spinning, non-spherical nanoparticle in free space. The emission is critically dependent on the material's optical anisotropy and can be enhanced by tuning the rotation frequency near material polaritonic resonances. Ultimately, the authors conclude that while the mechanism is theoretically sound, the predicted emission rates are too small for current experimental detection.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses the coupling between mechanical motion (rotation) and the quantum vacuum, which is a core topic in quantum optics and open quantum systems. The use of material resonances to enhance vacuum effects is highly relevant to cavity QED and quantum sensing.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the generation of dynamical Casimir photon pairs from a non-spherical particle spinning in free space, offering an alternative to traditional oscillating boundary sources.

**Main result.** The emission rate is governed by the material's anisotropy function and is maximized by tuning the rotation frequency near a polaritonic resonance. However, predicted rates remain exceedingly small for realistic materials.

**Method.** The authors developed a theoretical framework using the dipole approximation to calculate the emission rate ($\Gamma(\Omega)$) by analyzing the coupling between field components at shifted frequencies ($\omega \pm 2\Omega$).

**Model / system.** The system modeled is a neutral, non-spherical (spheroidal) particle spinning in free space. The analysis incorporates the particle's dispersive material response, characterized by the anisotropy function $\Delta(\omega) = \alpha_{\parallel}(\omega) - \alpha_{\perp}(\omega)$.

**Key observables.** The total emission rate ($\Gamma(\Omega)$) and the frequency spectrum ($d\Gamma/d\omega$) of the emitted photon pairs.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The rotation frequency ($\Omega$), the material resonance frequency ($\omega_T$), and the particle's geometry (eccentricity $e$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the dipole approximation and treats the electromagnetic field quantum mechanically. A key limitation noted is the difficulty of observing this effect in free space.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the DCE radiation from a rotating spheroid, showing the spectrum and the enhancement ratio ($\Gamma/\Gamma_{qs}$) for a BST spheroid, demonstrating resonance peaks near $\Omega \sim \omega_T$.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from the general theory of DCE from spinning anisotropic particles, deriving the emission rate based on the anisotropy function. It then analyzes different regimes (quasi-static vs. resonant) and optimizes the geometry and material parameters to maximize the predicted emission.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider a non-spherical neutral particle spinning in free space and interacting with the electromagnetic quantum vacuum. When the rotation axis is orthogonal to the particle symmetry axis, the scattered field develops frequency sidebands that induce the parametric emission of dynamical Casimir photon pairs. Under the structural constraint of a maximum tip velocity, the emission rate is maximized for a nearly spherical geometry and is further enhanced near a polaritonic resonance. For realistic material parameters, even these optimized upper bounds remain exceedingly small, setting stringent quantitative limits on free-space rotational dynamical Casimir emission with a single nanoparticle.

</details>

### [Multifractal Complexity of the Chandler Wobble and Its Anomalous Disappearance (2015--2020): A MFDFA Study](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29056v1)

**Authors:** Sebastián Jaroszewicz, Nahuel Mendez, Maria P. Beccar-Varela, Maria Cristina Mariani  
**Type:** both · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29056v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5**

Bottom row: residual polar motion xres p (left) and yres p (right), which contain predominantly the Chandler wobble plus broadband stochastic noise and are the starting point for the multifractal analysis.</sub>

The dominant peak at f ≈0.843 cpy (T ≈433 days) corresponds to the Chandler wobble. Vertical dashed lines mark the bandpass filter limits (0.743–0.943 cpy) used for CW extraction.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<sub>Figure 3 presents ACW(t) and the unwrapped phase ϕCW(t) over the full record. The extracted amplitude time series ACW(t) exhibits several noteworthy dynamical fea- tures over the observational record. Following a broad maximum between approximately 1990 and 2010 with peak amplitudes reaching ∼200 mas, the signal underwent a progressive</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.29056_figures/2605.29056_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Generalised Hurst exponent h(q) as a function of q for the four analysed series computed over the full 1962–2024 record. The clear non-linearity of h(q) and its strong q-dependence, especially for the amplitude series ACW and δACW, confirm the multifractal nature of the signals.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>
