The Chandler wobble amplitude ACW displays the largest spectral width ∆α, indicating the strongest degree of multifractality.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work uses Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis on decades of Earth's polar motion data to study the Chandler Wobble. It finds that the CW's anomalous near-disappearance is marked by a measurable collapse in its multifractal complexity, suggesting a fundamental dynamical regime change. The study highlights that multifractal metrics can serve as potential early-warning indicators for major transitions in Earth's rotation dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** The application of multifractal analysis to geophysical time series provides a powerful tool for detecting non-equilibrium transitions and critical slowing-down signatures, which are concepts relevant to understanding complex dynamics in many-body systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study characterizes the multifractal complexity of the Chandler Wobble (CW) and investigates the physical nature of its anomalous near-disappearance between 2015 and 2020.

**Main result.** The CW amplitude dynamics show a collapse in long-range persistence and multifractal spectral width during the anomaly, indicating a genuine dynamical regime change, while the amplitude and phase fluctuations remain decoupled and highly complex.

**Method.** Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MFDFA) is applied to polar motion data, supplemented by sliding-window analysis and rigorous surrogate-data testing to distinguish correlation sources.

**Model / system.** The system is the Earth's rotation pole motion, specifically analyzing the Chandler Wobble (CW) component extracted from decades of daily IERS EOP C04 polar motion data.

**Key observables.** Generalized Hurst exponents h(q), multifractal spectral width (Δα), local persistence H(t), and the CW amplitude (ACW(t)).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The anomalous period (2015–2020), the CW period ($\sim 433$ days), and the time span (1962–2024).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The multifractality originates from a combination of long-range temporal correlations and heavy-tailed excitation statistics; the decline in complexity signals a dynamical regime change rather than simple amplitude damping.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the non-linear scaling of h(q) confirming multifractality, and temporal plots illustrate the systematic collapse of H(t) and Δα(t) for geometric residuals during the anomalous period.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from global characterization of multifractality using MFDFA, to isolating the CW signal, and finally employs a sliding-window analysis to track the temporal evolution of complexity metrics across distinct geophysical regimes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Chandler wobble (CW) -- the $\sim$433-day free nutation of Earth's rotation pole -- experienced an anomalous near-disappearance between 2015 and 2020, followed by a re-excitation with an approximately $180^{\circ}$ phase reversal. Using Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MFDFA) applied to more than six decades (1962--2024) of daily IERS EOP C04 polar motion data, this study provides the first multifractal characterisation of the CW and its recent anomaly. Global MFDFA shows that the residual polar motion components and the CW amplitude are genuine multifractal processes with strongly $q$-dependent generalised Hurst exponents and broad singularity spectra. Surrogate-data tests with shuffled and phase-randomised ensembles demonstrate that this multifractality originates from the combined action of long-range temporal correlations and heavy-tailed excitation statistics. A sliding-window analysis reveals a pronounced collapse in long-range persistence and multifractal spectral width of the geometric polar motion signal several years before and during the 2015--2020 amplitude minimum, indicating a genuine dynamical regime change rather than a simple suppression of oscillation amplitude. In contrast, the amplitude- and phase-related variables retain broad multifractal spectra and stable scaling exponents across all epochs, revealing a dynamical decoupling between the geometry of the CW and the multiscale structure of its amplitude and phase fluctuations. These findings highlight the CW amplitude as an exceptionally multifractal integrator of geophysical excitation and suggest that multifractal metrics may provide early-warning indicators of major transitions in Earth rotation dynamics.

</details>

**Summary.** This work uses RIXS to map the collective charge excitations in electron-doped cuprates across their phase diagram. It reveals a continuous evolution of this excitation, transforming from a plasmon to a hybrid mode, which establishes a unified picture of charge dynamics across the Mott transition. This finding links charge fluctuations directly to lattice vibrations, offering insight into the mechanism behind high-temperature superconductivity.

**Why it may be interesting.** The concept of a charge excitation mode persisting and transforming across a Mott transition via strong coupling to lattice vibrations provides a rich model for understanding emergent quantum phases in correlated materials, relevant to open quantum systems dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study addresses the outstanding question of how collective charge excitations evolve across the Mott transition in strongly correlated systems like cuprates.

**Main result.** The charge excitation undergoes a continuous transformation from an acoustic plasmon in the metallic regime to a gapped hybrid mode, culminating in a nearly dispersionless 139 meV excitation at half filling.

**Method.** The research utilizes Resonant Inelastic X-ray Scattering (RIXS) measurements, complemented by comparisons with ARPES and Raman spectroscopy.

**Model / system.** The physical system is electron-doped cuprates, specifically $	ext{Nd}_{2-x}	ext{Ce}_x	ext{CuO}_4$, which are strongly correlated materials exhibiting unconventional superconductivity.

**Key observables.** Low-energy charge response, plasmon dispersion, gapped hybrid mode energy (139 meV), and comparison to oxygen breathing phonon energies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Doping level ($x$), energy scale (139 meV), and the transition across the Mott insulating to metallic state.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The charge excitation is understood as a hybrid mode formed by strong coupling between the plasmon and lattice degrees of freedom ($2\Omega$ excitation).

**Figures summary.** Figures show RIXS spectra tracking the charge excitation dispersion across doping levels, illustrating the transformation from plasmon to gapped/flat mode, and comparing this to phonon features.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically tracks the evolution of charge excitations across the doping phase diagram, contrasting the metallic, intermediate, and half-filling regimes, and establishing a unified picture of charge dynamics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Metallic systems with delocalized valence electrons host collective charge density oscillations known as plasmons. On the other hand, conventional insulators do not have free electrons and the low energy charge degrees of freedom are pinned to the ions. The fate of the collective charge excitations in the intermediate regime is an outstanding question. This problem is especially important for strongly correlated systems such as the layered cuprates, where unconventional superconductivity and other emergent phenomena arise from valence electrons on the border between Mott localization and itinerancy. Using resonant inelastic X-ray scattering, we track this evolution in the prototypical electron-doped cuprate Nd2-xCexCuO4. We find a continuous transformation of the low-energy charge response: from an acoustic plasmon in the metallic regime, to a gapped hybrid mode at intermediate doping, and finally to a nearly dispersionless 139 meV excitation at half filling. Remarkably, the 139 meV excitation has approximately twice the energy of the oxygen breathing phonon responsible for the dispersion kink observed in angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy, and is consistent with a putative 2-phonon excitation observed in Raman spectroscopy. These results establish a unified picture of collective charge excitations across the phase diagram of electron-doped cuprates, showing that such modes persist across the Mott transition via strong coupling to lattice degrees of freedom and revealing a missing link in the charge dynamics of carrier doped Mott insulators.

</details>

**Summary.** This work rigorously compares the Localization Landscape Theory (LLT) to Anderson localization on the Bethe lattice. The authors find that while LLT captures some features, the two transitions are fundamentally distinct, and LLT fails to reproduce the full quantum critical behavior of the Anderson model. This establishes LLT as a powerful, yet limited, tool for understanding the very low-disorder regime.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous theoretical benchmark, showing where a powerful classical analogy (LLT) breaks down when confronted with true quantum interference effects in disordered systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper compares the predictions of the Localization Landscape Theory (LLT) with the actual physics of Anderson localization, specifically on the Bethe lattice.

**Main result.** The AL transition and the LLT percolation transition are distinct, showing different critical behaviors; LLT fails to reproduce the full quantum critical properties of AL.

**Method.** The analysis uses exact solutions available on the Bethe lattice, comparing spectral properties derived from Green's functions (Anderson) against classical percolation/landscape variables (LLT).

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the Anderson model with on-site disorder potentials on the Bethe lattice, using LLT as a classical analogy for understanding the localization transition.

**Key observables.** Mobility edge ($E_{loc}$), LLT percolation threshold ($E_{perc}$), Inverse Participation Rate (IPR), Correlation functions, and Density of States (DOS).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Disorder strength ($W$), Energy ($E$), Coordination number ($K$), and the spectral shift parameter ($X$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Bethe lattice is used as an ideal, exactly solvable model, and the analysis relies on comparing asymptotic behaviors derived from cavity methods.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the phase diagram showing the LLT mobility edge versus the actual AL mobility edge, and show differences in the large-distance decay of correlation functions.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically compares the mathematical frameworks of AL and LLT on the Bethe lattice, analyzing critical exponents, spectral edges, and tail behaviors of associated probability distributions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Localization Landscape Theory (LLT) offers a classical analogy for understanding Anderson localization through an effective confining potential, whose percolation threshold has been proposed to mark the mobility edge. While this correspondence shows striking numerical agreement in three dimensions, its theoretical foundations remain an open question. In this work, we extend the analysis of the LLT on the Bethe lattice presented in~\cite{Tonetti2026}. In this setting in both the Anderson localization transition and the LLT percolation problem admit exact solutions. Our analysis reveals that the two transitions are distinct, with markedly different critical behaviors. Notably, the LLT percolation transition falls into the standard mean-field universality class, in sharp contrast with the unconventional critical behavior of the Anderson transition on the Bethe lattice. Nonetheless, the LLT framework reproduces several exact results, capturing nontrivial features of the very low-disorder regime: it predicts the position of the isolated eigenvalue, the minimal disorder at which both the LLT percolation curve and the mobility edge first appear, and the Aizenman--Warzel lower bound for localization. We also study the dependence of the LLT percolation threshold on the energy shift, evaluate the LLT prediction for the Density of States, and derive several results on the statistical properties of the variables controlling the problem. Finally, we develop an extreme-value analysis showing that the LLT prediction for the Density of States overestimates the amplitude of the tails close to the boundary of the continuous spectrum. These findings provide an exact analytical benchmark showing that, despite its geometric appeal, the LLT does not generally reproduce the quantum critical properties of Anderson localization, while still offering a powerful tool to understand its very low-disorder regime.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper rigorously analyzes the performance of BB84, SARG04, and BBM92 QKD protocols over simulated underwater quantum channels. It models the complex noise effects of the water using Kraus operators to calculate key metrics like QBER and quantum correlation. The findings demonstrate that environmental factors like turbidity and ambient light are critical limitations, dictating the achievable secure communication distance.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a detailed, quantitative analysis of decoherence and noise effects (depolarization, attenuation) on fundamental quantum communication protocols, which is crucial for developing practical quantum technologies in harsh environments.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study analyzes the performance of three Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) protocols (BB84, SARG04, and BBM92) when implemented over a realistic underwater quantum channel.

**Main result.** The maximum secure transmission distance is highly dependent on water turbidity, ambient irradiance (day vs. night), and the specific QKD protocol used, with depolarization and scattering being major limiting factors.

**Method.** The performance is analyzed by deriving analytical expressions for the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER) and Quantum Gain ($Q_\mu$), which are then validated using Monte Carlo simulations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is an underwater optical channel, modeled as a noisy open quantum system. The quantum evolution is described by a Completely Positive and Trace-Preserving (CPTP) map using Kraus operators to account for attenuation and depolarization.

**Key observables.** Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), Quantum Correlation ($\langle\sigma_x \otimes\sigma_xangle$), and Quantum Gain ($Q_\mu$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Water type (clear, coastal, turbid), ambient irradiance (daytime vs. nighttime), pupil diameter, and source position relative to Alice and Bob.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes non-turbulent channels for some derivations, and the modeling incorporates approximations for photon statistics (Poissonian) and channel noise sources (background light, dark counts).

**Figures summary.** Figures compare QBER vs. distance for BB84 and SARG04 across different water types and irradiance levels, and show the dependence of QBER and quantum correlation on source position for BBM92.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically compares the three protocols, deriving QBER formulas for each, modeling the non-unitary channel evolution using Kraus operators for damping and depolarization, and validating these analytical results against extensive Monte Carlo simulations across various environmental scenarios.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Underwater quantum key distribution (UQKD) ensures unconditional communication security based on the fundamental principles of quantum mechanics. This study examines the performance of the BB84, SARG04, and the entanglement-based BBM92 protocols under the impact of both system optical parameters and the physical effects of the underwater channel. The main performance criteria considered are the quantum bit error rate (QBER) and the quantum correlation between the communicating parties as functions of the propagation distance in three types of non-turbulent seawater: clear, coastal, and turbid. For the BBM92 protocol, the quantum channel is modeled using Kraus operators, which characterize the combined effects of attenuation and depolarization on maximally-entangled photons, allowing the derivation of our analytical expression of the QBER, based on the correlation losses between measurements. Monte Carlo simulation results validate the analytical expression of the QBER for all the studied protocols, under various water types, atmospheric conditions, and system parameters. The results determine the conditions under which QKD protocols achieve optimal performance for secure and efficient underwater quantum communications.

</details>

**Authors:** Wei Zhang, Jiangbo Luo, Nikolai Peshcherenko, Zheyu Wang, Chun Wai Tsang, Kwing To Lai, King Yau Yip, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Junxiong Hu, Yang Zhang, Swee K. Goh, A. Ariando  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29665v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5**

**Summary.** This work investigates the superconducting state of the kagome metal CsV3Sb5 by examining the effect of sample thickness. The authors demonstrate that as the material is thinned below a critical thickness, the superconducting pairing symmetry changes from conventional to a chiral state. This transition is evidenced by the emergence of non-reciprocal transport and a zero-field superconducting diode effect, establishing thin flakes as a platform for topological quantum devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** The observation of spontaneous time-reversal symmetry breaking and chiral superconductivity in a layered material via thickness control provides a clean platform for studying topological superconductivity, relevant to advanced quantum device engineering.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to resolve the pairing symmetry in the kagome metal CsV3Sb5 and understand how superconductivity evolves as the sample thickness is reduced.

**Main result.** The superconducting state undergoes a thickness-induced crossover from a conventional 3D s-wave pairing in the bulk to a 2D chiral superconducting phase that spontaneously breaks both inversion and time-reversal symmetries in ultrathin flakes.

**Method.** The research combines thickness-dependent transport measurements, zero-field superconducting diode effect (SDE) measurements, and upper critical field anisotropy studies.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the kagome metal CsV3Sb5, studied in bulk form versus exfoliated ultrathin flakes. The theoretical analysis models the superconducting state using Ginzburg-Landau functionals to predict non-reciprocal behavior and $s+is$ pairing.

**Key observables.** Nonreciprocal second-harmonic magnetotransport signals, zero-field superconducting diode effect (SDE), upper critical field anisotropy ($\Gamma$), and out-of-plane coherence length ($\xi_c$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Thickness (critical transition around 100 nm), magnetic field ($H$), and temperature ($T$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The non-reciprocal effects and SDE are fundamentally linked to the dimensional crossover and the breaking of P and T symmetries in the 2D limit.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the systematic disappearance of nonreciprocity and SDE as flakes become thicker, alongside data demonstrating the crossover of $H_{c2}$ anisotropy from 3D to 2D behavior as thickness decreases.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically compares bulk measurements (suggesting s-wave) with measurements on increasingly thin flakes, using transport diagnostics (SDE, nonreciprocity) and anisotropy analysis ($H_{c2}$, $\xi_c$) to build a case for a thickness-driven chiral superconducting phase.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Superconductivity and its potential applications are governed by the symmetry of the superconducting order parameter. In the kagome metal CsV3Sb5, most bulk studies indicate conventional s-wave pairing. However, ultrathin flakes exhibit nonreciprocal transport, in particular a zero-field superconducting diode effect, which requires broken inversion and time-reversal symmetries. Here, using thickness dependent transport measurements, we observe the emergence of non-reciprocal second-harmonic magnetotransport signals and a zero-field superconducting diode effect, accompanied by a pronounced reduction of the out-of-plane coherence length with decreasing thickness. Upper critical field measurements further reveal a dimensional crossover from three-dimensional superconductivity in bulk to two-dimensional superconductivity in thin flakes. These findings indicate a thickness-induced chiral superconducting phase that breaks both inversion and time-reversal symmetries in the two-dimensional limit. Our work not only clarifies long-standing controversies regarding the pairing symmetry in CsV3Sb5, but also establishes thin-flake kagome superconductors as a versatile platform for engineering nonreciprocal quantum devices and exploring emergent topological phases.

</details>

We note that the experimental profiles were normalised to the number of electrons the profiles represent up to 10 a.u.. We show the experimental error bars for every fifth data point. We convolute our calculations with a Gaussian...</sub>

</details>

The inset zooms into B(rz) to highlight it at the lattice vector along each high symmetry. Right column: the auto-correlation function anisotropies between the different high symmetry directions. **Summary.** This work investigates how well theoretical methods predict the electron momentum density by comparing calculated Compton profiles against experimental measurements for several elements. The authors find that while advanced methods like QSGW improve upon DFT, the inclusion of the full many-body reduced density matrix, as captured by $G^0W^0$, is essential for achieving the best agreement with experiment. This highlights the critical role of correlation effects in determining the true electronic structure.

**Why it may be interesting.** The focus on probing the many-body nature of the ground state via Compton scattering provides a unique, highly sensitive diagnostic tool to validate complex correlated electron theories beyond standard band structure calculations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Assessing the accuracy of theoretical methods in describing the ground-state many-body electron momentum density (EMD) by comparing predictions to experimental Compton scattering data.

**Main result.** The use of the many-body reduced density matrix, particularly within the $G^0W^0$ framework, significantly improves agreement with experimental Compton profiles compared to single-particle approximations like DFT or QSGW.

**Method.** Theoretical calculations employ advanced many-body perturbation theory methods, specifically QSGW and $G^0W^0$, to compute the EMD, which is then used to predict the Compton profile.

**Model / system.** The study applies these methods to several elemental materials, including Li, Si, Cr, and Ni, to test the robustness of the theoretical framework across different electronic structures.

**Key observables.** Compton profile ($J(p_z)$), Electron Momentum Density ($ho(\mathbf{p})$), and the auto-correlation function ($B(r)$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The comparison hinges on the distinction between single-particle descriptions (DFT, QSGW) and those incorporating full many-body occupation effects ($G^0W^0$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary approximations involve the use of the $G^0W^0$ self-energy approximation and the reliance on the reduced density matrix formalism to capture correlation effects.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare experimental Compton profiles and directional differences for Li, Si, Cr, and Ni against predictions from DFT, QSGW, and $G^0W^0$ across various high-symmetry directions.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically compares results for multiple materials (Li, Si, Cr, Ni), first establishing the theoretical framework (DFT vs. GW methods) and then demonstrating, through specific material examples, that incorporating the many-body reduced density matrix is crucial for matching experimental observables.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The ground-state many-body electron momentum density, which can be probed by x-ray Compton scattering, holds insights into the electronic structure of materials. Comparisons between the measured so-called Compton profiles and the theoretical ones are invaluable in assessing the successes and failures of the methodology used to generate the theoretical ground-state electronic structure. Here, we present calculations of the Compton profiles of Li, Si, Cr, and Ni using the state-of-the-art QSGW method within the Questaal package compared with density functional theory (DFT), one-shot $GW$ ($G^0W^0$) predictions and with experiment. This comparison reveals significant differences between the QSGW and $G^0W^0$ methods which we attribute to the distinction between the single particle density provided by the QSGW method and the many-body density that we construct from the $G^0W^0$ theory; although in general the QSGW description of the electronic structure is superior to that of $G^0W^0$, we find the use of the many-body reduced density matrix is key to improving the agreement of the Compton profile with experiment.

</details>

### [Non-Abelian Mixer for QAOA on Hybrid Oscillator-Qubit Quantum Processors](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30234v1)

**Authors:** Thinh Le, Hansika Weerasena, Jianqing Liu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30234v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.30234_figures/2605.30234_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

In each layer k, Ex(√π/2, ∆) couples the GKP-encoded oscillator modes to the ancilla register, the Max-Cut cost unitary UC(γk) applies graph-dependent phases, and E† x(√π/2, ∆) transfers these phases back to the GKP encoding. The local mixers U (i) M (Θk) are then applied across oscillator–qubit pairs. A final Ex(√π/2, ∆) is applied before qubit readout. Here, mi and qi denotes the i-th oscillator mode and ancilla qubit, respectively.</sub>

</details>

(c) shows the mean performance over repeated optimizations as a function of mixer depth d with N = 4 graph instance and ∆= 0.45. (d) shows the corresponding mean performance as a function of ∆at fixed N = 4 and Nmax = 10. In (c) and (d), bars denote optimal-solution probability and lines denote approximation ratio.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.30234_figures/2605.30234_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3a and 3b compare the proposed NA-QSP mixer with the standard transverse-field mixer by reporting the improvement in each metric. Here, improvement refers to the difference between the corresponding metric obtained with the NA-QSP mixer and that obtained with the transverse- field mixer. The most important observation is that the NA- QSP mixer yields a consistently positive improvement in both approximation ratio and optimal-solution probability across all</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a novel, hardware-native non-Abelian mixer designed for running QAOA on hybrid oscillator-qubit quantum processors. By implementing this mixer, the authors significantly improve the performance metrics—including solution quality and optimal sampling probability—for solving the Max-Cut problem compared to standard mixers. This work suggests a general principle: algorithmic design should leverage the specific, non-Abelian control primitives available on the target quantum hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a concrete, hardware-aware algorithmic improvement for variational quantum algorithms (QAOA) on emerging hybrid quantum hardware, which is highly relevant for researchers working on quantum control and quantum computation.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the need for advanced quantum algorithms, specifically QAOA, tailored for the unique architecture of hybrid continuous-variable (CV) and discrete-variable (DV) quantum processors.

**Main result.** The proposed hardware-native non-Abelian mixer consistently outperforms the standard transverse-field mixer, leading to measurable improvements in both the expected solution quality and the probability of finding an optimal solution for the Max-Cut problem.

**Method.** The authors developed a hybrid CV-DV QAOA ansatz utilizing a non-Abelian mixer, which was then rigorously benchmarked against the standard mixer using simulation techniques.

**Model / system.** The system models a hybrid quantum processor coupling bosonic oscillators (encoded via GKP states) to discrete qubits. The problem solved is the Max-Cut problem, governed by a specific cost Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Approximation ratio and the probability of sampling an optimal solution ($P_{opt}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Graph size ($N=4, N=5$), mixer depth ($d$), and the GKP envelope parameter ($\Delta$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the feasibility of implementing the non-Abelian mixer using native hardware primitives like conditional displacements and single-qubit rotations.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare the performance improvement of the NA-QSP mixer versus the standard mixer across different graph sizes and show the dependence of performance on mixer depth and the GKP parameter $\Delta$.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the gap in QAOA development for hybrid CV-DV systems. It then proposes the non-Abelian mixer and the corresponding ansatz. Finally, it evaluates this ansatz by benchmarking its performance against existing mixers using quantitative metrics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The realization of universal control in hybrid oscillator-qubit quantum processors enables the systematic design and implementation of quantum algorithms. However, the algorithmic development for such platforms remains at an early stage. While the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) has been extensively studied in both continuous-variable (CV) and discrete-variable (DV) quantum systems, its development in the hybrid CV-DV setting remains limited. In this paper, we propose a hardware-native non-Abelian mixer for QAOA on hybrid CV-DV quantum processors and develop a corresponding hybrid ansatz for the Max-Cut problem. We evaluate the proposed ansatz on unweighted Erdős-Rényi graphs and benchmark it against the standard transverse-field mixer using the approximation ratio and optimal-solution probability. Across all graph sizes and Fock cutoffs in our simulations, the proposed non-Abelian mixer consistently improves both expected solution quality and the probability of sampling an optimal solution relative to the transverse-field mixer. These results indicate that the proposed non-Abelian mixer is a promising building block for QAOA on hybrid oscillator-qubit platforms.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.28949"></a>
### [Order-disorder trade-off in dirty quantum systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28949v1)

**Authors:** Jinmin Yi, Chong Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28949v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.28949_figures/2605.28949_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Adapted from Ref. [11]. Example of I± notation: the spins in the interval I are shown in black; the spins in I−and I+ are shown in red and yellow; and the spins in I′ ≡ ␀ I−∪I ∪I+c are shown in blue. The region I′ is buffered from I by I±.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work proves a fundamental trade-off theorem stating that a gapped quantum system cannot simultaneously exhibit both long-range order and long-range disorder in the presence of quenched disorder. By analyzing correlation functions and rare-region effects, the authors provide a powerful, rigorous tool to classify disordered quantum phases, extending concepts like the Lieb-Schultz-Mattis theorem to dirty systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous mathematical framework connecting order and disorder parameters provides powerful diagnostic tools for characterizing topological phases in the presence of realistic disorder, which is a major challenge in condensed matter theory.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper establishes a rigorous trade-off theorem governing the coexistence of order and disorder parameters in gapped quantum spin systems subjected to quenched disorder.

**Main result.** For gapped ensembles, the system must possess either a non-zero order parameter or a non-zero disorder parameter, but not both; this trade-off extends to nearly gapped regimes.

**Method.** The proof combines probabilistic arguments, utilizing concepts like the Local Perturbation Principle and analyzing the incompatibility of local ordering and disordering events.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on one-dimensional quantum spin systems (e.g., Ising chains) with quenched disorder, analyzed via ensemble averages of correlation functions.

**Key observables.** Order parameter (long-range correlation $\langle O_i O_j angle$) and Disorder parameter (string correlator $\langle O_i O_j \prod S_k angle$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The gap size ($\epsilon$), the disorder strength, and the decay rate of rare-region probabilities ($p|R|$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the system is gapped or nearly gapped, and relies on the Local Perturbation Principle (LPPL) for bounding correlations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the notation for defining local operators on disjoint intervals ($I, I', I_\pm$) used in the rigorous mathematical proof structure.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the trade-off theorem by first establishing the necessary conditions for order/disorder parameters in gapped systems, then extending this to nearly gapped regimes, and finally applying the result to derive constraints like the disordered Lieb-Schultz-Mattis theorem.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We prove a trade-off theorem for order and disorder parameters in one-dimensional quantum spin systems with quenched disorder. For a disordered ensemble with exact Ising symmetry and average translation symmetry, any gapped ensemble must have one and only one of the following: an $O(1)$ order parameter or an $O(1)$ disorder parameter with even parity, both of the Edwards-Anderson type. The result extends to nearly gapped ensembles that accommodate Griffiths-type rare-region effects. These results offer a powerful and rigorous framework to understand the disorder effects beyond perturbative approaches. As applications, we (1) establish the existence of string order parameters for SPT phases; (2) derive a Lieb-Schultz-Mattis-type constraint for disordered ensembles, which requires a nearly gapped ensemble to spontaneously break the symmetry; and (3) discuss similar trade-off relations for disordered fermion chains, leading to an improved understanding of certain "intrinsically disordered" topological phases.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.29959"></a>
### [Quantitative semidefinite certificates for ground-state energies of Pauli Hamiltonians](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29959v1)

**Authors:** Igor Klep, Nando Leijenhorst, Victor Magron  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29959v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29959_figures/2605.29959_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Normalized smallest Krawtchouk roots controlling the convergence rates in Theorems 1 and 2. The solid curve shows ξ40,4 d+1/40, while the dashed orange curve is the asymptotic limit ϕ4(t) =</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops rigorous mathematical guarantees for approximating the ground-state energy of Pauli Hamiltonians using semidefinite programming relaxations. By adapting kernel methods from commutative algebra to the noncommutative Pauli algebra, the authors establish explicit, quantitative convergence rates for the approximation error. These results provide the first such guarantees for this important class of quantum many-body problems.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides rigorous, quantitative mathematical tools to analyze quantum many-body problems, which is crucial for understanding the computational complexity and feasibility of finding ground states in quantum simulation or quantum computation.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the need for quantitative, explicit finite-level convergence rates for semidefinite programming and noncommutative sum-of-squares hierarchies used to certify ground-state energies of Pauli Hamiltonians.

**Main result.** The authors prove explicit error bounds for both lower and upper bound hierarchies, showing the error decays at a rate controlled by the smallest root of a Krawtchouk polynomial, $\xi_{n,4}^{d+1}/n$.

**Method.** The core method involves adapting the Christoffel–Darboux kernel technique from commutative polynomial optimization to the noncommutative Pauli algebra using specialized reproducing kernels.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the $k$-local Hamiltonian problem on $n$ qubits, where the Hamiltonian is represented by a polynomial in the Pauli algebra. The results are generalized to arbitrary $k$-local Hamiltonians by embedding them into an even-weight Hamiltonian on $n+1$ qubits.

**Key observables.** The primary observables are the true ground-state energy ($\lambda_{\min}(p)$) and the bounds derived from the hierarchies ($
u_d(p)$ and $\mu_d(p)$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The convergence rate depends on the number of qubits ($n$), the hierarchy level ($d$), and the polynomial degree ($k$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The main theorems require the Hamiltonian polynomial to be even-weight (containing only even-weight Pauli terms). The analysis relies on constructing specific reproducing kernels for the Pauli algebra.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares the normalized smallest Krawtchouk roots ($\xi_{40,4}^{d+1}/40$) against the relaxation fraction $d/n$, illustrating the convergence rate towards an asymptotic limit $\phi_4(t)$.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the theory by first establishing the necessary kernel constructions for the Pauli algebra, then deriving the explicit error bounds for the lower and upper bound hierarchies, and finally generalizing the results to arbitrary Hamiltonians via qubit embedding.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The $k$-local Hamiltonian problem is a central model for quantum many-body systems and Hamiltonian complexity. Semidefinite programming and noncommutative sum-of-squares hierarchies provide systematic certificates for ground-state energies, but existing finite-convergence results give no quantitative guarantee on the accuracy of the low hierarchy levels accessible in computation. We prove explicit finite-level convergence rates for these hierarchies in the Pauli setting. For $k$-local Hamiltonians whose Pauli expansion contains only even-weight terms, we show that both the NPA-type lower-bound hierarchy and the upper-bound hierarchy on the spectral minimum have error at most $C(k)ξ^{n,4}_{d+1}/n$, where $ξ^{n,4}_{d+1}$ is the smallest root of a Krawtchouk polynomial and $C(k)$ is independent of the number of qubits $n$ and the hierarchy level $d$. General $k$-local Hamiltonians reduce to this even-weight case by adding one ancilla qubit while preserving the spectrum. The proof constructs almost-reproducing kernels for the Pauli algebra and relates their spectra to Krawtchouk polynomials, giving a noncommutative analogue of recent kernel-based convergence analyses for commutative polynomial optimization. These results provide the first quantitative finite-level accuracy guarantees for noncommutative semidefinite relaxations of Pauli Hamiltonians.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29137v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

</details>

**Summary.** This tutorial offers a comprehensive, guided introduction to Quantum Error Correction (QEC), addressing the fundamental challenge of noise in quantum computation. It systematically develops the necessary concepts—from stabilizers and syndromes to advanced code families like qLDPC and topological codes—to achieve fault-tolerant quantum computation. The paper is essential for understanding the theoretical tools required to build reliable quantum hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** It is highly relevant as it provides the theoretical roadmap for achieving fault tolerance, which is the central goal for any theoretical physicist working on scalable quantum computation.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary obstacle to building useful quantum computers is environmental noise, which necessitates the use of Quantum Error Correction (QEC) to protect fragile quantum information.

**Main result.** The tutorial provides a comprehensive, operational guide to modern QEC, covering core concepts from stabilizer codes to advanced families like qLDPC and topological codes.

**Method.** The paper develops concepts by building a mathematical framework, starting from quantum postulates, extending to classical error correction analogies, and culminating in the theory of fault-tolerant computation.

**Model / system.** The focus is on abstract quantum information models (qubits, Hilbert spaces) and various error-correcting codes (e.g., Surface Codes, qLDPC codes) designed to encode logical information across many physical qubits.

**Key observables.** Logical failure rates, code distance (d), error thresholds, and the ability to detect and correct specific error types (bit flips, phase flips).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Physical error rates ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$), required logical error rates ($10^{-9}$ or lower), and the physical qubit overhead required for fault tolerance.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The discussion assumes the existence of a physical mechanism (QEC) capable of overcoming noise, and it builds upon the mathematical formalism of quantum mechanics (unitary evolution, measurement postulates).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the basic structure of quantum circuits, showing wires, unitary gates (like CNOT), and measurement points, alongside examples of preparing entangled states like the Bell state.

**Paper structure.** The tutorial progresses logically: first establishing the mathematical foundations of quantum states and evolution; then introducing the necessity of QEC due to noise; followed by covering core concepts (codes, syndromes, stabilizers); and finally detailing advanced code families and fault-tolerance protocols.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Noise is one of the central obstacles to building useful quantum computers, and quantum error correction (QEC) provides the framework for protecting quantum information against it. Unlike classical error correction, QEC must preserve fragile quantum states without copying them, measuring them directly, or destroying the information they encode. Driven by rapid progress in both theory and experiment, this challenge has grown into one of the most active areas of quantum information science. This tutorial gives a guided introduction to modern QEC, developing the core concepts of codes, syndromes, stabilizers, decoding, and fault tolerance before connecting them to major code families and current research directions. We cover both established constructions and newer developments, including topological and subsystem codes, bosonic and qudit codes, dynamical codes, and quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes. The emphasis is on building operational understanding: explaining not only what the main objects are, but how they are used in code design, error diagnosis, decoding, and fault-tolerant computation. The tutorial is intended for newcomers seeking a first path through QEC, as well as researchers looking for a coherent reference for the concepts, code families, and tools that arise in current work.

</details>

**Summary.** This work theoretically analyzes the phase diagram of the quantum Spin-5/2 Blume-Capel model under the influence of transverse and random crystalline fields. Using mean-field theory, the authors map out how anisotropy parameters dictate the nature of magnetic ordering. The findings reveal that while most transitions are second-order, specific conditions can trigger first-order transitions, providing a detailed understanding of spin-lattice coupling effects.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed mapping of the phase diagram, particularly the identification of first-order transitions induced by anisotropy, provides rich insights into how local field anisotropies can fundamentally alter the critical behavior of quantum magnetic systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the thermodynamic properties and phase transitions of the quantum Blume-Capel model with spin S=5/2 when subjected to transverse and random crystalline fields.

**Main result.** The system generally exhibits second-order phase transitions, but specific positive anisotropy values can induce a first-order transition between different ordered spin states. Negative transverse anisotropies enhance magnetic order by raising the critical temperature.

**Method.** The analysis employs a mean-field approach based on Bogoliubov's inequality for the Gibbs free energy, minimizing the free energy with respect to the average magnetization.

**Model / system.** The system is modeled by the quantum Spin-5/2 Blume-Capel Hamiltonian, incorporating ferromagnetic exchange, longitudinal, and transverse single-ion anisotropies. The Hamiltonian includes terms related to nearest-neighbor interactions and single-ion anisotropy in $S_z$ and $S_x$ directions.

**Key observables.** Magnetization (m) versus Temperature (T) diagrams, the nature of phase transitions (first-order vs. second-order), and the critical temperatures (Tc).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Ferromagnetic exchange coupling (J), longitudinal anisotropy (D), and transverse anisotropies (Dx, Dy).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary approximation is the mean-field treatment, which neglects higher-order spin correlations between sites.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the magnetization vs. temperature plane for various combinations of anisotropy parameters, distinguishing between standard second-order behavior (solid lines) and first-order transitions (dashed lines).

**Paper structure.** The paper details the model and methodology, presents numerical results analyzing the magnetization curves across different anisotropy regimes, and concludes by summarizing the phase diagram's dependence on anisotropy signs and magnitudes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we investigate the thermodynamic properties of the quantum Blume-Capel model with spin \( S = 5/2 \) in the presence of transverse and random crystalline fields. The system is described by a Hamiltonian that includes ferromagnetic exchange interactions between nearest neighbors, a longitudinal single-ion anisotropy, and a transverse single-ion anisotropy. Using a mean-field approach based on Bogoliubov's inequality for the Gibbs free energy, we derive the fundamental thermodynamic potential and the equation of state for the magnetization. The influence of the longitudinal and transverse anisotropy parameters on the magnetic ordering and phase transitions is analyzed in detail. We present magnetization versus temperature diagrams for various combinations of the anisotropies, exploring both positive and negative values. Our results reveal that the system exhibits standard second-order phase transitions for most parameter ranges, with no evidence of tricritical behavior. However, for certain positive values of the anisotropies, the model displays a first-order phase transition within the ordered phase, characterized by a jump from a higher-spin ordered state to a lower-spin ordered state. The critical temperatures are shown to be sensitive to the magnitude and sign of the anisotropy parameters. In particular, negative transverse anisotropies favor magnetic order, raising the critical temperature, while positive anisotropies promote disorder, lowering the critical temperature. This study provides a comprehensive analysis of the phase diagram of the \( S = 5/2 \) quantum Blume-Capel model and highlights the role of transverse fields in modifying the critical behavior.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.28945"></a>
### [Quantum-Enhanced Zero-Error Communication and Storage under Positional Uncertainty](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28945v1)

**Authors:** Arnau Diebra, David González-Lociga, Mark Hillery, John Calsamiglia, Emili Bagan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28945v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.28945_figures/2605.28945_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Action of the elements of Z4 on four sites arranged on a ring.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical paper analyzes quantum communication and storage capacity when the physical arrangement of information carriers is subject to unknown permutations. It demonstrates that quantum encoding techniques, utilizing principles from representation theory, allow for a dramatic enhancement in the number of reliably transmitted messages compared to classical limits. This establishes a fundamental quantum advantage for information processing in disordered or scrambled physical architectures.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous, group-theoretic quantification of quantum advantages in communication channels that are physically realized when particle positions are scrambled, which is highly relevant for quantum memory and quantum networking.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To determine the maximum number of perfectly distinguishable messages that can be communicated or stored when the positional identity of the physical carriers is lost due to unknown reordering (positional uncertainty).

**Main result.** Quantum protocols provide a fundamental advantage over classical methods, allowing the recovery of the full identity-channel capacity ($d^n$) for cyclic uncertainty, compared to classical scaling of $dn/n$.

**Method.** The analysis uses group theory, specifically Burnside's Lemma and character theory, to compare the number of distinguishable states in classical versus quantum encoding schemes under the action of permutation groups.

**Model / system.** The model involves information carriers (qubits or $d$-level systems) whose positions are subject to a permutation group $G$ (e.g., cyclic $C_n$, dihedral $D_n$, or symmetric $S_n$).

**Key observables.** The number of distinguishable messages ($N_c$ for classical, $N_q$ for quantum, $N_a$ for ancilla-assisted).

**Important parameters / regimes.** n (number of carriers), d (alphabet size/internal states), G (the symmetry group).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The protocols assume the absence of positional metadata, and the quantum advantage relies on encoding information into coherent superpositions.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the action of group elements on site configurations (e.g., $n=4$ qubits on a ring) and show the resulting distinct classical necklace configurations.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes a general framework using group theory, applies it to specific symmetries (cyclic, dihedral, symmetric groups), and derives general formulas for the quantum advantage in message capacity.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Permutation channels model communication and storage scenarios in which the positional identity of the physical carriers is partially or completely lost, so that the transmitted information is only accessible up to an unknown reordering. Here we show that quantum mechanics can dramatically enhance zero-error communication through such channels. For cyclic reorderings of $n$ $d$-level systems, and in the absence of positional metadata, the number of classical zero-error messages scales asymptotically as $d^n/n$, whereas quantum protocols can fully recover the identity-channel value $d^n$. Ancilla-assisted protocols further increase this number to $d^{2n}/n$, enabling dense coding under positional uncertainty. We also analyze dihedral permutation channels and derive general Pólya-like formulas for the number of distinguishable messages in a broad class of permutation groups. Finally, for the symmetric group $S_n$, corresponding to complete scrambling of the information carriers, the number of distinguishable messages scales as $n^{d-1}$ classically, compared with $n^{d(d+1)/2-1}$ for quantum protocols and $n^{d^2-1}$ in the ancilla-assisted setting. Our results establish a fundamental quantum advantage for communication and storage under positional uncertainty.

</details>

### [Restoring Velocity Immunity via Dynamic Mirror Compensation in a Large-Area Dual-Atom-Interferometer Gyroscope](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29929v1)

**Authors:** Jie Gu, Yin-fei Mao, Zhan-Wei Yao, An-qing Zhang, Si-Bin Lu, Shao-kang Li, Min Jiang, Xiao-Li Chen, Min Ke, Xi Chen, Run-Bing Li, Jin Wang, Ming-Sheng Zhan

**Summary.** The research introduces a dynamic mirror compensation technique to correct for velocity-dependent phase shifts in large-area atom interferometers caused by Earth's rotation. By actively compensating for the Coriolis effect, the authors restored velocity immunity, significantly improving the gyroscope's stability and paving the way for its use in high-precision navigation and geophysics.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly addresses a major systematic error source (Coriolis coupling) in advanced quantum sensing platforms, providing a robust experimental technique for achieving higher precision in inertial measurements.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The gyroscope's inherent velocity immunity, crucial for accurate inertial sensing, is broken by the Coriolis effect induced by Earth's rotation in large-area atom interferometers.

**Main result.** The authors successfully implemented a dynamic mirror compensation scheme, reducing the phase's velocity dependence by a factor of 40 and achieving a high rotation sensitivity of $1.3 	imes 10^{-8} 	ext{ rad/s}/	ext{Hz}^{1/2}$.

**Method.** They actively rotated the Raman mirrors during the pulse sequence to cancel the time-dependent angular offset caused by the Coriolis effect, using a multi-stage calibration process.

**Model / system.** The experiment uses a large-area dual-atom-interferometer gyroscope based on $^{87}	ext{Rb}$ atoms in a Mach-Zehnder configuration. The system's phase shift is analyzed for its dependence on rotation ($\Omega$) and atomic velocity ($v'$).

**Key observables.** Interference phase ($\delta\varphi_a, \delta\varphi_b$), fringe contrast ($C$), rotation phase ($\varphi_\Omega$), and the measured stability/sensitivity ($	ext{rad/s}/	ext{Hz}^{1/2}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interference area of $21.1 	ext{ cm}^2$; velocity dependence reduction factor of 40; stability of $1.9 	imes 10^{-10} 	ext{ rad/s}$ at $4500 	ext{ s}$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on a 'decouplable calibration-compensation chain' involving contrast, phase, and dynamic compensation steps to isolate and null systematic errors.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the apparatus schematic, the restoration of maximum fringe contrast after compensation, and the comparison of the rotation phase slope vs. interrogation time before and after compensation.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the physical setup, identifies the Coriolis-induced systematic error, proposes the dynamic mirror compensation scheme across three calibration stages, presents quantitative results showing the restoration of velocity immunity, and concludes with performance metrics and future outlook.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose and demonstrate a dynamical mirror compensation scheme to restore velocity immunity in a large-area dual-atom-interferometer gyroscope. In an ideal Mach-Zehnder configuration, the phase shift is inherently immune to atomic velocity, but this property is broken by the Earth's rotation via the Coriolis effect. We overcome this by actively rotating the Raman mirrors during the pulse sequence to cancel the time-dependent angular offset. The implementation relies on a decouplable calibration-compensation chain to remove rotation-induced time-dependent terms. The scheme is validated on a dual-atom-interferometer gyroscope with an interference area of 21.1 cm^2. After compensation, the phase's dependence on atomic velocity is reduced 40-fold, and the velocity contribution to scale-factor stability is evaluated to be 0.13 ppm. The sensor achieves a rotation sensitivity of 1.3\times10^{-8} rad/s/Hz^{1/2} and a stability of 1.9\times10^{-10} rad/s at 4500 s integration, together with a common-mode noise rejection ratio of up to 459, demonstrated in a seismic event. This work removes a key obstacle to scale-factor stabilization in atom-interferometer gyroscopes and paves the way for their applications in inertial navigation and geophysics.

</details>

**Summary.** This work bridges non-equilibrium statistical physics and machine learning by showing that stochastic resetting dynamics can exactly reproduce the result of ridge regression in the stationary mean. It develops a general framework showing that the specific distribution of reset times dictates the resulting spectral filter, offering insights into the statistical cost of using random resetting versus deterministic regularization.

**Why it may be interesting.** The connection between non-equilibrium dynamics (stochastic resetting) and fundamental ML regularization techniques (Ridge) provides a novel physical interpretation for regularization, potentially guiding the design of physical learning algorithms.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper connects the mathematical framework of stochastic resetting from non-equilibrium statistical physics to the established regularization technique of ridge regression in statistical learning.

**Main result.** The stationary mean of the gradient flow process subjected to Poisson resetting exactly reproduces the ridge estimator, demonstrating a deep connection between these two seemingly disparate fields.

**Method.** The analysis uses continuous-time gradient flow equations, solving for the stationary mean by incorporating the reset rate into the differential equation, and generalizing this to arbitrary renewal reset laws.

**Model / system.** The model is linear regression, $J(\mathbf{w}) = rac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y}\|^2$, whose dynamics are governed by a continuous-time gradient flow, which is perturbed by a Poisson or general renewal resetting process.

**Key observables.** The stationary mean estimator ($\mathbf{m}_\infty$), the spectral shrinkage filter $g_S(\mu)$, and the total estimation risk $R(r; eta_0)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The reset rate $r$ (acting as the regularization parameter $\lambda$), the noise levels ($\sigma^2_\eta$, $\sigma^2$), and the spectral eigenvalues ($\mu_i$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the noise is additive and the process can be modeled by a renewal structure; the equality holds exactly only at the level of the mean, not for individual paths.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate gradient-flow trajectories showing the match at the mean, and compare risk landscapes for different reset-time laws (Poisson vs. Periodic vs. Gamma) showing how the optimal regularization parameter shifts.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the exact identity for Poisson resetting (matching ridge), then generalizing the spectral filter to arbitrary renewal laws, and finally analyzing the full statistical risk (including variance costs) by comparing different reset-time laws against the ideal ridge penalty.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We connect stochastic resetting from non-equilibrium statistical physics with ridge regularization in statistical learning. For linear gradient flow, resetting to the origin at rate $r$ produces stationary mean $(X^\top X+rI)^{-1}X^\top y$, exactly the ridge estimator with penalty $λ=r$. This uses the known Laplace-transform relationship between ridge regression and exponential-time averaging of gradient flow, with the exponential time now interpreted as the stationary age associated with Poisson resetting. We then extend this identity to general renewal reset laws: the exponential reset time distribution is the unique renewal law whose stationary mean reproduces scalar ridge in every eigendirection as an exact filter identity for every positive curvature, while non-exponential renewal laws generate alternative spectral filters. At the fluctuation level, we study a separate additive Ornstein-Uhlenbeck extension with constant diffusion, interpreted as a stylized SGD approximation. In this setting, the equality holds only at the level of the mean, since the reset process has a nonzero stationary covariance from accumulated OU noise and reset-timing variance, whereas deterministic ridge is a fixed estimator with the same center. Stylized experiments compare the deterministic renewal-induced filters directly and illustrate when filters induced by non-exponential reset-time laws can differ predictively from ridge. The results for the stationary mean and the induced spectral filters are established for continuous-time gradient flow with isotropic resetting on quadratic objectives; the covariance and risk formulas additionally assume additive noise with state-independent covariance.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes using Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQA) to solve nonlinear finite element analysis problems for hyperelastic materials. It tackles the quantum hardware limitation by approximating the nonlinear energy functional using polynomials, allowing the energy to be evaluated via parameterized quantum circuits. The work demonstrates the feasibility of this hybrid quantum-classical method for near-term quantum devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** While the application is in continuum mechanics, the core methodology—mapping a complex, nonlinear physical functional into a measurable cost function for a VQA—is a general and highly relevant technique for quantum simulation of complex quantum or classical systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the computational challenge of solving nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs) arising from hyperelastic material mechanics using quantum computation.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate the feasibility of using a Variational Quantum Algorithm (VQA) framework, combined with polynomial approximations, to estimate the potential energy functional for hyperelastic materials on near-term quantum devices.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical approach is employed where the energy functional is evaluated using parameterized quantum circuits (QNPU framework), and the resulting cost function is minimized classically using gradient-based optimizers.

**Model / system.** The physical system is hyperelastic materials governed by nonlinear elasticity, modeled via a potential energy functional derived from the strain energy density (e.g., Neo-Hookean model). The problem is reduced to a 1D finite element discretization.

**Key observables.** The potential energy functional $E[u]$ serves as the cost function, and the primary observables are the estimated energy $E$ and its gradient $
abla E$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The polynomial approximation order, the number of qubits ($n$), and the iteration count ($T_{it}$) are critical parameters influencing accuracy and complexity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The approach relies heavily on approximating nonlinear strain energy density terms using low-degree polynomial expansions (Taylor or IHT series) to ensure compatibility with current NISQ hardware.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the VQA framework setup, comparisons of different polynomial approximations (Taylor vs. IHT), and the convergence of the solution profile ($u_{VQ}$) for varying approximation orders and system sizes.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a structure detailing the physical problem (nonlinear PDEs), the necessary mathematical reductions (1D, polynomial approximations), the quantum implementation details (QNPU primitives, block encoding), and finally, numerical results comparing different approximation schemes and analyzing computational scaling.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

This manuscript explores a variational quantum formulation for nonlinear elasticity problems arising from hyperelastic material models, targeting near term noisy intermediate scale quantum (NISQ) devices. The approach leverages the potential energy structure of hyperelasticity and employs a hybrid quantum classical framework in which the energy functional is evaluated using parameterized quantum circuits and optimized through classical routines. To enable implementation on current quantum hardware, polynomial approximations of the nonlinear strain energy density are introduced, yielding a representation compatible with variational quantum algorithms. The methodology is demonstrated on a one dimensional NeoHookean material model using finite element discretizations with first and second order shape functions and nonhomogeneous boundary conditions. Numerical experiments investigate the influence of the polynomial approximation order on the accuracy and efficiency of the proposed approach, illustrating its feasibility for near term quantum devices.

</details>

</details>

**Summary.** This work develops an analytic bootstrap method to constrain the conformal data of $O(N)$ Boundary Conformal Field Theories with interacting boundaries. By solving a crossing symmetry equation derived from the two-point function, the authors determine universal constraints and calculate critical exponents for various fixed points in dimensions $d=4-\epsilon$ and $d=3-\epsilon$. This provides a unified theoretical framework for analyzing boundary critical phenomena.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous application of bootstrap techniques to boundary critical phenomena provides powerful, non-perturbative constraints on correlation functions, which is a key theoretical tool for understanding critical behavior in condensed matter systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To derive universal constraints on conformal data for $O(N)$ Boundary Conformal Field Theories (BCFTs) with interacting boundaries.

**Main result.** The authors successfully apply the analytic bootstrap to determine OPE and BOE coefficients, identifying new boundary fixed points in $d=4-\epsilon$ and providing conformal data for the tricritical $O(N)$ model in $d=3-\epsilon$.

**Method.** The core method is the analytic bootstrap, which equates two expressions for the two-point function (derived from Bulk OPE and Boundary OPE) to establish a crossing symmetry equation. This equation is solved order-by-order in the $\epsilon$ expansion.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on $O(N)$ BCFTs in dimensions $d=4-\epsilon$ and $d=3-\epsilon$, specifically analyzing models with interacting boundaries, such as those involving boundary cubic couplings.

**Key observables.** Conformal data, including scaling dimensions ($\Delta_O, \hat{\Delta}_O$), OPE coefficients ($\lambda_n$), and BOE coefficients ($\mu_m$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The expansion parameter $\epsilon$, the number of components $N$, and the coupling constants associated with boundary interactions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on the analytic bootstrap framework, which is generalized to handle non-trivial boundary interactions, and involves expanding correlation functions in powers of $\epsilon$.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the crossing symmetry equation, RG flow diagrams showing the stability of fixed points as a function of $N$, and summaries of calculated conformal data in tables.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the bootstrap equation by equating bulk and boundary expansions of the two-point function. It then solves this equation iteratively in $\epsilon$ to determine the coefficients, analyzes the stability of fixed points via RG flow, and applies the results to specific models like the tricritical $O(N)$ model.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate $O(N)$ boundary conformal field theories (BCFTs) with boundary interactions in $d=4-ε$ and $d=3-ε$ employing the analytic bootstrap. By deriving universal constraints on conformal data, we show that infinitely many operator expansions can be expressed in terms of a finite set of inputs. Complementing the analytic bootstrap with a perturbative renormalization-group analysis, we identify totally new boundary fixed points in $d=4-ε$, including non-unitary ones, generated by a boundary cubic coupling, and compute their conformal data to leading order. Moreover, we leverage our solution in $d=3-ε$ to extract, for the first time, the boundary conformal data for the tricritical $O(N)$ model. Altogether, our approach provides a unified prescription for BCFTs with interacting boundaries and streamlines the determination of bulk and boundary operator expansions.

</details>

### [Connection Factorization in Constrained Quantum Mechanics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29241v1)

**Authors:** A. Nuramatov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29241v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

</details>

**Summary.** This work develops a geometric framework to analyze quantum mechanics constrained to curved spaces using connection factorization. It demonstrates that the Laplacian operator can be factored using geometric connections, and it finds a significant cancellation of geometric terms for Dirac fermions under specific geometric conditions. This suggests the existence of underlying nilpotent differential complexes governing the quantum dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** The deep connection between geometric constraints (curvature, connection forms) and the fundamental structure of quantum operators (Laplacian, Dirac) provides powerful mathematical tools applicable to quantum systems in curved backgrounds, which is a key area in theoretical physics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the mathematical structure of quantum motion when constrained to lower-dimensional manifolds like curves and surfaces.

**Main result.** It shows that the Laplace operator admits an exact half-connection factorization, and for Dirac fermions in Fermi-type reductions, the standard scalar geometric contribution cancels out, leaving a residual spinorial structure.

**Method.** The analysis heavily relies on differential geometry tools, including orthonormal moving frames, structure equations, and connection factorization methods applied to the Laplacian and Dirac operators.

**Model / system.** The model involves quantum particles whose dynamics are restricted to curved spaces (curves, planar nets, surfaces). The mathematical machinery uses geometric objects like connection one-forms and curvature tensors.

**Key observables.** Laplace operators, quadratic geometric invariants (analogous to supersymmetric Riccati potentials), and the residual first-order spinorial derivative structure.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Curvatures (mean $H$, Gaussian $K$), connection coefficients ($k_1, k_2$), and the scalar Jensen–Koppe–da Costa contribution ($V_{dC}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the use of orthonormal moving frames and highlights that the cancellation of the scalar geometric contribution is specific to Fermi-type geometry.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the framework by first analyzing the Laplacian factorization on general manifolds, then specializing to planar orthogonal nets using structure equations, and finally examining the implications for Dirac operators and the resulting nilpotent covariant differential complexes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate constrained quantum motion on curves and surfaces using connection factorization methods. We show that Laplace operators admit an exact half-connection factorization generated by connection one-forms. The first-order part of the Laplacian is identified with the Darboux rotational connection of the orthogonal frame. Elimination of this rotational connection naturally produces quadratic geometric invariants analogous to supersymmetric Riccati potentials. Using orthonormal moving frames, we derive effective geometric contributions for planar curves, spatial curves, and embedded surfaces. We further analyze Dirac reductions using structure equations and show that for Fermi-type reductions the scalar Jensen--Koppe--da Costa contribution is cancelled in the reduced Dirac sector, leaving a residual first-order spinorial derivative structure. The resulting framework suggests the existence of hidden nilpotent covariant differential complexes naturally generated by geometric connection structures.

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical work analyzes the critical behavior of the disordered N-color Ashkin-Teller model using advanced scattering theory and the replica method. It determines the exact RG fixed points, revealing that the weak disorder regime supports a continuous line of fixed points. This demonstrates a form of superuniversality where critical exponents are robust against changes in the system's symmetry parameter N.

**Why it may be interesting.** The concept of superuniversality, where critical exponents are independent of the symmetry parameter N despite the model depending on N, is a deep theoretical result relevant to understanding universality classes in complex interacting quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to determine all solutions to the exact renormalization group (RG) fixed point equations for the disordered N-color Ashkin-Teller model.

**Main result.** The weak disorder sector exhibits a line of fixed points with Ising thermal exponents and continuously varying magnetic exponents for N>1, indicating superuniversality in random criticality.

**Method.** The methodology employs scale invariant scattering theory in two dimensions, utilizing the replica method to handle quenched disorder and solving crossing/unitarity equations derived from scattering amplitudes.

**Model / system.** The system is the N-color Ashkin-Teller model, which couples N Ising models via four-spin interactions. The disorder is quenched, characterized by two independent random coupling distributions.

**Key observables.** Critical exponents (thermal and magnetic), scattering amplitudes ($S_i$), and the scaling dimension of the energy density field ($X_\epsilon$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** N (number of colors), disorder strength parameters ($|S_4|$ and $|S_7|$), and the replica number ($n 	o 0$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the scattering framework, conformal invariance, and the assumption of energy-independence of scattering amplitudes.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate elastic scattering amplitudes, RG flows for varying N, and phase diagrams for related random bond Ising models.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first defining the Hamiltonian and the RG framework, then analyzing the pure model fixed points, and finally extending the analysis to the disordered case using the replica method to find the continuous line of fixed points.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The $N$-color Ashkin-Teller model corresponds to $N$ Ising models coupled by four-spin interactions. We consider the two-dimensional case in presence of quenched disorder and use scale invariant scattering theory to determine all the solutions of the exact renormalization group fixed points equations. The weak disorder sector is characterized by a solution that, for any fixed $N$ larger than 1, is a line of fixed points with Ising thermal exponents and continuously varying magnetic exponents. The number of fixed point solutions allowed by the symmetries of the model increases at strong disorder illustrating the growing dependence on the distributions of the two random couplings. The presence of some critical exponents which do not depend on the symmetry parameter $N$ confirms this type of superuniversality as a peculiar feature of random criticality.

</details>
