<a id="paper-2605.30013"></a>
### [Elfs, transducers and quantum walks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30013v1)

**Authors:** Simon Apers, Jérémie Roland, Yuxin Zhang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30013v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.30013_figures/2605.30013_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Coupling between a random walk and elfs process through stopping rules ν1 &lt; · · · &lt; νρ. Figure from [AP22].</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces and refines the Electric Flow Sampling (elfs) tool within the quantum walk framework. By establishing zero-error quantum transducers, the authors develop advanced algorithms to estimate graph properties like effective resistance with optimal scaling. This work demonstrates practical quantum speedups for computational tasks, such as semi-supervised learning, on structured graphs.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous development of error-free quantum transducers for graph processes provides powerful, general tools applicable to simulating complex quantum dynamics, which is foundational for quantum computation theory.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To develop improved quantum walk algorithms for estimating physical quantities like effective resistances and sampling random walk arrival distributions.

**Main result.** The authors establish zero-error transducers for key quantum operations, leading to quantum walk algorithms that achieve optimal error scaling and up-to-quadratic quantum speedups for tasks like semi-supervised learning on expander graphs.

**Method.** The core method involves refining the Electric Flow Sampling (elfs) tool using the theory of quantum transducers to implement error-free quantum operations, such as reflections about subspace intersections.

**Model / system.** The study is set on undirected graphs $G=(V, E, w)$, analyzing quantum walks defined on the edge space $\ell(E)$. Key concepts include the electric flow state $|fangle$ and the quantum walk operator $U$.

**Key observables.** Effective Resistance ($R_s$), Hitting Time ($HT_s$), Escape Time ($ET_s$), and the quantum speedup factor for learning tasks.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The error tolerance ($\epsilon$) and the graph structure (e.g., expander graphs) dictate the complexity scaling.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The development relies heavily on the theoretical framework of zero-error quantum transducers and the ability to bound various hitting times on graphs.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the coupling between the elfs process and random walks via random stopping times, and theorems detail the structure of the quantum walk operator transduction.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the zero-error transducer for elfs (Theorem 1.1), then applying this to estimate effective resistance (Theorem 1.2), and finally simulating the elfs process to achieve speedups for learning tasks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electric flow sampling (elfs) is a new tool in the quantum walk toolbox and a useful primitive for solving search, sampling and optimization problems on graphs. We refine this tool by showing that there exists a zero-error transducer for implementing elfs. More broadly, we establish a zero-error transducer for reflecting about the intersection of two subspaces, yielding an errorfree transducer version of the effective gap lemma. Building on this result, we obtain improved quantum walk algorithms for estimating effective resistances and span program witness sizes with an optimal error scaling, and for sampling from the random walk arrival distribution, via the composition of many elfs. Using this last algorithm, we obtain an up-to-quadratic quantum speedup for semi-supervised learning on expander graphs.

</details>

### [Evaluating Parameter Transfer in FALQON Across Graph Families](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29917v1)

**Authors:** Alisson dos Passos Fumaco, Marcos Vinicius Reballo, Fernando Augusto Caletti de Barros, Gabriel Fernandes Thomaz, Eduardo I. Duzzioni  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29917v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29917_figures/2605.29917_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1. Representative transfer trajectories for dense 14-node Erd˝os–R´enyi recipients (p ∈{0.8, 0.9, 1.0}) from 8-node donor graphs. The transferred mean approximation-ratio curves (blue) exhibit narrow variance and mono- tonic growth, consistently matching or exceeding the donor training trajec- tories (red).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29917_figures/2605.29917_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2. Representative transfer trajectories for sparse 14-node Erd˝os–R´enyi recipients (p ∈{0.2, 0.3, 0.4}) from 3-regular donor graphs of sizes 8, 10, and 12. The transferred curves (blue) remain below the donor references (red) for p = 0.2, with the gap narrowing as recipient density increases.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.29917_figures/2605.29917_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3. Comparison of transfer performance across donor sizes for (a) 3- regular and (b) Erd˝os–R´enyi donors (trained at p = 0.5). The substantial overlap in error bars indicates that transfer quality is resilient to donor size, achieving highly competitive and statistically comparable performance even with 8-node donors.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how parameters learned by the FALQON quantum optimization algorithm can be reused (transferred) from small test graphs to solve larger, different graph problems like Max-Cut. The key finding is that the target graph's structure, not the source graph's size, determines success. This suggests a pathway to significantly reduce the computational overhead required for scaling quantum optimization algorithms.

**Why it may be interesting.** The findings on parameter transferability and resilience to donor size are crucial for developing scalable quantum algorithms, suggesting that small, cheap computations can provide robust parameters for much larger, resource-intensive problems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper evaluates the parameter transferability of the FALQON quantum optimization algorithm when applying parameters learned on small graph instances to optimize larger or different graph structures.

**Main result.** Transfer success is primarily dictated by the structural properties of the recipient graph, showing high resilience to the size of the donor graph, especially for dense target graphs.

**Method.** The authors use an empirical study comparing parameter sequences optimized on small donor graphs (e.g., 8-node) versus larger recipients (14-node) across 3-regular and Erdős–Rényi graph families.

**Model / system.** The problem is Max-Cut, formulated as an Ising Hamiltonian solved using the FALQON algorithm, which treats optimization as a closed-loop control problem governed by a time-dependent Schrödinger equation.

**Key observables.** Approximation ratio (solution cut value relative to the exact optimum), transfer success, and convergence trajectories.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Donor sizes ($n \in \{8, 10, 12\}$), recipient size ($n'=14$), graph density ($p$), and graph families (3-regular, Erdős–Rényi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes exact state-vector simulation to isolate parameter transferability from hardware noise, and it notes that performance is highly resilient to donor size.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate representative transfer trajectories, showing successful transfer for dense recipients and a clear performance gap for sparse cross-family cases, while also demonstrating parameter overlap across different donor sizes.

**Paper structure.** The work systematically evaluates parameter transferability across varying problem sizes and Hamiltonian families, comparing results for dense versus sparse graph structures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We evaluate FALQON parameter transfer for Max-Cut, transferring sequences from small donors ($n \in \{8,10,12\}$) to 14-node recipients. Using 3-regular and Erdős-Rényi families, we show that transfer success is dictated by the recipient graph, not the donor. Transfer excels for dense recipients -- achieving high approximation ratios regardless of the donor -- but remains challenging in sparse cross-family cases. Crucially, performance is highly resilient to donor size, with 8-node donors matching larger instances. Thus, cheap small graphs can provide robust parameters for larger targets, significantly reducing the measurement overhead of the feedback loop.

</details>

### [High-Fidelity ROI CT Reconstruction with Limited Quantum Resources via Hybrid Classical-Quantum Refinement](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29472v1)

**Authors:** Hyunju Lee, Jeonghwa Lee, Kyungtaek Jun  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29472v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29472_figures/2605.29472_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Overview of the proposed hybrid ROI-refinement pipeline for Sample 1 using the SART+QTR configuration. SART first produces the global coarse estimate 𝑰𝒈𝒍𝒐𝒃𝒂𝒍. After zero-masking the ROI in 𝑰𝒈𝒍𝒐𝒃𝒂𝒍, its projection image is subtracted from the experimental projection image 𝑷(𝜽, 𝒔) to form the residual projection image 𝑷𝒓𝒆𝒔(𝜽, 𝒔). The ROI is then refined by solving a QUBO with the D-Wave hybrid solver, and the optimized ROI is inserted back into the coarse image to obtain the final refined reconstruction.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29472_figures/2605.29472_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3. Final reconstruction results for the three phantom samples. Samples 1 and 2 were defined on 𝟏𝟐𝟎× 𝟏𝟐𝟎 grids, whereas Sample 3 was defined on a 𝟏𝟖𝟎× 𝟏𝟖𝟎 grid; the ROI size was 𝟔𝟎× 𝟔𝟎 in all cases. Each row corresponds to one sample, and each column shows the ground truth, QTR+QTR, FBP+QTR, and SART+QTR results. For Sample 3, the FBP+QTR and SART+QTR results were obtained using a denser angular sampling in the coarse- reconstruction stage, while the second-stage ROI refinement was performed with the same residual-based QTR formulation.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.29472_figures/2605.29472_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. ROI absolute difference maps |𝑮𝑻−𝒓𝒆𝒄𝒐𝒏𝒔𝒕𝒓𝒖𝒄𝒕𝒊𝒐𝒏| for the three phantom samples. Samples 1 and 2 have size 𝟏𝟐𝟎× 𝟏𝟐𝟎, whereas Sample 3 has size 𝟏𝟖𝟎× 𝟏𝟖𝟎; the ROI size is 𝟔𝟎× 𝟔𝟎 in all cases. Each row corresponds to one sample, and each column corresponds to one reconstruction pipeline. Lower intensity indicates smaller reconstruction error inside the selected ROI. In QTR+QTR, both stages used 60 projection views, and the detector-position dimension was reduced to 60 before the first-stage QTR reconstruction. For Sample 3, the FBP+QTR and SART+QTR results used 180- view coarse reconstruction and 60-veiw second-stage QTR refinement.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.29472_figures/2605.29472_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig.4. . Final reconstruction results for the phantom sample. Sample was defined on a 𝟏𝟖𝟎× 𝟏𝟖𝟎 grid; the ROI size was 𝟔𝟎× 𝟔𝟎 in all cases. Each row corresponds to one sample, and each column shows the ground truth, QCSTR+QCSTR, FBP+QCSTR, and SART+QCSTR results.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.29472_figures/2605.29472_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 5. X-ray projection images of a mixed sample composed of a high- X-ray-MAC inner specimen embedded within a low-X-ray-MAC outer sample. To investigate the detectability of an internal crack, an artificial crack was introduced into the inner specimen, and projection images were obtained at two angles differing by 90°: (a) and (b).</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper addresses the challenge of performing high-fidelity CT reconstruction with limited quantum resources by proposing a hybrid ROI refinement framework. It first uses classical methods to establish a stable global image estimate, and then applies quantum optimization only to the Region-of-Interest (ROI) using a residual projection formulation. The results demonstrate that this targeted quantum refinement strategy is highly effective and superior to purely quantum approaches for complex, real-world imaging data.

**Why it may be interesting.** While the application is in medical imaging, the core methodology—using classical computation to stabilize a global background estimate before applying quantum optimization to a localized, constrained subspace—is a generalizable paradigm for hybrid quantum-classical solvers in inverse problems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core challenge is achieving high-fidelity reconstruction of a Region-of-Interest (ROI) in Computed Tomography (CT) using quantum optimization techniques when quantum resources (qubits) are severely limited.

**Main result.** A hybrid framework combining classical global reconstruction (like SART) with quantum refinement applied only to the ROI significantly outperforms purely quantum methods, with SART+QTR/QCSTR achieving the lowest reconstruction error.

**Method.** The authors propose a two-stage hybrid approach: first, a coarse global image is reconstructed classically; second, the problem is reformulated as a residual projection-image minimization, which is then solved using quantum optimization (QUBO) only over the ROI.

**Model / system.** The physical system is CT reconstruction, modeled by the Radon transform, where the inverse problem is solved by minimizing discrepancies between measured projections and projections derived from the reconstructed image. The quantum part is formulated as a QUBO problem.

**Key observables.** Root Mean Square Error ($	ext{RMSE}_{	ext{ROI}}$) and Mean Absolute Error ($	ext{MAE}_{	ext{ROI}}$) are the primary metrics used to quantify reconstruction fidelity in the ROI.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The study is particularly relevant under reduced-angle or sparse-view measurements, which exacerbate ill-posedness in the inverse problem.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that the global background can be reliably stabilized by classical methods, allowing quantum resources to be efficiently focused on local, high-detail refinement.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the hybrid pipeline flow, showing the progression from the global estimate to the residual projection image, and comparing final reconstructed images for various phantom samples.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the limitations of full-image quantum reconstruction, proposes the hybrid ROI refinement framework, details the residual projection-image formulation, and validates the method by comparing multiple classical/quantum pipelines on complex phantom samples.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum optimization for computed tomography (CT) reconstruction is constrained by the number of binary variables required for image representation, making direct whole-image quantum reconstruction difficult for large or structurally complex objects. We propose a hybrid region-of-interest (ROI) refinement framework in which a coarse global image is first reconstructed by quantum tomographic reconstruction (QTR) and quantum compressed sensing tomographic reconstruction (QCSTR), filtered backprojection (FBP), or simultaneous algebraic reconstruction technique (SART), and quantum optimization is then applied only to the selected ROI through a residual projection-image formulation. This strategy reduces the effective QUBO size while preserving high-fidelity reconstruction in the target region. Experiments on three discrete phantom samples show that both QTR/QCSTR+QTR/QCSTR and SART+QTR/QCSTR achieve accurate ROI reconstruction for moderate-size cases under a reduced-angle setting. For the largest and most complex sample, the quality of the coarse global estimate becomes critical, and the best result is obtained when a stable classical coarse reconstruction is combined with second-stage ROI-only QTR/QCSTR. Among the tested pipelines, SART+QTR/QCSTR achieves the lowest average ROI RMSE and MAE. The results indicate that the practical advantage of quantum-assisted CT reconstruction lies in reserving quantum optimization for local refinement while using classical reconstruction to stabilize the global background.

</details>

The median is shown as a black horizontal line. Whiskers extend to 1.5 × IQR, and black dots denote outliers.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.29242_figures/2605.29242_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 6(a) displays the expectation values for up to five iterations, where step = 3 corresponds to the first search peak. Within four steps, our extrapolation outperforms others in approaching the ideal values. At step = 5, all extrapolation approaches fail, presumably because the circuit depth is excessive, causing noise to completely alter the output structure. Fig. 6(b) focuses on sampling experiments at the first search peak to assess the stability of our model. We performed 1e6 shots per circuit to obtain extrapolated values and repeated the entire procedure ten times. To mitigate readout errors, we adopted</sub>

</details>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a novel Hybrid Gaussian-exponential Zero-Noise Extrapolation (G-E ZNE) technique to mitigate gate errors in quantum circuits with periodic structures. By leveraging a Central Limit Theorem proof showing noise scaling follows a log-normal distribution, the method offers superior error suppression compared to existing state-of-the-art techniques. This is crucial for advancing reliable quantum computation on current noisy hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** The rigorous mathematical derivation connecting noise scaling to a log-normal distribution provides a powerful, theoretically grounded tool for error mitigation in near-term quantum hardware.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal is to suppress gate errors in current Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) hardware, particularly for quantum circuits exhibiting periodic structures.

**Main result.** The proposed Hybrid Gaussian-exponential Zero-Noise Extrapolation (G-E ZNE) method significantly reduces bias compared to previous techniques, especially for deeper circuits.

**Method.** The authors derive a hybrid extrapolation model by analyzing the noise amplification factor's statistical behavior, which is shown to approach a log-normal distribution via a Central Limit Theorem.

**Model / system.** The work analyzes quantum circuits, specifically focusing on those with periodic structure, under Pauli diagonal errors. The theoretical framework involves analyzing the transfer of Pauli operators via an approximate Markov process.

**Key observables.** Expectation values of Pauli operators ($\langle P_i angle$), circuit fidelity ($F$), and the noise amplification factor ($\lambda$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Circuit depth/number of periods ($m$), noise strength ($\epsilon$), and the extrapolation parameter ($k$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the noise scaling factor follows a log-normal distribution, and the derivation relies on approximating the Markov process for Pauli operator transfer.

**Figures summary.** The notes do not detail specific figures, but mention performance assessment on Ising dynamics, random circuits, and Grover search, showing error reduction vs. depth.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by reviewing existing ZNE techniques (sZNE, IC-ZNE, pZNE), deriving the theoretical basis for the hybrid model using Markov process analysis and the Central Limit Theorem, and finally validating the model's performance on various quantum circuit benchmarks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Zero-noise extrapolation provides a practical means of suppressing gate errors in current noisy intermediate-scale quantum hardware. The accuracy of the zero-noise estimate depends sensitively on the fidelity of the assumed noise model to the actual error scaling. This work introduces a hybrid Gaussian-exponential extrapolation scheme tailored for quantum circuits with periodic structure, which are ubiquitous in quantum algorithms. Under Pauli diagonal errors, by constructing and analyzing an approximate Markov process for the transfer of Pauli operators, we prove a central limit theorem: the noise amplification factor weakly approaches a log-normal distribution, which motivates augmenting the standard exponential model with Gaussian variance corrections. The resulting model requires no prior noise characterization and applies directly to arbitrary periodic circuits. Performance is assessed on Trotterized Ising dynamics, random circuits, and Grover search algorithm using Qiskit noise simulators. For moderate to large circuit depths, the hybrid model yields measurable reductions in bias relative to previous extrapolation variants, indicating its utility for error mitigation on near-term quantum hardware.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.30238"></a>
### [Indefinite Causal Order Reverses the Real-Complex Hierarchy](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30238v1)

**Authors:** Jacopo Surace, Shintaro Minagawa, Ravi Kunjwal  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30238v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.30238_figures/2605.30238_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Real–complex hierarchy across causal assumptions. For fixed definite multipartite causal structures, QT is strictly richer than RQT [4]. For bipartite definite-order correlations, RQT and QT coincide [7, 8]. For process correlations with indefinite causal order, the hierarchy reverses: RQT is strictly richer than QT, as shown in this work. The point p⋆denotes the separating correlation constructed here.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical paper explores how quantum correlations are constrained by the assumed causal order and the underlying mathematical structure of quantum theory. It proves that when causality is indefinite, the physical constraints of Real Quantum Theory allow for strictly more correlations than those predicted by standard Complex Quantum Theory. This reversal of the complexity hierarchy is a key finding in quantum process theory.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a deep, operational understanding of how fundamental assumptions about physical symmetries (like reality constraints) can fundamentally alter the correlations achievable in quantum systems, especially when causality itself is fuzzy.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether causal relations can be subject to quantum indefiniteness and compares the operational distinction between real and complex quantum theories under such indefinite causal order.

**Main result.** Under indefinite causal order, Real Quantum Theory (RQT) realizes strictly more process correlations than standard Complex Quantum Theory (QT), reversing the hierarchy observed for fixed causal orders.

**Method.** The analysis uses the process-matrix framework to characterize achievable process correlations under symmetry constraints, comparing those derived from unitary (Twirled) and antiunitary (Real) symmetries.

**Model / system.** The study models quantum processes using process matrices in finite-dimensional bipartite qubit systems, comparing the constraints imposed by complex quantum theory (QT) versus real quantum theory (RQT).

**Key observables.** The set of achievable process correlations ($	ext{Cproc,fin}$), the Lazy Guess Your Neighbour’s Input (LGYNI) causal inequality, and the structure of the process matrix in the Pauli basis.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Finite dimensionality of the process matrices; the nature of the symmetry constraint (unitary vs. antiunitary).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is restricted to finite-dimensional process correlations and relies on the operational definition provided by the process-matrix framework.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the hierarchy of correlations across different causal assumptions (definite vs. indefinite) and compare the constraints imposed by different symmetries.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds by first establishing the equivalence of QT correlations with those from twirled theories, then proving that the antiunitary symmetry (RQT) imposes strictly weaker constraints, leading to the reversal of the QT/RQT hierarchy under indefinite causality.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Can causal relations be subject to quantum indefiniteness, similar to other physical properties? The process-matrix framework formalises this possibility: valid processes are defined by what local laboratories can implement, without assuming a global causal order. Standardly, the local labs are assumed to implement arbitrary quantum instruments. We ask what happens when symmetries restrict these local operations. Symmetry constraints, such as those arising from missing reference frames, superselection constraints, or the antiunitary symmetry defining real quantum theory, enlarge the admissible process cone. Do these extra processes generate genuinely new correlations? We prove a sharp dichotomy: no for any finite unitary symmetry, yes for real quantum theory. Recent work has shown that, under fixed and definite causal order, complex quantum theory is strictly richer than real quantum theory. Our work shows that this hierarchy is reversed under indefinite causal order: real quantum theory realizes strictly more process correlations than complex quantum theory.

</details>

### [Open Problem: Separating Geometric and Algorithmic Compression via Cayley-Table Completion](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29885v1)

**Authors:** Dongsung Huh  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29885v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<img src="2605.29885_figures/2605.29885_page6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This work tackles the fundamental theoretical challenge of moving deep learning beyond continuous geometric assumptions to robustly discover discrete, exact algebraic laws. By framing the problem as Cayley-table completion, the authors propose using specialized tensor factorization techniques to test for underlying discrete axioms like associativity. This research aims to provide the mathematical tools necessary for AI to autonomously derive fundamental physical laws.

**Why it may be interesting.** While highly theoretical, the focus on finding rigorous inductive biases for machine learning has implications for modeling complex, discrete physical systems where simple continuous approximations fail.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the theoretical gap between modern deep learning's reliance on continuous geometric compression and the need to model discrete, exact algorithmic rules.

**Main result.** It proposes Cayley-table completion as a canonical testbed to formally establish recovery bounds for discrete algebraic structures, bridging continuous and discrete learning theory.

**Method.** The authors use operator-valued tensor factorizations regularized by a flatness prior to implicitly encode and test for discrete algebraic axioms, contrasting this with standard low-rank geometric regularization.

**Model / system.** The primary model is Cayley-Table Completion, which involves recovering a discrete binary operation (a group structure) from partial observations, serving as the discrete analogue to matrix completion.

**Key observables.** Exact discrete associativity, low linear rank (geometric bias), and the formal recovery bounds for the completion problem.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The regularization strength of the flatness prior ($\mathcal{H}(\Theta)$) and the sample size ($O(n \log n)$ entries).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The core assumption is that algorithmic complexity minimization can be formalized and tested using continuous optimization techniques like flatness priors.

**Paper structure.** The paper is structured around identifying the theoretical divide between geometric and algorithmic compression, proposing Cayley-table completion as the testbed, detailing the tensor factorization approach, and concluding with open problems regarding formal separation theorems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Modern statistical learning theory and deep learning characterize generalization primarily in terms of continuous capacity control (e.g., norm-based regularization, margin maximization, low-rank bias). While highly successful in continuous domains, deep learning consistently fails to extrapolate exact algorithmic or discrete algebraic rules, reflecting a missing inductive bias toward algorithmic complexity minimization. We propose the Cayley-table completion as the canonical testbed for this missing bias, serving as the discrete algebraic counterpart to matrix completion. Just as matrix factorization combined with weight decay yields an implicit geometric bias toward low linear rank, recent results demonstrate that operator-valued tensor factorizations paired with a flatness prior yield an implicit algorithmic bias toward exact discrete associativity. We pose the open problem of establishing formal exact recovery bounds for Cayley-table completion, and challenge the community to generalize continuous flatness priors to autonomously discover broader discrete algorithmic axioms without combinatorial search.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.29969"></a>
### [Prototype-Guided Latent Alignment for Data-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Molecular Foundation Models](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29969v1)

**Authors:** Rushikesh Pawar, Harshit Rawat, Ayush Kumar, Phani Motamarri  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29969v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29969_figures/2605.29969_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Prototype latent alignment workflow: The pipeline proceeds in three stages to produce a fine-tuned model. Prototype Extraction: Latent representations (hj) from a source subset (D′ s) are projected via PCA and clustered using a Gaussian Mixture Model to obtain source interaction prototypes (µI, ΣI). Prototype Assignment: Target samples (Dt) are passed through the foundation model to obtain latents (hj), which are similarly projected, and assigned to the closest interaction prototype via Mahalanobis distance (dM), entirely within the interaction latent space. Prototype Alignment: The assigned distance (dM) is minimized by aligning target nodal energy latents (ej) with the...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29969_figures/2605.29969_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Architectural overview of MACE with interaction and nodal energy latents. For atom j in molecule i, its local 1-hop neighborhood is embedded into an initial interaction latent h(0) j . This representation is iteratively refined through L interaction blocks, producing updated latents h(l) j . At each stage, readout networks map these interaction latents to nodal energy latents. These contributions, together with atomic energy terms, are aggregated to obtain the final nodal energy, from which total energy is computed and forces are derived as energy gradients with respect to atomic positions (Figure inspired by Batatia et al. [2022]).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.29969_figures/2605.29969_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Source and target molecules used in the rMD17 transfer setup in ST1.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a prototype-guided latent alignment technique to improve the data efficiency of fine-tuning Molecular Foundation Models (MLIPs). By anchoring the learning process to structural prototypes derived from source data, the method mitigates overfitting and poor generalization when applied to novel, data-scarce chemical systems. It demonstrates superior predictive accuracy compared to standard fine-tuning baselines.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter potentials, the core methodology—using latent space clustering and alignment to enforce inductive biases from source data onto scarce target data—is highly relevant to developing robust ML models for complex quantum many-body systems where data is expensive.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Large-scale pre-trained foundation models for MLIPs fail to generalize to out-of-distribution (OOD) target materials, especially when labeled data is scarce.

**Main result.** The proposed prototype-guided alignment method significantly improves predictive accuracy in low-data regimes, achieving up to an 18% reduction in energy MAE over standard fine-tuning.

**Method.** The approach aligns target atom energy contributions to structural prototypes derived from the source domain by minimizing a novel Nodal Energy Alignment Loss (L_NEAL).

**Model / system.** The study focuses on Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs) implemented via Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) like MACE and SchNet, applied to molecular systems.

**Key observables.** Energy Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Force MAE (reported in meV or meV/Å).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low-data regimes (e.g., 5-20 training examples), alignment loss weight (lambda_NEAL), and the epoch threshold for loss application (N_NEAL).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes that local structural similarities between source and target domains imply analogous energy contributions, allowing for prototype-based guidance.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the three-stage workflow: Prototype Extraction (clustering source latents), Prototype Assignment (mapping target latents to nearest prototype), and Prototype Alignment (minimizing energy latent distance).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of OOD generalization in MLIPs, details the prototype-based alignment mechanism (including loss formulation), presents experimental validation on benchmarks like rMD17 and MACEOFF23, and concludes with ablation studies confirming the necessity of the alignment term.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have transformed materials discovery by leveraging graph neural networks (GNNs) to predict material properties with near density functional theory (DFT) accuracy. While large-scale pretrained foundation models offer transferable baseline representations, they frequently struggle to generalise to out-of-distribution (OOD) target systems -- a common challenge in modelling complex or chemically diverse materials. Fine-tuning is the standard remedy, but the high cost of generating DFT-labelled configurations confines adaptation to data-scarce regimes, where over-parameterised GNNs amplify overfitting and degrade target-domain performance. To address this, we propose a prototype-based alignment approach for data-efficient fine-tuning of MLIPs. Our method identifies local structural similarities between the source and target domains by grouping atoms with analogous chemical environments based on their latent representations. Each target-domain atom's energy contribution is aligned to its source-domain prototype, introducing an inductive bias that anchors fine-tuned representations to the pretrained structure, encouraging effective reuse of learned interactions and improving generalisation without restrictive assumptions on the target chemistry. We evaluate our method on the rMD17 benchmark using equivariant MACE and invariant SchNet across varying data budgets, and extend evaluation to the MACE-OFF foundation models on the SPICE dataset. Our approach consistently improves predictive accuracy in the low-data regime, reducing energy MAE by up to 18% over standard fine-tuning baselines.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.29944"></a>
### [Quadratic Sums-of-Powers for Fixed-Parameter Tractable Quantum-Circuit Simulation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29944v1)

**Authors:** Alexis de Colnet, Floris Geerts, Rihan Hai, Alfons Laarman, Joon Hyung Lee, Guillermo A. Pérez  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29944v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.29944_figures/2605.29944_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: A tensor-network graph and its corresponding line graph.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.29944_figures/2605.29944_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: The SOP variable graph GC for the circuit C of Example 1 after pinning |000⟩→|000⟩. Every edge carries the constant cross-term sign ω4 8 = −1, contributed as (−1)xuxv. Vertices carry unary phases ωbv 8 : the only nontrivial one here is bx2,1 = 1 (from the T gate), giving the phase ω8 = eiπ/4 at x2,1 = 1.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops a novel, highly efficient algorithm to simulate quantum circuits by reformulating the amplitude calculation as evaluating a quadratic Sum-of-Powers (SOP) polynomial. The complexity is shown to depend only on the rank-width of the associated variable graph, which is proven to be a superior structural measure compared to treewidth or linear rank-width for this problem.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful, structure-aware tool for quantum simulation that moves beyond general graph parameters like treewidth, offering a potentially much tighter complexity bound for specific quantum circuits.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of efficiently computing the output amplitude of a quantum circuit, which is mathematically equivalent to evaluating a quadratic Sum-of-Powers (SOP) polynomial over binary variables.

**Main result.** They introduce an algorithm whose time complexity is exponential only in the rank-width of the associated path-variable graph, providing a provably superior simulation bound compared to methods based on treewidth or tensor network contraction complexity.

**Method.** The core method is a dynamic programming approach parameterized by the rank-width of the SOP variable graph, which is optimized by transforming the residue counting problem into the Fourier domain to remove the modulus factor from the exponent.

**Model / system.** The system is general quantum circuits, specifically those built from Hadamard and diagonal gates, and applicable to the universal Clifford+T gate set. The simulation is framed as calculating the amplitude $\langle z|C|yangle$ by mapping it to a quadratic SOP form.

**Key observables.** The output amplitude $\langle z|C|yangle$, which is determined by the count $N_j$ of assignments $\mathbf{x}$ such that the quadratic SOP polynomial $f(\mathbf{x})$ equals $j \pmod{r}$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The rank-width ($	ext{rw}$) of the SOP variable graph $G_C$ is the critical structural parameter governing the complexity. The modulus $r$ (e.g., $r=8$ for $\{H, T, CZ\}$) is also crucial.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the circuit can be successfully mapped to a quadratic SOP form, and the complexity improvement relies on the rank-width being significantly smaller than the treewidth or linear rank-width.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the relationship between the SOP variable graph $G_C$ and the tensor-network line graph $L(NC)$, and demonstrate the separation between rank-width, linear rank-width, and treewidth using specific graph constructions.

**Paper structure.** The paper first establishes the SOP viewpoint for quantum simulation, compares this to existing methods (tensor networks, BDDs), identifies rank-width as the key structural parameter, and then develops and analyzes a specialized dynamic programming algorithm parameterized by this rank-width.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Strongly simulating a quantum circuit, that is, computing an output amplitude, amounts to summing the circuit's Feynman paths, a weighted count over assignments to the Boolean ``path'' variables. The circuit's gates induce correlations among these variables, forming a graph whose structure determines the hardness of the simulation task. This sum-of-powers viewpoint underlies recent simulators built on knowledge-representation tools from artificial intelligence, namely binary decision diagrams and weighted model counting. We show that the structural quantity most accurately governing the difficulty is the rank-width of the path-variable graph, and we give an algorithm that evaluates the amplitude in time that is exponential only in this rank-width and polynomial in the circuit size. Rank-width can be far smaller than the widths that control competing methods: as corollaries, our algorithm reproduces a recent decision-diagram simulation breakthrough as a special case and matches the Markov--Shi tensor-network contraction bound. To complement this, we exhibit circuit families on which our algorithm provably beats both competing methods. The new method applies to every circuit built from Hadamard and diagonal gates, in particular to circuits over Clifford+T. In practical terms, general-purpose decision-diagram and model-counting tools can serve as the workhorse, with our specialized algorithm dispatched to exploit a small rank-width of the associated graph when it is present.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29774v1>

(b) Difference between Harris and KS total energy at Li–H distance 1.55 Å with different ionicity.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper presents quantum algorithms designed to accelerate DFT calculations by proposing methods that eliminate the need for costly electronic density readout. By employing techniques like the Harris functional and wavefunction copies, the authors demonstrate potential exponential speedups for calculating total energy and band structures. This work advances the feasibility of using quantum computation for materials science simulations.

**Why it may be interesting.** The focus on quantum algorithms for electronic structure, especially developing techniques to bypass the density readout bottleneck, is highly relevant to quantum simulation and quantum chemistry.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge is accelerating Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations on quantum computers while circumventing the computationally expensive readout of the electronic density.

**Main result.** The authors propose quantum algorithms, particularly leveraging the Harris functional, that can evaluate total energy with a potential exponential speedup by avoiding density readout entirely.

**Method.** The work utilizes qubit-efficient encoding schemes and quantum algorithms like Adiabatic Time Evolution (ATE) and Probabilistic Imaginary Time Evolution (PITE) to simulate the Kohn-Sham Hamiltonian.

**Model / system.** The model is electronic structure calculation using Kohn-Sham (KS) DFT for materials like LiH and BCC Li metal. The Hamiltonian is derived from the KS equations, and the complexity arises from the density-dependent potential.

**Key observables.** Total energy, band energies, Density of States (DOS), and state fidelity during time evolution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The efficiency depends on avoiding density readout; the accuracy of the Harris functional depends on the input density ($ho_{in}$) being close to the true KS density ($ho_{KS}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The full SCF calculation using wavefunction copies is noted as a 'galactic algorithm' due to exponential qubit scaling, making the Harris functional approach more practical.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the reproduction of the true energy surface for Li-H, the comparison of quantum vs. classical DOS and band structure for BCC Li, and the evolution of state fidelity during ATE.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of DFT acceleration, proposes the qubit-efficient encoding and the Harris functional to bypass density readout, details the quantum algorithms (ATE/PITE) for energy calculation, and validates these methods on molecular and solid-state examples.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

While quantum computers have shown significant promise for electronic structure calculations, their potential to accelerate density functional theory (DFT) calculations remains unclear. In this work, we present a qubit-efficient encoding scheme for wavefunctions in Kohn--Sham (KS) DFT, together with a quantum algorithm that computes all occupied orbitals simultaneously. We further show that our algorithm is particularly well suited to the Harris functional, enabling the total energy to be evaluated with a potential exponential speedup over classical approaches by entirely avoiding the costly readout of the electronic density. In addition, we propose a second method for achieving self-consistent DFT calculations using multiple copies of the wavefunction, which likewise circumvents density readout. The applicability of our algorithms is demonstrated through several numerical examples, and their efficiency is compared with that of existing approaches.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.28927"></a>
### [Quantum encodings that preserve persistent homology](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28927v1)

**Authors:** Arthur J. Parzygnat, Andrew Vlasic  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28927v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.28927_figures/2605.28927_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. A dataset obtained by sampling from probability distributions concentrated near geometric shapes representing the letters A, B, and C. The data point markers are chosen differently to illustrate what happens to this dataset under a quantum encoding later in Figure 2.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.28927_figures/2605.28927_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. The dataset from Figure 1, with data vectors in the form x = (x1, x2), is mapped to the Bloch sphere by dense angle encoding |x⟩:= cos ␀ x1</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates quantum encodings designed to process classical data while ensuring the resulting quantum representation preserves the underlying topological structure, specifically persistent homology. It proposes a direct encoding approach to avoid computationally expensive steps in standard quantum TDA. The research is crucial for developing robust quantum machine learning algorithms that analyze the shape of complex datasets.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work bridges the gap between topological data analysis (a mathematical tool) and quantum computation, suggesting new constraints on how quantum algorithms can be designed to process real-world, geometrically structured data.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core problem is determining which quantum encodings can process classical data while preserving the topological invariants (like persistent homology) derived from that data.

**Main result.** The authors argue for the necessity of finding structure-preserving quantum encodings that act directly on the classical data points, bypassing resource-intensive construction of combinatorial objects.

**Method.** The work involves analyzing various quantum state encodings (e.g., Angle, Amplitude, Square-root) and comparing the resulting quantum metrics against classical topological invariants using distance measures like the Gromov–Hausdorff distance.

**Model / system.** The model involves classical datasets, such as point clouds in Euclidean space, which are mapped onto quantum states ($ho(x)$) via specific quantum encodings acting on qubits.

**Key observables.** Topological invariants (persistent homology, Betti numbers), quantum state distances (e.g., Bures, Hilbert-Schmidt), and distortion metrics (e.g., Gromov–Hausdorff distance).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The dimensionality of the data ($d$), the number of qubits used in the encoding, and the parameters defining the specific encoding function.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that the topological structure of the data is the most critical feature to preserve during quantum encoding, and that direct encoding is superior to encoding derived combinatorial structures.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate data sampled near geometric shapes and show the distortion introduced by specific quantum encodings when mapping the classical data onto the quantum state space.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the need for topological data analysis, then reviewing various quantum encoding schemes (Angle, Amplitude, etc.), and finally focusing on the mathematical criteria required for these encodings to preserve topological features.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Given a data set with a notion of distance, such as a point cloud in Euclidean space, topological data analysis (TDA) uses techniques from algebraic topology and metric geometry to infer the topology of a hypothetical manifold from which the data are sampled. This inference is achieved by calculating topological invariants, some of which are difficult to compute classically. Meanwhile, quantum TDA utilizes quantum processes to extract the invariants used in making such inferences in an attempt to speed up the computations. Because applying transformations to the original classical dataset could alter the associated topological invariants, we investigate which quantum encodings would best preserve the invariants of the original dataset. This line of inquiry is distinct from standard approaches in quantum TDA, whose typical starting point is not from the classical dataset directly, but rather from the associated combinatorial objects, such as simplicial complexes, which typically demand a lot of resources to construct. We take the first step at a more direct approach by focusing on which quantum encodings acting directly on the data are admissible for applying quantum algorithms to extract topological features from classical datasets.

</details>

### [Quantum Implicit-Explicit Schemes for Multiscale Ordinary and Partial Differential Equations via Schrödingerization](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29423v1)

**Authors:** Qitong Hu, Xiaoyang He, Shi Jin, Xiao-Dong Zhang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29423v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This paper introduces a novel quantum Implicit-Explicit (IMEX) scheme utilizing the Schrödingerization framework to solve multiscale PDEs on quantum computers. Its key breakthrough is achieving a query complexity that is independent of the small scaling parameter $\varepsilon$, a major hurdle in simulating stiff, multiscale physical systems. This advances the practical application of quantum computation to complex physics problems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides a concrete quantum computational pathway for simulating complex physical systems (like those governed by multiscale PDEs) where traditional numerical methods struggle with stiffness, directly impacting quantum simulation feasibility.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing quantum algorithms to solve multiscale Ordinary and Partial Differential Equations (ODEs/PDEs) whose discretization parameters are independent of the small scaling parameter $\varepsilon$.

**Main result.** The proposed quantum IMEX scheme achieves query complexity independent of the scaling parameter $\varepsilon$, significantly improving upon previous quantum methods.

**Method.** The approach uses a quantum Implicit-Explicit (IMEX) scheme combined with the Schrödingerization framework to map the differential equation into a unitary evolution suitable for quantum simulation.

**Model / system.** The methods are applied to multiscale ODEs and PDEs, exemplified by the linear heat equation and the multiscale telegraph equation, which exhibit widely separated temporal or spatial scales.

**Key observables.** Query complexity, auxiliary register width, and global error bounds.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Scaling parameter $\varepsilon$, time step $\tau$, spatial step $h$, and the number of time steps $N_t$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the entries of the system matrices are of order $O(1)$ and that the solution remains bounded.

**Figures summary.** Figures show numerical results comparing the quantum IMEX scheme against classical methods for the 1D and 2D Heat Equations, demonstrating convergence across different $\varepsilon$ values.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from general IMEX theory to the Schrödingerization framework, analyzes error propagation and convergence bounds, and concludes with specific numerical applications to the Heat and Telegraph equations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this paper, we present a quantum implicit-explicit (IMEX) scheme for multiscale ordinary and partial differential equations whose discretization parameters are independent of the scaling parameter $\varepsilon$. A key ingredient of our approach is a continuous-time formulation of classical IMEX schemes, which decouples the evolution time of the quantum algorithm from the physical time of the differential equation and is therefore particularly useful in multiscale settings. Building on this idea, we employ the Schrödingerization framework [Phys. Rev. Lett. 133 (2024), 230602] to implement IMEX schemes on quantum computers. Compared to previous HHL type quantum AP scheme [J. Comput. Phys. 471 (2022), 111641], this new method requires narrower -- an extra logarithmic factor -- auxiliary register numerical examples on linear heat and multiscale telegraph equations demonstrate the independence in $\varepsilon$ of the method.

</details>

### [Quantum optimization beyond QUBO for industrial logistics and scheduling](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30252v1)

**Authors:** Juan F. R. Hernandez, Pavle Nikacevic, Enrique Solano, Chinonso Onah, Agneev Guin, Arne-Christian Voigt, Archismita Dalal  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.30252v1>

**Summary.** This work investigates using quantum optimization to solve complex industrial logistics problems, proposing Higher-Order Unconstrained Binary Optimization (HUBO) as an improvement over standard QUBO. While HUBO reduces the required number of qubits, it significantly increases circuit depth due to higher-order terms. The authors conclude that current hardware limitations necessitate a shift toward hybrid quantum-classical workflows for practical application.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed analysis of resource scaling, gate overheads, and the explicit comparison between QUBO and HUBO provides a rigorous theoretical roadmap for when and how quantum computation can tackle real-world combinatorial optimization challenges.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the need for quantum optimization methods that can accurately model complex industrial scheduling and routing problems, moving beyond the limitations of standard Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO).

**Main result.** Higher-Order Unconstrained Binary Optimization (HUBO) formulations are necessary to faithfully encode complex constraints, offering better qubit scaling than QUBO, but their practical implementation is limited by increasing circuit depth.

**Method.** The authors compare HUBO and QUBO encodings for problems like CVRP and scheduling, validating them using classical solvers and benchmarking quantum execution via Bias-Field Digitized Counterdiabatic Quantum Optimization (BF-DCQO) in simulation.

**Model / system.** The models involve mapping logistics problems (e.g., CVRP, assembly scheduling) into Hamiltonians, comparing quadratic (QUBO) vs. higher-order (HUBO) penalty terms. The quantum simulation uses BF-DCQO, which evolves the system Hamiltonian $H(\lambda)$ over time.

**Key observables.** Qubit count, circuit depth (number of two-qubit gates), approximation ratio, and quantum gate fidelity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Gate fidelity ($f_{2q}$), coherence time, and the required penalty coefficients ($\lambda$) for constraint enforcement.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumptions involve the feasibility of hybrid quantum-classical workflows and the necessity of fault-tolerant hardware for large-scale problems.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the workflow for QUEST and CVRP, comparing two-qubit gate counts for QUBO vs. HUBO under different connectivity assumptions, and showing approximation ratios for different solvers.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from identifying the limitations of QUBO for complex constraints to developing the HUBO framework for three specific industrial problems (QUEST, CVRP, Scheduling). It details the Hamiltonian construction, compares resource scaling (qubits vs. gates), and concludes by advocating for hybrid quantum-classical approaches.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The increasing complexity of industrial scheduling and transport routing problems motivates the study of alternative optimization formulations and computational paradigms. In this work, we study how higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) formulations of such problems map onto quantum optimization workflows in both noisy and fault-tolerant regimes. We consider three representative logistics and manufacturing use cases and formulate each as a HUBO problem. This captures process intricacies, such as highly correlated assembly-line scheduling rules, which are difficult to express faithfully with the standard quadratic (QUBO) form, while at the same time reducing the number of binary variables required in the quantum mapping, thus lowering qubit demand. We compare the HUBO formulations with corresponding QUBO encodings, highlighting a key trade-off: while HUBO reduces qubit requirements through compact binary encoding, it introduces higher-order interaction terms that increase circuit depth, limiting feasibility on current quantum hardware. The proposed formulations are validated using classical solvers across several problem instances and benchmark small routing problem instances using bias-field digitized counterdiabatic quantum optimization in classical simulation. We complement these results with a resource and scalability analysis, focusing on the capacitated vehicle routing problem as a representative large-scale industrial use case. Our analysis indicates that while HUBO formulations offer advantages in qubit scaling compared to QUBO encodings, their practical implementation is constrained by gate fidelity, coherence, and circuit depth, making hybrid quantum-classical workflows and early fault-tolerant quantum hardware the most plausible settings for their practical use.

</details>

**Authors:** Hiroshi Ohno  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29546v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</details>

<img src="2605.29546_figures/2605.29546_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Results for the approximated complexity as a function of Ntrials</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper derives theoretical bounds on the Rademacher complexity for quantum circuits parameterized by Pauli strings. By analyzing the complexity, the authors establish how the generalization error scales with the number of parameters ($L$) and training samples ($M$). This work provides a crucial statistical framework for understanding the learning capacity of quantum machine learning models.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides rigorous statistical learning theory tools (Rademacher complexity) to quantify the generalization capacity of quantum machine learning models built from Pauli circuits, which is crucial for assessing the practical utility of these quantum models.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study analyzes the generalization properties of parameterized quantum circuits (unitaries) generated by Pauli strings by deriving explicit Rademacher complexity bounds.

**Main result.** The derived bounds show that the Rademacher complexity scales as $O(L^{3/2}/\sqrt{M})$ for the full parameter domain, improving to $O(L/\sqrt{M})$ when the parameter domain is restricted.

**Method.** The authors use Rademacher complexity ($	ext{RM}(F)$) as a measure for generalization gap bounding, applying techniques involving Lipschitz constants and covering number estimates.

**Model / system.** The model is a parameterized unitary $U(	heta) = \prod_{j=1}^{L} \exp(-i	heta_j G_j)$, where the generators $G_j$ are $n$-qubit Pauli strings. The expectation value $f(x; 	heta)$ is the observable analyzed.

**Key observables.** Rademacher complexity ($	ext{RM}(F)$), Generalization Gap, expectation value $f(x; 	heta)$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** L (Number of parameters), M (Number of training samples), n (Number of qubits).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theoretical analysis relies on bounding the covering number and using the Lipschitz constant; numerical experiments use a random-search approximation.

**Figures summary.** The notes describe figures showing the dependence of the approximated empirical Rademacher complexity on sample size $M$ and parameter size $L$, consistent with the predicted $1/\sqrt{M}$ and $L$ scaling.

**Paper structure.** The paper develops the theoretical bounds using complexity measures, compares these bounds to classical models, and supports the findings with numerical experiments analyzing scaling with $L$ and $M$.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this study, we analyze the Rademacher complexity $ \mathcal{R}_{M} $ of a parameterized unitary whose generators are chosen from $ n $-qubit Pauli strings. Although generalization bounds for quantum machine learning models have been studied in several settings, explicit Rademacher-complexity bounds for parameterized unitaries generated by Pauli strings remain less transparent. We derive simple scaling bounds in terms of the number of parameters $ L $ and the number of training samples $ M $: $ \mathcal{O}(\frac{L^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\sqrt{M}}) $ for the full parameter domain and $ \mathcal{O}(\frac{L}{\sqrt{M}}) $ for a restricted parameter domain. Furthermore, we compare the obtained results with those for a classical linear model class and suggest a potential statistical-complexity advantage when the norms of both the input and the parameter in the classical model scale with the number of parameters. Numerical experiments provide qualitative evidence consistent with the predicted scaling.

</details>

**Authors:** Wen Chen, An Min Wang

(a): The evolution of N=20 worlds configuration of smooth model V (x) = erf(2(x −1)) −erf(2(x + 1)) + 2 with ∆t = 5 × 10−2 and total 40000 steps in minimum effective region [−4, 4]. (b): The bandwidth hn of the system in (a). (c): The density of ground states of system in (a) (solid line) and the solution from matrix Numerov algorithm with grids of 100 (plots). (d): The energy error varies with parameter ν in error function model Vν(x) = [erf(ν(x −1)) −erf(ν(x + 1)) + 2].</sub>

**Summary.** This paper extends the Many-Interacting-Worlds (MIW) method to simulate quantum systems with singular potentials, such as the Coulomb potential. By replacing these singularities with asymptotically smooth models, the authors demonstrate that their dynamical algorithm yields results consistent with established quantum mechanical solvers. This advances the applicability of the MIW framework to more complex, realistic physical models.

**Why it may be interesting.** The development and rigorous testing of a novel numerical framework (MIW) to handle complex, singular potentials in quantum mechanics is highly relevant for developing robust computational tools for AMO physics simulations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of applying the Many-Interacting-Worlds (MIW) method to solve bounded quantum systems, especially those involving singular potentials like the Coulomb potential.

**Main result.** The MIW dynamical algorithm successfully reproduces results consistent with standard quantum mechanics methods (like the Numerov method) when singular potentials are replaced by asymptotically smooth models.

**Method.** The core method is the MIW dynamical algorithm, which simulates the system's evolution using an interacting 'worlds' approach. This is supplemented by kernel estimation for density approximation and replacing singular potentials with smooth asymptotic forms.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on solving the time-independent Schrödinger equation for various bounded potentials, including the harmonic oscillator, the 2D Coulomb potential, and finite trap potentials, extending the MIW method to 2D and higher dimensions.

**Key observables.** Stationary state energies ($E_n$), total energy convergence, and comparison of the MIW energy ($E_{	ext{miw}}$) against the standard matrix Numerov energy ($E$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The smoothness parameter ($
u$ or $\mu$) used in the asymptotic potential replacement, the time step ($\Delta t$), and the number of interacting worlds ($N$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes that potentials can be accurately approximated by smooth asymptotic forms near singularities, and that the dynamical algorithm will converge to the true stationary state energy.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the convergence of the total energy error for the 2D Coulomb potential as the smoothness parameter ($\mu$) is varied, and show the evolution of world configurations and bandwidth for both 1D and 2D systems.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds upon previous work on the harmonic oscillator, details the use of kernel estimation for density, introduces the asymptotic smoothing technique for singular potentials, and validates the dynamical algorithm's convergence against established quantum mechanical solvers.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The deterministic many-interacting-worlds method proposed in 2014 showed potential among the numerous interpretation of quantum mechanics. The successful application of this method in harmonic oscillator has been promoted for a long time. In this article we continue the idea about using this method to solve some bounded systems different from harmonic oscillator potential and extend to 2 dimension cases. We focus on the potential with singularity like coulomb potential and finite trap potential by some asymptotic smooth method. The numerical simulation mainly based on the dynamical algorithm proposed in many-interacting-worlds method will be used to approach the stationary states of given systems. Our results shows the consistency to the matrix Numerov method in standard quantum mechanics in solving bounded systems and provides the possibility to solve more complex systems.

</details>

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.28993"></a>
### [Reinterpreting Memory Effects in Nonequilibrium Systems: From Temporal Dynamics to Steady-State Signatures via NEGF](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28993v1)

**Authors:** Pragya Chaudhary  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28993v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **4/5** · 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **3/5** · `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.28993_figures/2605.28993_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.28993_figures/2605.28993_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.28993_figures/2605.28993_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.28993_figures/2605.28993_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. SCBA: Ainel(τ) versus τ Plot.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.28993_figures/2605.28993_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Self-consistent Born approximation (SCBA) self- energy diagram. The thick fermion line denotes the fully dressed Green’s function G, while the phonon propagator re- mains bare.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical work develops a comprehensive framework using Non-equilibrium Green's Functions to analyze memory effects in quantum transport. It rigorously distinguishes between Markovian dynamics, associated with local self-energies (like static disorder), and Non-Markovian dynamics, arising from time-correlated interactions such as electron-phonon coupling. The transmission function T(E) is proposed as the key, experimentally accessible observable to diagnose these fundamental differences in memory behavior.

**Why it may be interesting.** The explicit connection drawn between the mathematical structure of the self-energy (time-local vs. time-nonlocal) and the physical memory regime (Markovian vs. Non-Markovian) provides a powerful, general diagnostic tool for open quantum systems, relevant to quantum optics and condensed matter dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to reinterpret memory effects in non-equilibrium quantum transport, establishing a rigorous connection between the system's temporal dynamics and measurable steady-state signatures.

**Main result.** The study distinguishes between Markovian dynamics (e.g., static disorder) and genuine Non-Markovian behavior (e.g., electron-phonon coupling), showing that the spectral function and transmission function are key diagnostics for these memory effects.

**Method.** The analysis employs the Non-equilibrium Green's Function (NEGF) formalism within the Schwinger-Keldysh path integral framework, utilizing Dyson expansions and approximations like the Born and Self-Consistent Born Approximations.

**Model / system.** The work is based on 2D lattice systems starting from a tight-binding Hamiltonian, extended to include both static disorder (elastic scattering) and electron-phonon coupling (inelastic scattering). Paradigmatic models include the Hofstadter and RKKY-coupled systems.

**Key observables.** Spectral function, Transmission Function T(E), and the time-dependent memory kernel A(t).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Scattering strength ($\lambda$), coupling constants ($g_q$), and the ratio of coupling rates ($\gamma$ vs. $\omega_q$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on comparing the results from the 1PI vs. 2PI effective actions, where the 2PI approach is necessary to capture the non-Markovian memory effects arising from the bath.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the decaying (Markovian) vs. oscillatory (Non-Markovian) nature of the memory kernel, and compare the resulting transmission function T(E) for different scattering regimes in the Hofstadter and RKKY models.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the general NEGF formalism and the distinction between elastic/inelastic self-energies, to analyzing memory signatures via the spectral function, and finally applying the framework to specific 2D models (Hofstadter, RKKY) using the transmission function as the ultimate diagnostic tool.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate memory effects and quantum transport in two-dimensional lattice systems within the framework of non-equilibrium Green's functions and Schwinger-Keldysh non-equilibrium quantum field theory. Starting from a 2D tight-binding Hamiltonian, we employ the Dyson expansion on the Keldysh contour and the second-order Born and self-consistent Born Approximation to derive the electronic self-energies associated with elastic and inelastic scattering mechanisms.Static disorder produces a local self-energy and a rapidly decaying memory kernel, characteristic of Markovian dynamics, whereas electron-phonon coupling generates temporally nonlocal self-energies and genuine Non-Markovian behavior. We demonstrate that these distinct memory signatures are directly reflected in the spectral function, which we propose as a diagnostic probe of non-equilibrium memory effects. Further we explore 1PI and 2PI effective actions to see their memory perspectives studying their coarse-graining behavior. Building on this theoretical framework, we further apply the conventional NEGF formalism to two paradigmatic two-dimensional models-the Hofstadter and an RKKY-coupled system to explore how different microscopic Hamiltonians influence Markovian and Non-Markovian nature. Our results provide a unified connection between scattering mechanisms, memory effects, and quantum transport in low-dimensional systems.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper details a novel method to simulate arbitrary curved spaces and field theories using arrays of superconducting Josephson-coupled wires. By engineering the graph topology, researchers can realize geometries like Anti-de Sitter space, providing an experimentally accessible platform to test concepts like holographic duality. The work is significant for its direct link between superconducting circuit measurements and fundamental physics in curved spacetime.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a concrete, physical platform (superconducting circuits) to test deep theoretical concepts like holographic duality and field theories on curved spaces, bridging condensed matter hardware with high-energy theory.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper introduces a framework to emulate arbitrary graphs and curved spaces by engineering superconducting circuits, allowing the study of field theories on these emergent geometries.

**Main result.** The superconducting array can experimentally realize Anti-de Sitter (AdS) spaces and demonstrate that holographic duality remains robust even when strong disorder is introduced into the Josephson couplings.

**Method.** The approach involves mapping continuous field theories onto discrete graph Hamiltonians using Josephson-coupled superconducting wires, and analyzing the system's behavior in various coupling limits.

**Model / system.** The physical system is an array of superconducting wires forming a graph structure, where the superconducting phases encode scalar field theories. The Hamiltonian is governed by Josephson coupling energies across the graph edges.

**Key observables.** Measurable circuit observables like current and voltage relations, which map to two-point correlators in the emulated field theory.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The ratio of coupling strengths ($J$ vs $J_w$), the wire length relative to a critical scale ($L/L^\star$), and the degree of disorder in the Josephson couplings.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is the 'rigid phase' approximation, where the superconducting phase is assumed to take a single value along the wire up to a certain length scale $L^\star(T)$.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the mapping from field variables to wire phases and Josephson junctions, the structure of bipartite vs. non-bipartite graph realizations, and the general lattice mesh for manifold triangulation.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from the general concept of graph emulation to specific Hamiltonian formulations, analyzes the limits of the phase rigidity approximation, and applies the framework to complex geometries like AdS spaces to test holographic duality.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce a framework for emulating graphs and, through them, curved spaces of arbitrary dimension, using arrays of superconducting wires. The array consists of two stacked layers of wires, horizontal and vertical, such that wires are parallel within each layer and perpendicular between layers. By discretizing a space into a graph, assigning a superconducting wire with a rigid phase to each vertex, and coupling pairs of wires through Josephson junctions along the graph edges, arbitrary geometries and topologies can be engineered in a controlled setting. The superconducting phases then realize scalar field theories on the emergent geometry. We establish experimentally realistic conditions for implementing these architectures and develop a dictionary relating measurable circuit observables to quantities in the emulated field theory. As an application, we develop the implementation of hyperbolic (Anti-de Sitter) spaces of constant negative curvature and use them as an experimentally accessible platform to explore holographic duality in arbitrary dimensions. We investigate the effects of disorder in the Josephson couplings, which translate into metric variations in the bulk-boundary correspondence, and analyze their impact on boundary scaling exponents both analytically and numerically, finding that holographic duality remains robust even in the presence of strong disorder. Beyond holography, the framework opens a broad range of architectural possibilities, including the exploration of physics on highly nontrivial graphs and toy models of dynamical spacetimes.

</details>
