**Summary.** The paper introduces a quantum light scattering-type SNOM (q-SNOM) to overcome limitations in probing quantum effects in solids. By using entangled photons, the technique achieves nanoscale visualization of single polariton self-interference and measures quasiparticle dynamics with femtosecond resolution. This establishes a powerful new platform for studying quantum materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work bridges quantum optics and condensed matter physics by providing a novel, non-destructive, and highly sensitive tool to observe fundamental quantum wave-particle duality and dynamics in solid-state systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The core challenge is developing a method to experimentally probe quantum phenomena, such as entanglement and quantum correlations, in solid-state materials at length and time scales below the diffraction limit.

**Main result.** The authors successfully developed a quantum light scattering-type SNOM (q-SNOM) that directly visualizes the self-interference of single hybrid light-matter quasiparticles and enables femtosecond-resolution measurement of polariton propagation dynamics.

**Method.** The technique uses entangled photon pairs generated via SPDC, integrating them into a SNOM setup. Measurements rely on coincidence detection and time-of-flight metrology to enforce single-photon counting and measure temporal correlations.

**Model / system.** The experiment is performed on atomically layered van der Waals (vdW) semiconductors, such as MoS2, which host hybrid light-matter quasiparticles called polaritons. The setup utilizes entangled photons as quantum probes.

**Key observables.** Single polariton interference fringes, the second-order correlation function g^(2)(τ), and the time delay (Δτ) of quasiparticle propagation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Length scales below the diffraction limit, femtosecond time resolution, and single-photon detection regime.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The technique assumes that the temporal correlation provided by entangled photons can accurately map the ultrafast dynamics of the material excitations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the q-SNOM setup, show single-photon interference patterns, present the measured g^(2)(τ) curves, and plot extracted time delays (Δτ) against theoretical predictions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the limitations of current quantum probing techniques, details the development and implementation of the q-SNOM setup, presents evidence of single-photon self-interference, and finally applies the technique to measure the ultrafast time-of-flight dynamics of polaritons.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Entanglement and quantum correlations are central to the physics of quantum materials, yet they have remained notoriously difficult to probe experimentally. Probing these phenomena in solids requires quantum optical probes that operate at the native length and time scales of material excitations, below the diffraction limit of light. Developing the requisite tools has previously been infeasible due to the extremely weak intensities of state-of-the-art quantum light sources and extreme inefficiency of near-field light-matter interactions. In this work, we circumvent these challenges and develop a quantum light scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscope (q-SNOM) that can explore the broad domain of solid-state quantum effects at length scales below the diffraction limit. In its first application, we image in real space the self-interference of single hybrid light-matter quasiparticles in a van der Waals semiconductor, providing a direct nanoscale visualization of the wave-particle duality. We also introduce a polaritonic time-of-flight metrology that exploits the temporal correlations among entangled photons to observe the quasiparticle propagation dynamics with femtosecond resolution. This work sets the stage for nanoscale exploration and control of quantum effects in materials.

</details>

### [Critical states and anomalous wave transport in an aperiodic polariton monotile](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29023v1)

**Summary.** This paper analyzes wave transport in a polariton system engineered onto an aperiodic 'Hat' monotile lattice. By analyzing both single-particle and mean-field dynamics, the authors confirm the existence of critical states and demonstrate anomalous wave transport, exhibiting distinct super-diffusive and sub-diffusive scaling regimes. The findings provide a platform for studying complex wave dynamics in engineered quasiperiodic media.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work directly bridges the physics of aperiodic order (quasicrystals/monotiles) with wave localization and transport phenomena, which are central themes in disordered systems, making it highly relevant to AMO and quantum optics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates wave transport, transverse localization, and scaling properties of quantum modes within the aperiodic 'Hat' monotile quasilattice.

**Main result.** The system exhibits critical states characterized by anomalous transport regimes, showing evidence of both super-diffusive and near sub-diffusive polariton transport.

**Method.** The research employs both theoretical diagonalization of the Schrödinger equation and numerical simulation of wavepacket dynamics using non-Hermitian and nonlinear mean-field models.

**Model / system.** The system is based on cavity-polaritons confined in a 2D Monotile quasilattice, modeled by a Schrödinger equation incorporating an optically induced potential $V(x, y)$. The dynamics are extended to include gain ($\kappa$) and loss ($\gamma$) for non-Hermitian analysis.

**Key observables.** Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), Generalized Dimension ($D_q$), effective spatial width ($	ext{weff}$), and temporal autocorrelation function ($C(t)$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Potential amplitude ($V_0$), lattice separation ($a$), and non-Hermitian parameters ($\kappa, \gamma$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Initial analysis assumes a Hermitian problem, but later sections extend to non-Hermitian dynamics to model dissipation and gain relevant for polariton condensation.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the Monotile structure, the dependence of localization measures ($I_2, D_2$) on energy, and log-log plots showing the time evolution of wavepacket width ($	ext{weff}$) confirming super- and sub-diffusive scaling.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from analyzing the Hermitian eigenvalue problem (localization/criticality) to studying wavepacket dynamics (anomalous transport) in the non-Hermitian regime, culminating in a mean-field description of polariton condensate formation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recently "the Hat" monotile was introduced into the family of aperiodic tilings and quasicrystals boasting physical properties lying at the boundary of ordered and disordered systems. Here we study the two-dimensional wave transport, transverse localization and scaling properties of the quantum modes in a Monotile quasilattice. Our system is based on reconfigurable optical lattices for cavity-polaritons which provide flexible means to study wavepacket dynamics, strong nonlinear phenomena, and power-driven condensation in this new type of an aperiodic tiling. We confirm the existence of localized and critical states in the Monotile through direct diagonalization of the Schrödinger equation. Scaling analysis on the moments of the wavefunction distribution reveals anomalous transport regimes of super-diffusive and near sub-diffusive polariton transport associated with the fractal structure of the Monotile Hilbert space. We propose a strategy using resonantly excited polariton fluids to verify our findings.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Summary.** This work presents a general, systematic method to derive the effective dynamics of nonlinear systems driven by high-frequency oscillations. By expanding the action in powers of $1/\omega$, the authors provide effective Lagrangians and potentials that govern the slow, large-scale motion. This is crucial for analyzing stability in complex systems like magnetic traps and pendulums.

**Why it may be interesting.** The technique of deriving effective Hamiltonians/Lagrangians from rapidly oscillating driving fields is a powerful tool for analyzing non-adiabatic dynamics in quantum and classical systems, relevant for Floquet engineering and quantum optics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper develops a systematic method to determine the effective action governing the large-scale nonlinear dynamics of physical systems subjected to a rapidly oscillating external force.

**Main result.** The effective Lagrangian and potential are derived order by order in inverse powers of the oscillation frequency, revealing that the stability analysis can often be reduced to the linear Mathieu equation.

**Method.** The core method involves a high-frequency expansion, separating the system's motion into slow and fast components, and systematically calculating the effective dynamics using averaging techniques.

**Model / system.** The analysis is applied to various classical systems, including the Kapitza pendulum, charged particles in oscillating magnetic fields, and general nonlinear systems in curved configuration spaces.

**Key observables.** Effective Lagrangian ($L_{	ext{eff}}$), Effective Potential ($V_{	ext{eff}}$), and the transition curves defining dynamical stability.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Oscillation frequency ($\omega$), expansion parameter ($\epsilon$), and the ratio of characteristic time scales.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on the assumption of fast oscillation ($\omega$ is large) and is an approximation derived through perturbation theory.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the effective potential and particle trajectories for various systems, such as the Kapitza pendulum and magnetic traps, comparing exact solutions to those derived from the effective theory.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically builds the effective theory, first deriving the general expansion method, then applying it to specific physical models like the Kapitza pendulum and magnetic confinement, and finally detailing the high-order calculations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider systems characterized by the presence of a rapidly oscillating force. A general method is presented for the construction of the effective action governing the large-scale nonlinear dynamics of such systems order by order in inverse powers of the oscillation frequency $ω$. The explicit expression for the effective Lagrangian is derived up to ${\cal O}(1/ω^6)$ next-to-next-to-leading approximation. The general structure of the high-frequency expansion reveals a broad class of nonlinear systems whose transition curves are identical to those of the linear Mathieu equation, which enables a fully nonperturbative stability analysis in the case of strong driving and nonlinearity. The method is generalized to velocity-dependent forces and configuration space with curvature, characteristic to systems with constraints. Several applications are discussed in detail, including the dynamical magnetic trapping of electric charges.

</details>

The dashed red arrows represent the conical magnetic conﬁguration with a nonzero tilt angle α. (b-d) Energy bands of the HM for (b) α = 0 or π, (c) α = π</sub>

The parameters are taken as (a) N = 1 and µ = 1, (b) N = 1 and λ = 1, (c) λ = 0.75 and µ = 1, (d) N = 1 and µ = 1, (e) N = 1 and α = 0.25π, and (f) α = 0.25π and µ = 1.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper theoretically investigates the Josephson diode effect in junctions involving a helimagnet. Using Green's functions and symmetry analysis, the authors establish that the JDE critically depends on the magnetic configuration (requiring a conical state) and the superconducting pairing symmetry. The findings offer a pathway to realizing non-reciprocal, dissipationless electronic components without relying on spin-orbit coupling.

**Why it may be interesting.** The analysis of non-reciprocal transport phenomena in magnetic junctions provides insights into engineered quantum electronic devices, linking magnetism, superconductivity, and diode effects.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the Josephson diode effect (JDE) in superconducting junctions incorporating a helimagnetic material.

**Main result.** The JDE is found to require a conical magnetic configuration in the helimagnet and a non-zero chemical potential for s-wave superconductors, while p-wave superconductors can exhibit JDE even at zero chemical potential.

**Method.** The research employs the Green's function method, symmetry analysis, and energy band calculations to derive the supercurrent and diode efficiency.

**Model / system.** The system modeled is a one-dimensional junction composed of a superconductor (s-wave or p-wave) connected to a helimagnet. The physics is governed by the Bogoliubov-de Gennes Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Josephson diode efficiency ($\eta$), supercurrent nonreciprocity, and Critical Current-Phase Relations (CPRs).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Chirality ($\gamma$), tilt angle ($\alpha$), exchange coupling ($\lambda$), chemical potential ($\mu$), and the number of supercells ($N$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is limited to one-dimensional junctions and provides a scheme for JDE without spin-orbit coupling.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate energy band structures for different magnetic angles ($\alpha$), the dependence of JDE efficiency on $\alpha$, $\lambda$, $\mu$, and $N$, and the CPRs for different chiralities.

**Paper structure.** The paper builds from defining the Hamiltonian for the SC/HM/SC junction, analyzing the symmetry properties of the supercurrent, deriving the JDE efficiency, and finally discussing the specific conditions for s-wave and p-wave pairing.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the Josephson diode effect in the one-dimensional superconductor/helimagnet/superconductor junctions using the Green's function method. For the spin-singlet $s$-wave pairing in superconductors, it is found that the necessary conditions for the Josephson diode effect are the nonzero chemical potential and the conical magnetic configuration in the helimagnet. The diode efficiency is strongly dependent on the chemical potential, chirality, tilt angle and exchange coupling in the helimagnet. The high efficiency close to $40\%$ can be obtained for specific parameter values. The sign of the diode efficiency can be tuned by changing the chirality, tilt angle, exchange coupling and chemical potential. The dependence of the diode efficiency on the number of supercells in the helimagnet is also investigated. The characteristics of the supercurrent nonreciprocity and diode efficiency in the junctions are clarified through the symmetry analysis and the energy band calculations. The diode effect for the spin-triplet $p$-wave pairing in superconductors is also discussed and the nonzero chemical potential is no longer a necessary condition for the Josephson diode effect due to the equal-spin Cooper pair-mediated transport in the $p$-wave junctions. These results provide a scheme for the Josephson diode effect without spin-orbit coupling, which possesses the potential applications in the design of dissipationless electronic devices.

</details>

**Summary.** This work investigates how magnetic domain structures influence the electrical conductivity of a ferromagnetic film. By measuring magnetotransport properties near the material's coercive field, the authors successfully isolate and quantify the additional resistivity contribution arising specifically from the domain walls. This confirms that magnetic inhomogeneities are a significant, measurable factor in the material's electrical response.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the detailed analysis of non-equilibrium transport phenomena and the quantification of mesoscopic structural contributions (domain walls) to bulk properties is relevant to understanding dissipation mechanisms in complex quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to quantify the contribution of spatial magnetic inhomogeneities, such as domain walls, to the macroscopic magnetotransport response in a ferromagnetic film.

**Main result.** The research demonstrates that the contribution of magnetic domain walls to the resistivity is macroscopically measurable and can be comparable in magnitude to other established magnetotransport terms, especially at low temperatures.

**Method.** Magnetotransport measurements were performed by varying temperature and in-plane magnetic fields, analyzing the longitudinal resistivity ($\rho_L$) near the coercive field.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a 75 nm-thick $\text{Fe}_{0.5}\text{Pt}_{0.5}$ ferromagnetic film exhibiting striped magnetic domains. The analysis uses a generalized Ohm's law framework to model the electric field and resistivity components.

**Key observables.** Longitudinal resistivity ($\rho_L$), transverse resistivity ($\rho_T$), and the resistivity discontinuity ($\Delta\rho_{L,coer}$) near the coercive field.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature ($T$), in-plane magnetic field ($H$), and the coercive field ($H_{coer}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on fitting the macroscopic response to theoretical models derived from generalized Ohm's law, assuming the contribution of domain walls can be isolated.

**Figures summary.** Figures show magnetoresistance ($\rho_L$ vs. $\phi_H$) and resistivity vs. temperature ($T$) curves, highlighting local extrema near $H_{coer}$ and the temperature dependence of resistivity differences.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical framework using generalized Ohm's law to presenting measurements across varying fields and temperatures, culminating in the isolation and quantification of the domain wall contribution to the total resistivity.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

A 75 nm-thick Fe0.5Pt0.5 film is a ferromagnetic metal showing striped magnetic domains in remanence at room temperature. The magnetoresistance is characterized by varying the external temperature and the in-plane magnetic field intensity, thereby affecting its magnetic structure. Qualitatively, the resistivity is well described by using the generalized Ohm's law. High-field magnetotransport properties are successfully explained by considering the competition between the expected metallic behavior and the electron-magnon interaction. In the low-field condition, we size the contribution of the magnetic texture to the macroscopic magnetotransport response by introducing a new quantity. Consistent with the microscopic modulation of the lateral conduction, low-field measurements reveal inhomogeneities attributed to the spatial distribution of ferromagnetic domains and domain walls. By carefully analyzing the macroscopic response near the coercive field, the additional contribution to the resistivity is attributed to the domain walls themselves. In fact, this term could surpass the anisotropic term at low temperatures. In summary, this study demonstrates that spatial magnetic inhomogeneities are not only macroscopically measurable but also comparable in magnitude to other regularly considered terms, mainly at low temperatures.

</details>

### [Disentangling Spin Pumping and Two-Magnon Scattering Contributions to Gilbert Damping in YIG/V Bilayers](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29597v1)

**Authors:** S. Elkady, A. Tlais, H. Reslan, S. Isber, M. Haidar  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29597v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<img src="2605.29597_figures/2605.29597_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<img src="2605.29597_figures/2605.29597_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

**Summary.** This work investigates magnetic damping in YIG/V bilayers using FMR measurements to separate the effects of spin pumping and two-magnon scattering (TMS). The authors demonstrate that TMS is the dominant mechanism controlling the thickness dependence of damping, correcting previous analyses that overestimated the spin-pumping contribution. This provides a more accurate framework for quantifying spin transport parameters in spintronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the rigorous separation of damping mechanisms (spin pumping vs. extrinsic scattering) is fundamentally related to understanding energy dissipation and spin relaxation in nanoscale magnetic systems, which has parallels in open quantum system dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to accurately determine the relative contributions of spin pumping and two-magnon scattering (TMS) to the total Gilbert damping observed in YIG/V magnetic bilayers.

**Main result.** The analysis shows that TMS plays a dominant role in governing the thickness dependence of the damping in the nanometer regime, and neglecting it leads to a significant overestimation of the spin-pumping contribution.

**Method.** The authors used broadband Ferromagnetic Resonance (FMR) measurements combined with a sophisticated, thickness-dependent damping model that explicitly accounts for both spin pumping and TMS contributions.

**Model / system.** The physical system is Yttrium iron garnet (YIG) ferromagnetic layers grown on a Vanadium (V) normal metal layer. The analysis models the total damping ($\alpha$) as a sum of contributions from spin pumping ($\alpha_{sp}$) and two-magnon scattering ($\alpha_{TMS}$), alongside the intrinsic damping.

**Key observables.** Gilbert damping parameter ($\alpha$), FMR linewidth ($\Delta H$), and the effective spin-mixing conductance ($g^{\uparrow\downarrow}_{\mathrm{eff}}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The extracted effective spin-mixing conductance is found to be $g^{\uparrow\downarrow}_{\mathrm{eff}} = 1.33 	imes 10^{18}~\mathrm{m^{-2}}$, which is thickness-independent.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary assumption is that the total damping can be accurately decomposed into distinct, quantifiable contributions from spin pumping and TMS using established theoretical models.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate FMR spectra, the thickness dependence of the magnetization, and crucially, the separation of the total damping enhancement into components attributable to spin pumping and TMS.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first measuring the damping enhancement via FMR, then applying a comprehensive model that incorporates both spin pumping and TMS, and finally using this combined model to disentangle the contributions and extract accurate material parameters.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we investigate the magnetic damping and spin pumping response of YIG-based bilayers incorporating vanadium (V) as the normal metal layer via broadband ferromagnetic resonance (FMR) measurements as a function of YIG thickness. We show that the apparent enhancement of the Gilbert damping in YIG/V bilayers cannot be solely attributed to spin pumping. Instead, two-magnon scattering (TMS) plays a dominant role in governing the thickness dependence of the damping in the nanometer regime. By applying a thickness-dependent damping model that accounts for both spin pumping and two-magnon scattering contributions, we successfully disentangle the different relaxation contributions. Our analysis reveals that neglecting two-magnon scattering leads to an overestimation of the spin-pumping contribution and consequently to unphysically large values of the effective spin-mixing conductance. After isolating the intrinsic spin pumping contribution, we extract a thickness-independent effective spin-mixing conductance of $g^{\uparrow\downarrow}_{\mathrm{eff}} = 1.33 \times 10^{18}~\mathrm{m^{-2}}$. These findings provide a more accurate framework for quantifying spin transport parameters in FM/HM systems and emphasize the necessity of accounting for extrinsic damping mechanisms when interpreting spin pumping and inverse spin Hall effect experiments.

</details>

**Summary.** This work experimentally probes quantum criticality in the Integer Quantum Hall Effect by using a bilayer electron system where the interaction strength can be controlled via an overlying screening layer. By measuring the critical exponent $\kappa$, the authors demonstrate a clear transition from behavior indicative of long-range interactions to that of short-range interactions upon screening, providing crucial evidence for interaction effects in this regime.

**Why it may be interesting.** The ability to tune the interaction strength via an external gate voltage provides a clean experimental handle to test fundamental theoretical predictions regarding the universality class governing quantum critical points in strongly correlated electron systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how electron-electron interaction, specifically through screening, dictates the critical behavior (quantum criticality) during plateau-to-plateau transitions in the Integer Quantum Hall Effect (IQHE) regime.

**Main result.** The measurements provide direct evidence that screening the electron-electron interaction reduces the critical exponent $\kappa$ from a value consistent with long-range interactions ($z=1$) to a value consistent with short-range interactions ($z=2$).

**Method.** The critical exponent $\kappa$ is extracted by analyzing the temperature dependence of the derivative of the Hall resistance ($	ext{d}R_{xy}/	ext{d}B$) at the transition points.

**Model / system.** The experiment uses a GaAs double quantum well device containing two closely spaced 2D electron systems (top and bottom layers). The top layer density is tuned to control the screening environment for the bottom layer.

**Key observables.** The critical exponent $\kappa$, the Hall resistance $R_{xy}$ vs. magnetic field $B$, and the temperature dependence of these measurements.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The degree of screening, controlled by the top layer density ($n_t$), and the magnetic field strength relative to the magnetic length ($l_B$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on established scaling relations connecting the critical exponent $\kappa$ to the dynamic exponent $z$ based on the nature of the electron interaction (long-range vs. short-range).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the sample structure, the temperature dependence of $	ext{d}R_{xy}/	ext{d}B$ for varying screening conditions, and the resulting extracted values of $\kappa$ for both unscreened and screened regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically measures the critical exponent $\kappa$ by varying the screening environment (top layer density) and comparing the results to theoretical predictions for different interaction ranges.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Disorder-induced localization of electrons and electron-electron interaction are among the most fundamental problems in condensed matter physics. In two-dimensional electron systems, extensive studies have led to the emergence of a scaling picture, characterized by a set of universal critical exponents that govern the transitions between the integer quantum Hall plateaus. From the temperature dependence of the plateau-to-plateau transitions, experiments primarily report k ~ 0.42, implying a dynamic exponent z = 1, consistent with a theoretical picture where electrons have a long-range (1/r) interaction. Theory also predicts that z = 2 for short-range electron interaction, but an experimental verification has remained elusive. Here, we directly probe the influence of Coulomb interaction on these transitions using a bilayer electron system confined to a GaAs double quantum well device. The two layers are in close proximity, with an interlayer distance approximately equal to the magnetic length at the relevant magnetic fields. By tuning the electron density in the top layer, we access both insulating and metallic phases of the electrons in this layer as a function of magnetic field, allowing in-situ control of the unscreened and screened interaction strengths in the bottom layer as it goes through its plateau-to-plateau transitions. In the unscreened case, we measure k ~ 0.42 consistent with the widely reported value. More importantly, when screening is introduced, k is reduced to ~ 0.22, implying z = 2. Our results provide direct experimental evidence for the role of electron-electron interaction in determining critical behavior in the quantum Hall regime, and demonstrate screening as a powerful tuning parameter for engineering quantum criticality.

</details>

**Summary.** This work reports on the deterministic engineering of Bloch points—topological singularities in magnetization—within double-helix nanowires. By creating a structural defect at a chirality interface, the researchers impose competing topological constraints that allow precise control over the Bloch point's polarity and spin texture. The combination of X-ray tomography and micromagnetics provides strong evidence for this controlled topological state.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the concept of topological defects (Bloch points) and their deterministic control via engineered boundaries relates to topological quantum matter, which is a key area of interest for quantum information.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary challenge is achieving deterministic control over the position and specific spin texture of Bloch points, which are 3D topological singularities in magnetization.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that engineering a chirality interface between double-helix nanowires allows for the deterministic control of Bloch point polarity, circulation, and position.

**Method.** The study combines advanced experimental techniques like X-ray Magnetic Circular Dichroism (XMCD) tomography and electron holography with micromagnetic simulations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is magnetic Co double-helix nanowires, where the topological control is achieved by creating a structural defect (chirality interface) that imposes competing topological constraints on the magnetization.

**Key observables.** 3D magnetization vector m(r), magnetic vorticity field $\Omega(\mathbf{r})$, stray-field symmetry (monopole-like vs. asymmetric), and the helicity angle $\gamma$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The key parameters include the geometric chirality ($\chi_G$), the initial applied magnetic field ($H_{	ext{ini}}$), and the tilt angle ($	heta_{	ext{tilt}}$) used in fabrication.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the topological constraints imposed by the engineered interface are the dominant factor controlling the Bloch point structure, although pinning effects are noted as experimental limitations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the 3D structure, the chirality interface, and tomographic reconstructions of the magnetic induction ($\mathbf{B}$) and magnetization ($\mathbf{m}$), showing the characteristic symmetric stray-field patterns of Bloch points.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical need for controlled Bloch point generation to detailing the fabrication and characterization using multiple advanced imaging techniques (XMCD, holography), culminating in demonstrating deterministic control over the spin texture via field initialization and analyzing the underlying physical mechanisms (LLG dynamics).

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Bloch points are three-dimensional topological singularities in magnetization that play a key role in topological transformations of spin textures, such as skyrmion creation or annihilation. While topology often enforces the existence of Bloch points in confined geometries like cylindrical nanowires, deterministic control over their position and magnetic configuration remains challenging. Here we demonstrate the generation of Bloch points with controlled spin texture by engineering geometrical boundary conditions in three-dimensional nanomagnets. By introducing a chirality interface between two three-dimensional double-helix nanowires of opposite handedness, forming a kinked, non-collinear structure, we impose competing topological constraints that uniquely define the magnetization configuration surrounding the Bloch point. A saturating magnetic field nucleates head-to-head or tail-to-tail domain configurations at the chirality interface, producing a Bloch-point domain wall with deterministic polarity, circulation and helicity. This geometrical approach enables full three-dimensional control of Bloch point domain walls allowing deterministic engineering of their spin texture and its selective coupling to current-induced Oersted fields.

</details>

**Summary.** This work reports the development and application of a novel carbon-fiber-tip nanomanipulation system integrated into an SEM. The system achieves sub-100 nm precision to position magnetic nanoparticles ($	ext{Fe}_3	ext{O}_4$) onto $\mu$-SQUID arrays. This technique successfully enables the high-resolution, single-particle measurement of magnetic properties, advancing nano-magnetic characterization for quantum sensing applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** While the core physics is classical magnetism and condensed matter characterization, the extreme precision required for manipulating quantum-scale objects near quantum sensors (SQUIDs) touches upon the interface between classical mechanics and quantum measurement, which is relevant to quantum sensing applications.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The primary goal was to develop a precise nanomanipulation system to position individual magnetic nanoparticles (NPs) near highly sensitive superconducting quantum interference devices ($\mu$-SQUIDs) for magnetic characterization.

**Main result.** The system successfully positioned $	ext{Fe}_3	ext{O}_4$ NPs onto $\mu$-SQUIDs, allowing for the measurement of anisotropic magnetic hysteresis loops from single NPs.

**Method.** The authors utilized a custom carbon-fiber-tip based nanomanipulator integrated into an SEM, employing piezoelectric actuators for sub-100 nm positioning precision.

**Model / system.** The platform combines a carbon-fiber tip, which is etched via electrochemical methods, with a multi-stage piezoelectric positioning system (bimorph, piezo-tube, walker). The sample consists of a silicon chip containing an array of $\mu$-SQUIDs.

**Key observables.** Anisotropic magnetic hysteresis loops ($	ext{B}_f-	ext{H}$), NP placement precision ($\sim 100 	ext{ nm}$), and SQUID response periodicity ($\Phi_0$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Optimal tip apex radius ($\lesssim 100 	ext{ nm}$), initial etching current ($I_{	ext{in}}$) and resulting breakage time ($t_b$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The van der Waals force calculation assumes a simple sphere–sphere contact model, and the mechanical fragility of ultra-sharp tips is noted as a limitation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 details the relationship between tip breakage time and etching current. Figure 2 shows the schematic of the nano-manipulator. Figure 3 illustrates the successful placement of an NP onto a $\mu$-SQUID, and Figure 4 displays the measured magnetic hysteresis loops.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the development of the nanomanipulator system, characterizes the tip fabrication process, demonstrates the precise positioning of magnetic NPs onto the SQUID array, and finally presents the magnetic characterization results.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report a carbon-fiber-tip based nanomanipulation system integrated into a scanning electron microscope for individual nanoparticle (NP) manipulation on a surface. Electrochemically etched amorphous carbon fiber tips with excellent mechanical rigidity and sub-100 nm apex radii effectively reduce the van der Waals adhesion and enable reliable positioning of about 100 nm size NPs with about 100 nm precision. This system combines a piezoelectric bimorph for vertical tip motion, a four-quadrant piezo-tube for two-dimensional fine tip control and a two-dimensional piezoelectric walker for coarse lateral translation. Using this setup, we successfully position single Fe$_3$O$_4$ magnetic NPs on micron sized superconducting quantum interference devices for optimal magnetic coupling between them and probe a NP's magnetism.

</details>

### [Thermodynamic and magnetocaloric properties of a triangular spin-1/2 cluster with Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29989v1)

**Authors:** Jordana Torrico, Romulo A. Silva, S. M. de Souza, Onofre Rojas  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.29989v1>

**Summary.** This theoretical study explores the magnetic and thermodynamic behavior of a triangular spin-1/2 cluster featuring Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions. The authors map out various magnetic phases and demonstrate that the DM interaction critically tunes the magnetocaloric effect. The findings are highly relevant for designing molecular magnets for applications like quantum refrigeration.

**Why it may be interesting.** The detailed analysis of entropy plateaus, phase transitions, and the MCE provides a rich playground for understanding how geometric frustration and anisotropic interactions (like DM) govern macroscopic thermodynamic responses in quantum magnetic materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the magnetic and thermodynamic properties, particularly the magnetocaloric effect (MCE), of a triangular spin-1/2 cluster incorporating Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction.

**Main result.** The system exhibits complex magnetic phases (FM, FI, FR) and shows that the DM interaction significantly enhances the complexity of the MCE, leading to nontrivial entropy variations.

**Method.** The research employs theoretical calculations involving the exact energy spectrum diagonalization, leading to the derivation of thermodynamic quantities like entropy, specific heat, and the isothermal entropy change ($\Delta S$).

**Model / system.** The system is a triangular spin-1/2 cluster, modeled by a spin-1/2 Heisenberg Hamiltonian augmented with antisymmetric Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) exchange interactions. This model is relevant to $\text{Cu}_3$-based molecular magnets.

**Key observables.** Magnetization, magnetic susceptibility, entropy ($S$), specific heat ($C$), and the isothermal entropy change ($	ext{-}\Delta S$) characterizing the MCE.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The DM interaction strength ($D$), the relative strengths of exchange couplings ($J_1, J_2, J_3$), and the external magnetic field ($B$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis often uses simplified effective parametrizations for the DM vectors and assumes periodic boundary conditions for the cluster geometry.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the energy spectrum vs. magnetic field, phase diagrams showing FI, FM, and FR regions, and density plots mapping entropy variations in the $B-T$ and $B-D$ planes, alongside specific heat curves showing Schottky anomalies.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the Hamiltonian, derives the partition function and free energy, calculates key thermodynamic observables (M, $\chi$, S, C), maps out the ground-state phase diagram, and finally focuses on quantifying the MCE via entropy changes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a theoretical investigation of the magnetic and thermodynamic properties of the triangular spin-1/2 cluster with Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction, described by a spin-1/2 Heisenberg Hamiltonian with antisymmetric exchange interactions. The energy spectrum and ground-state phase diagram reveal the presence of ferromagnetic (FM), ferrimagnetic (FI), and frustrated (FR) phases, strongly influenced by the total spin and the DM interaction. We analyze magnetization and susceptibility, showing that at low temperatures the system exhibits a characteristic 1/3 magnetization plateau, while thermal fluctuations suppress magnetic order at higher temperatures. The entropy and specific heat display residual entropies due to ground-state degeneracies, Schottky-type anomalies at intermediate temperatures, and additional low-temperature features related to phase transitions. Particular attention is given to the magnetocaloric effect (MCE), characterized by both direct and inverse regimes depending on the magnetic field variation. We find that the DM interaction enhances the complexity of the MCE, leading to nontrivial entropy variations as a function of the magnetic field. These results provide insights into the role of frustration and anisotropy in tuning the MCE of properties triangular spin clusters, with relevance to \mathrm{Cu}_{3}-based molecular magnets.

</details>

**Summary.** This work investigates how energy and structure evolve in stacked 2D material heterostructures ($	ext{MoS}_2/	ext{WS}_2$) following intense laser excitation. Using ultrafast electron diffraction and thermal modeling, the authors demonstrate that interlayer coupling creates unique, ultrafast structural dynamics and influences heat dissipation across the interfaces. These findings are vital for engineering next-generation optoelectronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study provides quantitative insights into non-equilibrium energy transfer mechanisms (carrier-phonon coupling) in nanoscale materials, which is crucial for designing efficient energy harvesting and thermal management devices relevant to quantum technologies.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to understand the electronic coupling and energy flow (carrier and phonon transport) across layered two-dimensional (2D) heterostructures for use in next-generation optoelectronic devices.

**Main result.** The heterostructure exhibits ultrafast, carrier-coupled structural dynamics absent in individual monolayers, and the interlayer thermal boundary conductance (TBC) is comparable to, and potentially higher than, monolayer-substrate interfaces.

**Method.** The research combines ultrafast electron diffraction (UED) to probe structural dynamics with thermal transport modeling to analyze energy dissipation.

**Model / system.** The physical system is supported Transition Metal Dichalcogenide (TMD) heterostructures, specifically $	ext{MoS}_2/	ext{WS}_2$ bilayers, studied via photoexcitation. The analysis involves modeling heat diffusion across multiple interfaces using coupled partial differential equations.

**Key observables.** Photoinduced out-of-plane structural dynamics, interlayer charge transfer (ICT) dynamics, characteristic time constants ($	au_{	ext{OP}}$, $	au_{	ext{thermal}}$), and thermal boundary conductance (TBC).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fluence levels ($\sim 3.2 	ext{ mJ cm}^{-2}$), time scales (femtoseconds to picoseconds), and TBC values ($	ext{MW m}^{-2} 	ext{K}^{-1}$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The thermal model assumes local intralayer thermal equilibrium, and the UED analysis has limitations regarding probe depth and sensitivity to specific phonon modes.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare early-time diffraction intensity changes between the heterostructure and individual monolayers, illustrate the energy flow pathways involving ICT, and compare structural recovery dynamics and calculated temperatures using thermal models.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from characterizing the fundamental physical problem (energy flow in 2D HSs) to presenting ultrafast experimental measurements (UED) showing unique carrier-coupled dynamics, and finally applying thermal transport modeling to quantify energy dissipation across interfaces.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Understanding the electronic coupling and energy flow across layered two-dimensional heterostructures (HSs) is crucial to the exploitation of carrier and phonon transports as well as thermal management in next-generation optoelectronic devices. By using reflection ultrafast electron diffraction, we directly examine photoinduced out-of-plane structural dynamics of supported MoS2/WS2 bilayer HSs and their individual monolayers. Experimental evidence reveals the launch of ultrafast carrier-coupled intralayer atomic motions due to interlayer charge transfer across the van der Waals (vdW) heterojunctions that is absent for individual monolayers. Such a notable carrier-lattice correlation is in addition to the electronic coupling manifested in the enhanced optical absorption for HSs. Also, different pathways of energy flow as a result of carrier-phonon coupling and phonon scattering are reported with the corresponding characteristic times. On longer timescales, relaxation of thermalized atomic motions can be sufficiently described by a thermal transport model. A higher thermal boundary conductance (TBC) across MoS2/WS2 HSs is obtained compared to those at the monolayer-substrate interfaces; however, the similar TBC values suggest comparable couplings of phonons across vdW contacts. These results further shed light on the optical, phonon, and interfacial thermal properties of vertically-stacked vdW HSs.

</details>

### [FunKit: A computer algebra toolkit for functional approaches](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28935v1)

**Authors:** Franz R. Sattler  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28935v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.28935_figures/2605.28935_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: FunKit workflow: From theory specification to numerical evaluation.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.28935_figures/2605.28935_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Results of the Yang–Mills example in examples/Yang-Mills/. Left: Inverse gluon dressing 1/ZA(p) compared to lattice data from [43]. Center: Ghost dressing in the scaling limit. Right: Avatars of the strong coupling.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.28935_figures/2605.28935_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Performance comparison between FunKit (v1.0.0), DoFun (v3.0) [27, 28] and QMeS (v1.2) [29]. In all cases, we measured the total time to take the derivatives and truncate the expression. Especially for very large expressions with several thousand diagrams, e.g. the six-point and four-point flows, FunKit excels. All benchmarks were performed on a 16-core AMD Ryzen 9 7945HX machine. Two warmup-cycles were used before five benchmark runs, which led to the error bars as shown in the plots.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces FunKit, a sophisticated Mathematica toolkit designed to automate the derivation and tracing of functional equations in Quantum Field Theory. It allows physicists to systematically calculate complex quantities like flow equations and Dyson-Schwinger equations for various models. By integrating symbolic manipulation with code generation (C++/Julia/Fortran), FunKit provides a powerful, end-to-end computational platform for advanced theoretical physics research.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on QFT, the systematic, automated handling of complex functional derivatives, tensor contractions, and code generation is highly relevant to any field requiring the symbolic manipulation of high-dimensional correlation functions or master equations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the complex task of deriving and systematically tracing functional equations arising from arbitrary master equations in Quantum Field Theories (QFTs). It aims to provide a comprehensive computational framework for these derivations.

**Main result.** The authors introduce FunKit, a powerful Mathematica package that automates the entire pipeline, from theory specification and functional differentiation to tensor tracing and code generation for numerical evaluation.

**Method.** The methodology involves defining the theory's field content and master equations, followed by sequential computational steps: taking functional derivatives, applying systematic truncations, routing momenta, and finally tracing the resulting large tensor expressions.

**Model / system.** The toolkit is applicable to a wide range of QFT functional approaches, including Dyson-Schwinger equations (DSEs), functional Renormalization Group (fRG) equations (like the Wetterich equation), $n$PI equations, and Slavnov-Taylor identities. Examples include Yang-Mills and scalar theories.

**Key observables.** Correlation functions ($\Gamma_N$), propagators ($G_{ab}$), flow equations ($	ext{Flow2F}$, $	ext{Flow4F}$), and the 1PI generating functional ($\Gamma$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The calculation depends on the chosen regulator functions ($R_{ab}$), coupling constants ($\lambda[i]$), and the specific truncation scheme applied to the infinite set of diagrams.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The process relies on defining a global setup, specifying truncation rules, and assuming the ability to systematically manage large diagrammatic expressions through computer algebra.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the overall FunKit workflow, showing the pipeline from theory specification through functional derivation to final numerical code generation.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the toolkit's functionality across several sections, covering notation (Section 2), basic syntax (Section 3), tensor bases/diagrams (Section 4), code generation (Section 5), and culminating in full examples demonstrating the calculation of complex flow equations (Section 6).

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce FunKit, a Mathematica package for the derivation and tracing of functional equations from arbitrary master equations. FunKit provides an expression vocabulary and a set of rules that allow for derivations in any given field theory and master equation. It also allows users to add extensions for more specific equation systems. Therefore, it can be used in a wide range of situations, for example Dyson--Schwinger or functional RG equations, flowing reparametrisations, nPI equations, (modified) STIs and WTIs, functional Polchinski and Wegner flows, functional master equations with sources, and many others. Besides interfacing with the \FORM language to trace large tensor expressions efficiently, FunKit also provides facilities to export arbitrary Mathematica expressions to C++, Julia or Fortran code, including the results of derivations, which can then be evaluated numerically. Both the tracing and code generation can also be used independently and in combination with other packages.

</details>

### [Revealing quantum metric multipoles in magnetic topological insulator MnBi2Te4](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29595v1)

**Authors:** Lars Sjöström, Prasanna Rout, Shahid Sattar, Alexander Tyner, Maurice E. Bal, Ankit Khola, Elias Rasmussen, Khadiza Ali, Arumugum Thamizhavel, Uli Zeitler, Carlo M. Canali, Saroj P. Dash

**Summary.** This work reports the observation of high-order nonlinear electronic transport in the magnetic topological insulator $	ext{MnBi}_2	ext{Te}_4$. By measuring harmonics up to the seventh order, the authors demonstrate that the nonlinear response is dominated by odd harmonics and correlates strongly with the material's magnetic phase. This evidence allows them to microscopically identify the quantum metric multipoles as the key physical origin of the observed quantum geometric effects.

**Why it may be interesting.** The connection between measurable transport coefficients (nonlinear response) and fundamental geometric invariants (quantum metric multipoles) provides a novel, experimentally accessible probe of topological band structure, which is relevant to understanding Berry curvature effects in condensed matter.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates higher-order nonlinear electronic transport in magnetic topological insulators to probe the underlying quantum geometry, specifically the quantum metric multipoles.

**Main result.** The researchers observed higher-order nonlinear transport up to the seventh harmonic order, confirming an even-odd behavior where odd harmonics dominate. Scaling analysis identified quantum metric multipoles and Drude-like conductivities as the microscopic origins of this nonlinear response.

**Method.** Nonlinear electronic transport measurements were performed using a lock-in technique at high harmonic orders ($n\omega$) on exfoliated multilayer $	ext{MnBi}_2	ext{Te}_4$. Theoretical modeling was used to interpret the observed scaling relationships.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the magnetic topological insulator $	ext{MnBi}_2	ext{Te}_4$, which exhibits rich band topology and magnetic ordering (A-type AFM). The analysis relies on fitting the transport response to models incorporating quantum metric and Drude-like contributions.

**Key observables.** Higher-harmonic voltage responses ($V_{3\omega}, V_{5\omega}, V_{7\omega}$), even-odd behavior, and the scaling relationship $V_{3\omega} / (V_{\omega})^3 = \alpha \sigma_{xx}^2 + eta$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (T), applied magnetic field ($B$), and the harmonic order ($n$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the observed longitudinal nonlinear transport is governed by quantum metric multipoles and Drude-like contributions, while ruling out spurious effects like thermoelectric or capacitive coupling.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the higher-harmonic nonlinear transport, demonstrating the suppression of even harmonics and the strong correlation between the nonlinear signals and the magnetic phases of $	ext{MnBi}_2	ext{Te}_4$. Scaling plots confirm the linear relationship between the third harmonic and the square of the DC conductivity.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses by first establishing the feasibility of measuring high-order harmonics, then analyzing the even-odd symmetry and magnetic field dependence, and finally using scaling analysis and theoretical modeling to attribute the observed nonlinearities to specific quantum geometric terms.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nonlinear electronic transport has emerged as a powerful probe of the quantum geometry in topological quantum materials, where the band topology and broken symmetries facilitate power law current voltage responses beyond Ohms law. While nonlinear transport of the second and third orders has been studied in several quantum materials, higher-order transport has so far mainly remained experimentally inaccessible, leaving more detailed features of the quantum geometry unexplored. Here, we observe higher order nonlinear electronic transport up to the seventh harmonic order in multilayer magnetic topological insulator MnBi2Te4. We find an even-odd behavior where the odd order nonlinear transport components dominate while the even-order ones are suppressed. Temperature and magnetic field dependent measurements show a strong correlation between the nonlinear transport and the magnetic phases of MnBi2Te4. Through scaling analysis and theoretical calculations, quantum metric multipoles and nonlinear Drude conductivities are identified as the microscopic origins of the nonlinear transport.

</details>

**Summary.** This work investigates orbital magnetism and chiral superconductivity in multilayer graphene using nanoSQUID magnetometry. By correlating local magnetization measurements with transport data, the authors confirm the chiral nature of the superconducting state and reveal that the system's magnetic behavior is governed by the concentration of Berry curvature. The findings highlight a complex interplay between quantum geometry, doping, and superconducting pairing in these advanced 2D materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** The strong coupling between orbital magnetism (a topological/geometric property) and superconductivity in a 2D material like graphene is highly relevant to understanding exotic superconducting states and topological superconductivity, which are key areas in condensed matter theory.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to map and understand the orbital magnetization in electron-doped rhombohedral multilayer graphene, particularly in relation to its quantum geometric properties and superconducting states.

**Main result.** The research provides direct evidence of chiral superconductivity via a finite orbital magnetic moment and demonstrates that the orbital magnetization is highly sensitive to carrier density and displacement field due to Berry curvature concentration.

**Method.** The work combines advanced local magnetometry (nanoSQUID-on-tip) measurements with theoretical calculations based on continuum Hamiltonians to model the system's electronic structure.

**Model / system.** The physical system is electron-doped rhombohedral multilayer graphene (RMG) with thicknesses ranging from 3 to 13 layers. The theoretical modeling uses a continuum Hamiltonian incorporating interlayer hopping and electrostatic screening.

**Key observables.** Orbital magnetization ($\Delta B$), zero-resistance state (superconductivity), Berry curvature concentration ('Berry ring of fire'), and magnetic domain patterns.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Electron density ($n_e$), perpendicular displacement field ($D$), and layer number ($N=3$ to $13$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The interpretation of transport anomalies relies on assumptions about sample homogeneity, and the study suggests a subtle energetic competition underlies the observed chiral superconductivity.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate $\Delta B$ measurements showing magnetization peaks at finite density, comparisons between orbital ferromagnetism and chiral superconductivity, and spatial mapping of magnetic inhomogeneity in the superconducting regime.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from characterizing the orbital magnetization using magnetometry across varying doping/field regimes, to linking this magnetism to the superconducting state, and finally investigating the complex phase competition and domain formation in the chiral superconducting region.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electron doped rhombohedral multilayer graphene at high displacement field features an exceptionally flat band minimum with near-ideal quantum geometry. Experiments in this regime observe the formation of a 'quarter metal,' in which the electron liquid condenses into a single spin- and valley flavor. Remarkably, recent experiments have found a zero resistance state in the same region of the density- and displacement-field-tuned parameter space, attributed to the formation of a chiral superconductor characterized by a finite-momentum Cooper pair condensate. Here, we use nanoSQUID-on-tip magnetometry to map the orbital magnetization of electron-doped rhombohedral graphene devices ranging in thickness between 3 and 13 layers. Magnetization within the quarter metal phases peaks at finite density, consistent with concentration of the Berry curvature in a finite-momentum 'ring of fire'. Correlating transport and local magnetometry data in a tetralayer sample reveals that the superconducting state has a finite orbital magnetic moment, providing direct evidence of its chiral nature. We further show that widely observed stochastic switching of the resistivity in the metallic regime arises from a density-tuned sign change in the valley-resolved total magnetic moment. This leads to the formation of metastable magnetic domains under typical gate control sequences and can also be harnessed for electric-field controlled switching of orbital moment across the entire device. Unexpectedly, we find magnetic inhomogeneity specific to the apparent normal state of the chiral superconductor, suggestive of a strain-tuned competition between magnetic and non-magnetic ground states. Our results point to a subtle energetic competition underlying the observation of chiral superconductivity in a narrow range of layer numbers.

</details>

**Summary.** This work uses Molecular Dynamics simulations to reveal that resistive switching in Ta-HfO2-Pt memristors is controlled by the formation of a hybrid Ta-cation-rich filament, not just oxygen vacancies. By varying initial defect levels, the authors demonstrate that controlling defect engineering is crucial for achieving stable, reproducible device performance necessary for neuromorphic applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on solid-state electronics, the use of advanced MD simulations with dynamic charge transfer and the development of coupled atomistic/analytical models for non-equilibrium processes (like filament formation) showcases sophisticated computational techniques relevant to many-body dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to understand the complex, atomistic mechanisms governing resistive switching in Ta-HfO2-Pt memristors, moving beyond simple oxygen vacancy models.

**Main result.** Switching is governed by the field-driven formation and rupture of a hybrid Ta-cation-rich, oxygen-deficient filament, and initial defect concentrations significantly influence device variability.

**Method.** The study employs Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations with dynamic charge transfer, coupled with analytical modeling to correlate atomistic evolution with macroscopic IV characteristics.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a Ta-HfO2-Pt memristor. The simulation uses a Charge-Transfer Interatomic Potential (CTIP) and models the switching using first-order kinetic equations for filament growth (SET) and decay (RESET).

**Key observables.** Atomic charge distribution, stoichiometry, filament conductivity, IV characteristics, and conductance vs. pulse number.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Applied electric field (e.g., $\pm 1.4 	ext{ V/Å}$), initial oxygen vacancy concentration (e.g., 0.81% vs 1.35%), and temperature (300 K).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analytical model is limited to filamentary memristors, and the simulation relies on specific potentials and kinetic rate laws derived from MD observations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the MD setup, the full cycle switching process showing $	ext{TaO}_x$ formation and step-wise constriction/rupture, and conductance stability under pulsed operation for different defect levels.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from establishing the need for a hybrid model, detailing the MD simulation setup and potential functions, presenting the kinetic modeling of SET/RESET processes, and finally comparing the results across varying defect concentrations to discuss device variability.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Memristive devices have gained significant attention for their potential in next-generation non-volatile memory and neuromorphic computing architectures. Among emerging candidates, transition metal oxides have proven particularly promising. While the switching mechanism in Ta/HfO$_2$/Pt devices was long attributed solely to oxygen vacancy based filaments, recent experimental evidence suggests a more complex dual-regime: the diffusion of metal cations also contributes to the formation of a conductive bridge. However, the precise atomistic mechanisms governing this metal cation migration remain poorly understood. Additionally, the role of defects such as oxygen vacancies present in the transition metal oxide in determining the final filament size and shape is also not well understood. Here, we employ molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with dynamic charge transfer to provide a detailed analysis of the atomistic mechanisms governing the co-formation of Ta-cation and oxygen-deficient filaments. We clearly show how varying the initial oxygen vacancy concentrations and spatial configurations within the HfO$_2$ matrix influences the final morphology and dimensions of the conductive filament. The switching is governed by field-driven formation and rupture of a hybrid Ta-cation-rich, oxygen-deficient filament in HfO$_2$. Our simulations closely match experiment, validating the model as a robust framework for understanding switching in oxide memristors and guiding designs that reduce cycle-to-cycle and device-to-device variability -- key barriers to high-performance devices.

</details>

**Summary.** This work demonstrates that surface effects in layered quantum materials do not necessarily vanish in the bulk limit, contrary to conventional wisdom. By developing a general framework, the authors show that surface-originated anomalous transport can persist and even dominate the response, particularly when bulk symmetries forbid the effect. This opens a new avenue for designing quantum devices based on surface quantum geometry.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on condensed matter transport, the rigorous mathematical decomposition of total response into distinct, non-trivial surface and bulk components provides a powerful, generalizable theoretical tool for analyzing boundary effects in quantum materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper challenges the conventional assumption that surface contributions to physical responses in slab geometries diminish rapidly with increasing thickness.

**Main result.** The surface contribution remains finite and scales with the bulk term even in the thick-slab limit, and it can dominate the overall response when the bulk contribution is symmetry-forbidden.

**Method.** The authors developed a general theoretical framework using linear response theory to decompose the total response into distinct surface- and bulk-contributions.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on magnetoelectric multilayers, modeled using a tight-binding Hamiltonian subjected to an external gate electric field ($E_G$).

**Key observables.** Cross-field anomalous transport coefficients (e.g., anomalous Nernst effect), gate-induced orbital magnetization, and the decomposition of response coefficients ($\chi = \chi_{LP} + \chi_{LH}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The thickness of the slab ($L$), the applied gate electric field ($E_G$), and the material system ($	ext{MnBi}_2	ext{Te}_4$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is restricted to weak electric fields ($E_G$) and effects linear in $E_G$.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the schematic decomposition of responses into surface (LP) and bulk (LH) parts, and show the thickness dependence of the surface contribution, demonstrating saturation to a finite limit.

**Paper structure.** The paper first introduces the problem of surface vs. bulk contributions, develops the general decomposition framework, analyzes the scaling behavior in the thick limit, and finally applies these findings to predict a strong surface-dominated anomalous Nernst effect in $	ext{MnBi}_2	ext{Te}_4$.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In material systems with slab geometry, the surface contribution to physical responses is commonly expected to diminish rapidly with increasing thickness, giving way to the bulk response. Here, we show that this conventional wisdom is violated in a class of gate-induced responses, including gate-induced orbital and spin magnetization as well as cross-field anomalous thermoelectric transport. We develop a general framework for these effects, which naturally decomposes the total response into surface- and bulk-contributions treated on equal footing. Remarkably, the volume-averaged surface contribution remains finite in the thick-slab limit and exhibits the same thickness scaling as the bulk term. Furthermore, the surface response originates from band geometric quantities distinct from those in the bulk, being constrained solely by surface symmetries. As a result, it can dominate the overall response when the bulk contribution is symmetry-forbidden. Taking MnBi$_2$Te$_4$ multilayers as an example, we predict a strong surface-dominated cross-field anomalous Nernst effect arising from surface Berry curvature, which is readily accessible to experimental detection. These findings reveal a previously overlooked significance of surface response and open a new direction in the study of surface quantum geometry.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper experimentally demonstrates a strong, low-power induced nonlinear phase shift in forward volume spin waves propagating in YIG magnetic films. The effect is superior to surface wave counterparts, suggesting a pathway for fast and energy-efficient control of magnon transport. This work is significant for developing next-generation magnonic logic circuits.

**Why it may be interesting.** While focused on magnonics, the concept of nonlinear wave interaction and energy-efficient control of wave propagation shares conceptual links with nonlinear dynamics studied in quantum optics and open quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the induced nonlinear phase shift of forward volume spin waves (FVSWs) in perpendicularly magnetized magnetic films and one-dimensional magnonic crystals.

**Main result.** The nonlinear phase shift is significantly stronger for FVSWs in perpendicularly magnetized films compared to surface spin waves, allowing a $180^\circ$ shift with pump powers as low as a few milliwatts.

**Method.** The research combines experimental measurements using a Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) with theoretical modeling based on nonlinear damping theory.

**Model / system.** The physical system involves Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) films, sometimes structured into 1D magnonic crystals, which are excited and measured using microstrip antennas.

**Key observables.** Induced nonlinear phase shift ($\Delta\varphi_{1INL}$), amplitude-frequency characteristic (AFC), and nonlinear damping parameters ($
u_2, \varsigma_2$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pump wave power ($P_2$), film thickness (e.g., 5.7-$\mu$m, 13.6-$\mu$m), and operating frequencies relative to magnonic band gaps.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on comparing experimental data with theoretical predictions derived from nonlinear damping theory.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the experimental setup, phase vs. time measurements, AFCs for different film thicknesses, and plots showing $\Delta\varphi_{1INL}$ dependence on pump power, particularly highlighting saturation and band gap effects.

**Paper structure.** The paper details the experimental setup, presents measurements of the nonlinear phase shift dependence on pump power for various film geometries (bulk vs. 1D MC), and compares these results against theoretical models.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

A differential phase shift of a low-power spin wave (SW) induced by a high-power pumping wave co-propagating at different frequencies in perpendicularly magnetized magnetic films has been studied. We find that this effect for forward volume SWs propagating in yttrium iron garnet (YIG) films is stronger than that for surface SWs propagating in tangentially magnetized films. The results show that the induced nonlinear phase shift up to 180° takes place for pumping wave power of a few milliwatts. The phenomenon paves the way for fast and energy-efficient control of one-dimensional magnon transport.

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical study models acoustic wave propagation in composite rods containing a shape-memory alloy undergoing a first-order phase transition. It demonstrates that the material's inherent thermal hysteresis causes the resulting phononic band structure and transmission spectrum to become path-dependent. This highlights how macroscopic thermodynamic history can imprint itself onto the collective vibrational modes of a structured material.

**Why it may be interesting.** The concept of path-dependent properties (hysteresis) manifesting in collective excitations (phonons) is analogous to non-equilibrium dynamics studied in open quantum systems or driven many-body systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how the intrinsic thermal hysteresis present in a shape-memory alloy (NiTiCu) influences the collective acoustic response of a periodic composite structure.

**Main result.** The material's path-dependent thermal hysteresis is transferred to the acoustic properties, causing the phononic band structure and transmission spectrum to depend on the thermal history (heating vs. cooling).

**Method.** The analysis employs the transfer-matrix method to compute the Bloch band structure and transmission spectrum for the periodic superlattice.

**Model / system.** The system is a one-dimensional periodic composite rod made of alternating segments of NiTiCu (shape-memory alloy) and a polymer spacer (Parylene C). Material properties are modeled as functions of temperature, exhibiting first-order phase transitions.

**Key observables.** Bloch band structure, transmission coefficient ($	au$), and the specific acoustic impedance ($Z$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature ($T$), geometric filling fraction ($\phi$), and the material's elastic modulus ($E$) and density ($ho$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on the thin-rod approximation, assuming acoustic wave amplitudes are insufficient to alter the phase fraction, and that the material properties are solely functions of temperature.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the temperature dependence of acoustic impedance showing hysteresis, and the evolution of the phononic band structure, comparing distinct spectra for heating and cooling paths.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from analyzing the material's intrinsic hysteresis (acoustic impedance) to modeling the infinite periodic structure using the transfer-matrix method, and finally examining the measurable, path-dependent transmission spectrum of finite composite rods.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the propagation of longitudinal elastic waves in one-dimensional periodic composite rods composed of alternating segments of a shape-memory alloy (NiTiCu) and a polymer spacer (Parylene C). In the thin-rod regime, the longitudinal phase velocity reduces to $c=\sqrt{E/ρ}$, which coincides with the regime in which the elastic modulus of NiTiCu has been measured directly through its acoustic response across the martensitic transformation. Using the standard transfer-matrix method along the heating and cooling branches of the transformation separately, we compute the Bloch band structure of the infinite periodic system and the transmission spectrum of finite composite rods. Because the elastic modulus of NiTiCu follows different paths upon heating and cooling, the same external temperature within the transformation interval corresponds to two different phase fractions and, consequently, to two different phononic spectra. The resulting hysteresis of the underlying material is thus transferred to the collective acoustic response of the periodic structure: stop-band edges trace closed loops in the temperature--frequency plane, and the transmission coefficient of a finite rod at a fixed temperature depends on the previous thermal history. We further show that the geometric filling fraction of the active segment provides a complementary tuning mechanism, modifying the width of the spectral hysteresis loops and the position of specific gap closures independently of temperature. These results illustrate how a first-order structural phase transition with intrinsic thermal hysteresis manifests itself in the dispersion relation of a periodic elastic medium.

</details>
