**Summary.** This work investigates the potential for room-temperature superconductivity in $	ext{LaSc}_2	ext{H}_{24}$ by combining advanced theoretical calculations with multiple high-pressure electrical measurements. While theory suggests high $T_c$ under optimal conditions, the experimental results from synthesizing La-Sc alloys fail to confirm this superconductivity near room temperature. The paper highlights the significant gap between theoretical prediction and practical material synthesis.

**Why it may be interesting.** The interplay between complex theoretical models (like electron-phonon coupling) and challenging high-pressure experimental realization makes this relevant for understanding strongly correlated or exotic superconducting states in condensed matter.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper analyzes the theoretical feasibility and experimental reproducibility of room-temperature superconductivity in the high-pressure hydride $	ext{P6/mmm-LaSc}_2	ext{H}_{24}$. It contrasts theoretical predictions with results from high-pressure synthesis attempts.

**Main result.** While theory predicts high $T_c$ using specific methods, experimental synthesis attempts using La-Sc alloys failed to show superconductivity near room temperature, suggesting the material is difficult to realize or unstable under ambient synthesis conditions.

**Method.** The study combines theoretical calculations (Eliashberg function analysis, stability analysis using $\xi$) with multiple high-pressure electrical transport measurements using Diamond Anvil Cells (DACs).

**Model / system.** The physical system is the lanthanum-scandium hydride $	ext{LaSc}_2	ext{H}_{24}$ under extreme pressure (200-300 GPa). Theoretical modeling involves electron-phonon coupling calculations, while experiments use $	ext{La-Sc}$ alloy precursors reacted with hydrogen sources.

**Key observables.** Superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$), electrical resistance $R(T)$, stability parameter ($\xi$), and electron-phonon coupling constant ($\lambda$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pressure (220-300 GPa), Temperature (230-300 K), and the electron-phonon coupling broadening parameter ($\sigma$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Theoretical calculations rely on approximations like the tetrahedral method or Gaussian broadening of the delta function; experimental results are limited by the synthesis method and measurement window.

**Figures summary.** Figures show stability analysis ($\xi$) and resistance measurements ($R$ vs $T$) under DAC conditions, detailing both superconducting transitions and metallic behavior in synthesized La-Sc-H phases.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from theoretical analysis of $T_c$ dependence on calculation parameters ($\sigma$) and stability ($\xi$), to comparing theoretical predictions with multiple high-pressure electrical measurements performed on synthesized La-Sc-H samples.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we analyze the feasibility of room-temperature superconductivity in the lanthanum-scandium hydride $\textit{P6/mmm}$-LaSc$_2$H$_{24}$. We demonstrate that the electron-phonon coupling calculations performed using the $σ$-broadening of the double $δ$-function at the Fermi surface lead to a very strong dependence of $\textit{T$_c$}(σ)$ on the arbitrary $σ$, whereas the tetrahedral method for the electron-phonon interaction is free from this drawback and leads to $\textit{T$_c$}$ > 300 K at 300 GPa in agreement with previous predictions. By analyzing the stability of the metallic state of LaSc$_2$H$_{24}$ at 250-300 GPa, we show that this compound is at the edge of the stability region ($ξ$ = 0.54), similar to $\textit{fcc}$ LaH$_{10}$ at 140-150 GPa. Experimental attempts to synthesize LaSc$_2$H$_{24}$ at 250-280 GPa starting from the (La,Sc$_2$) alloy are unsuccessful and indicate the absence of even traces of superconductivity at 245-300 K in all the resulting La-Sc-H hydrides. The method for preparing the precursor by simultaneous deposition of La and Sc metals may be a key factor for the successful synthesis of LaSc$_2$H$_{24}$.

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical study examines graphene nanoribbons fused with porphine units to engineer specific electronic and magnetic properties. By breaking bipartite and time-reversal symmetries, the system can stabilize a ferromagnetic ground state with distinct majority and minority spin channels. This dual spin-filtering capability makes the structure highly promising for advanced spintronic applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** The explicit control over spin-dependent electronic properties via molecular engineering (porphine fusion) and symmetry breaking is highly relevant to designing next-generation spintronic devices.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how fusing a porphine unit between two zigzag-edge graphene nanoribbons modifies their electronic and magnetic properties by breaking bipartite and time-reversal symmetries.

**Main result.** The hybrid structure exhibits two nearly degenerate ground states: a semiconducting antiferromagnetic state and a conducting ferromagnetic state. The FM state is promising for dual spin-filtering due to opposite Fermi velocities for majority and minority spins.

**Method.** Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations, employing the GGA+U formalism for transition metal atoms, were used to analyze the electronic structure.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a hybrid structure formed by fusing a porphine ring (Por) between two Zigzag-edge Graphene Nanoribbons (ZGNRs), denoted as $m m’Z-Por$. Substitutions with transition metals (V, Fe, Cu) are also studied.

**Key observables.** Ground state energy difference ($\Delta$), net magnetization ($\mu_B$), spin-polarized band structures, Density of States (DOS), and spin-resolved Fermi velocities.

**Important parameters / regimes.** ZGNR width, presence/type of transition metal dopant (V, Fe, Cu), and the symmetry breaking induced by the porphine ring.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis relies on a mean-field description for interactions and assumes the porphine ligand geometry dictates the d-orbital splitting pattern.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate spin densities, spin-polarized band structures, and pDOS for various hybrid states (AFM vs. FM), detailing spin localization and orbital hybridization effects.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the electronic structure of the $m m’Z-Por$ system, comparing the AFM and FM ground states. It then details the effect of incorporating transition metals (V, Fe, Cu) and varying the ZGNR width, confirming the symmetry breaking mechanism and its implications for spin transport.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Hybrid structure of two zigzag-edge graphene nanoribbons with a fused porphine ring in between, results in two distinct nearly degenerate ground states: a semiconducting antiferromagnetic state and a conducting ferromagnetic state with unequal and opposite Fermi velocities of majority and minority spins, the former having slightly higher stability. Such ground states result from the broken bipartite symmetry induced by the porphine ring. The incorporation of different transition metal atoms in the porphine cavity reduces their energy difference but keeps their electronic properties mostly unchanged. The splitting of the $d$-orbitals in the distorted square-planar ligand field of porphine produces a high spin ground state that breaks the global time reversal symmetry ($\mathcal{T}$). The opposite Fermi velocities of the majority and minority spins in the ferromagnetic ground state and lower sensitivity of the conducting majority spin channel to the edge disorder, make this class of quasi-one-dimensional hybrid structures promising for dual spin-filtering device applications.

</details>

**Summary.** This theoretical study investigates electron doping in $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ using DFT calculations to understand its superconducting potential. The authors find that $\mathrm{Zr}$, $\mathrm{Hf}$, and $\mathrm{Th}$ are effective electron dopants, which enhances interlayer coupling ($t_{\perp}$) and suggests a pathway to higher $T_c$. The findings help clarify the complex pairing mechanisms in this class of nickelate superconductors.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work provides detailed theoretical insights into tuning superconductivity in nickelates by controlling electron doping, which is crucial for understanding unconventional pairing mechanisms in strongly correlated materials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates electron doping in the Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ to understand its potential impact on high-temperature superconductivity, contrasting it with existing hole-doping studies.

**Main result.** The authors identify $\mathrm{Zr}$, $\mathrm{Hf}$, and $\mathrm{Th}$ as efficient electron dopants, which can increase the interlayer hopping $t_{\perp}$ and potentially enhance the superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$).

**Method.** The research employs first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations, supplemented by the constrained random-phase approximation (cRPA) to estimate effective screened interactions.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ thin film, modeled with substitutions $\mathrm{La_{3-x}R_xNi_2O_7}$ where R is the dopant element. The analysis focuses on the electronic structure and interaction parameters governing superconductivity.

**Key observables.** Interlayer hopping integral ($t_{\perp}$), inter-layer superexchange coupling ($J_{\perp}$), electron counts in $e_g$ orbitals ($\mathrm{d}_{z^2}$ and $\mathrm{d}_{x^2-y^2}$), and Hubbard interaction parameters ($ar{U}$, $J_H/U$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The key parameters include the dopant identity ($\mathrm{Zr}, \mathrm{Hf}, \mathrm{Th}$ vs. $\mathrm{Ce}$), the ratio $t_{\perp}/t_{1x}$, and the strength of Hund's coupling ($J_H/U$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the superconducting properties are governed by the electronic structure changes induced by doping, particularly the modification of hopping integrals and correlation parameters.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate energy bands for various doped films, structural changes like bond length variations, and the fitting of band structures using a double-layer Hubbard model. Tables summarize calculated interaction parameters.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically analyzes the effect of different dopants (electron vs. hole) on the electronic structure of $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ using DFT. It then uses these results to calculate key superconducting parameters like $t_{\perp}$ and $J_{\perp}$, proposing a unified mechanism for superconductivity based on the doping type.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ has emerged as a promising platform for exploring and understanding high-temperature superconductivities. While most prior doping studies   have focused on hole doping via strontium (Sr) substitution or by tuning oxygen content, electron doping remains largely unexplored. In this work,we systematically investigate electron doping in $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$ thin films through   tetravalent element substitution, employing first-principles density functional theory calculations.   Our results reveal that, unlike in cuprates, $\mathrm{cerium}$ (Ce) doping is difficult to effectively introduce electron carriers into the low-energy bands. In contrast, zirconium (Zr), hafnium (Hf), and thorium (Th) can act as efficient electron dopants. These element substitutions can significantly increase the interlayer hopping $t_{\perp}$ between $d_{z^2}$ orbitals, which may lead to enhanced superexchange coupling $J_{\perp}$ , and thereby potentially elevated superconducting $T_c$. We evaluate the interaction parameters using constrained random phase approximation. Our results identify candidate dopants for achieving electron-doped $\mathrm{La_3Ni_2O_7}$, offering a route to clarify the ongoing debate on pairing mechanisms in this system.

</details>

### [Hole-doped superconductivity above 100 K in infinite-layer cuprate thin films](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29352v1)

**Authors:** Biemeng Jin, Saurav Prakash, Zhaoyang Luo, Shengwei Zeng, Jing-Yang Chung, Xing Gao, Zhi Shiuh Lim, Jiangbo Luo, King Yip, Wei Zhang, Nurul Fitriyah, Shuhan Lu, Taiyu An, Ping Yang, Qian He, Silvija Gradečak, Huajun Liu, A. Ariando

</details>

**Summary.** This paper reports the successful observation of hole-doped superconductivity in infinite-layer cuprate thin films ($	ext{Sr}_{1-x}	ext{Rb}_x	ext{CuO}_2$). The research confirms that superconductivity is achieved through a synergistic doping mechanism involving both cation substitution and the incorporation of apical oxygen. This work provides a crucial, structurally simple platform for investigating fundamental puzzles in high-temperature superconductivity.

**Why it may be interesting.** The work directly addresses high-Tc superconductivity, a canonical problem in strongly correlated electron systems, providing experimental evidence on doping mechanisms in a minimal cuprate structure.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The fundamental challenge was achieving and understanding hole-doped superconductivity in the structurally simple infinite-layer cuprate $	ext{ACuO}_2$ system via chemical substitution.

**Main result.** Superconductivity was observed in $	ext{Sr}_{0.9}	ext{Rb}_{0.1}	ext{CuO}_2$ thin films, with an onset temperature around 75 K, confirming hole-type carriers.

**Method.** The study employed a combination of advanced material synthesis (PLD), structural characterization (XRD, STEM), and transport/magnetic measurements (resistivity, Hall effect, Meissner effect).

**Model / system.** The physical system is the hole-doped infinite-layer cuprate thin film, $	ext{Sr}_{1-x}	ext{Rb}_x	ext{CuO}_2$, which serves as a model for high-temperature superconductivity.

**Key observables.** Superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$), zero-resistance state, Meissner effect (diamagnetism), positive Hall coefficient, and the presence of apical oxygen ($	ext{O}_{	ext{ap}}$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Doping ratios ($x=0.08, 0.10, 0.12$), critical magnetic fields ($H_{\perp c}$), and the temperature range up to 100 K.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The superconducting transition is described as 'broad,' potentially indicating a limited superconducting volume fraction.

**Figures summary.** Figures show structural confirmation via XRD/STEM, temperature-dependent resistivity curves, Hall measurements confirming hole doping, and M-H loops demonstrating the Meissner effect.

**Paper structure.** The paper systematically reports structural characterization, followed by transport measurements to confirm superconductivity, and finally uses advanced techniques like STEM-ABF to elucidate the role of apical oxygen in achieving the desired hole doping.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Since the discovery of superconductivity in (La,Ba)2CuO2 (Ref.~\cite{bednorz1986possible}), a broad family of structurally distinct cuprate superconductors has been proposed or engineered to elucidate the physics of high-temperature superconductivity~\cite{chu2015hole,plakida2010high}. Among them, the infinite-layer cuprate has the simplest structure, consisting only of the essential ingredients for superconductivity: CuO$_2$ square planes separated by spacer ions~\cite{siegrist1988parent}. Despite being proposed nearly 40 years ago, the hole-doped superconductivity via chemical substitution in this compound has not yet been achieved, a fundamental open question in the field. Here, we report the observation of superconductivity in the hole-doped infinite-layer cuprate thin film. Measurements of resistivity and magnetic-field response in Sr1-xRbxCuO2 single-crystal thin films show superconducting transitions with a high onset temperature of 100 K. Hole doping is achieved via the synergistic effect of rubidium substitution and apical oxygen incorporation, as evidenced by structural analysis and transport measurements. As the parent structure of the cuprate family~\cite{chu2015hole}, hole-doped infinite-layer cuprate provides a unique platform for revisiting key puzzles in cuprate superconductors~\cite{keimer2015quantum,tsuei2000pairing,armitage2010progress,dagotto1994correlated}, including strange metal~\cite{proust2019remarkable,taillefer2010scattering} and electron-hole symmetry~\cite{tohyama2004asymmetry,segawa2010zero,lee2014asymmetry}, while bridging to cuprate-nickelate symmetry~\cite{li2019superconductivity,zeng2022superconductivity,chow2025bulk,lechermann2020late}.

</details>
